sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 01 | Injury Free Workplace Plan

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. REGULATORY AUTHORITY

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.20(b) General Safety and Health Provisions

WISHA

WAC 296-155-100

Management's Responsibility

WISHA

WAC 296-155-105

Employee's Responsibility

WISHA

WAC 296-155-110

Accident Prevention Program

AGC

SHIP Best Practices Handbook Ch. 1

AGC

AGC CSEA Program Standards

2. PURPOSE

This policy establishes a proactive framework for maintaining an injury-free workplace, ensuring the health and safety of all personnel. It aims to prevent accidents, occupational illnesses, and property damage by integrating safety into all operational aspects.

3. SCOPE

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, vendors, and visitors present on any MH Construction job site or property. Compliance is mandatory for all individuals engaged in company operations.

4. DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Injury-Free Workplace

A work environment where all foreseeable hazards are identified, assessed, and controlled to prevent accidents and occupational illnesses.

Hazard

Any condition or act that has the potential to cause harm, injury, or illness.

Competent Person

One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Accident Prevention Program (APP)

A comprehensive written program designed to identify and control workplace hazards, ensuring a safe and healthy work environment.

Near Miss

An unplanned event that did not result in injury, illness, or damage, but had the potential to do so.

5. RESPONSIBILITIES

5.1 Company/Management Responsibilities

The Company and Management are responsible for providing a safe and healthy work environment, establishing clear safety policies, and allocating necessary resources for safety programs.

- Establish and maintain an effective Accident Prevention Program (APP) as required by WAC 296-155-110.

- Ensure compliance with all applicable OSHA (29 CFR 1926.20(b)) and WISHA regulations.

- Conduct regular safety audits and reviews to identify and mitigate hazards.

- Provide necessary safety training and personal protective equipment (PPE) to all employees.

5.2 Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

Supervisors and Foremen are directly responsible for implementing safety policies on job sites, ensuring employees follow safe work practices, and conducting daily safety briefings.

- Enforce all company safety rules and regulatory requirements.

- Conduct regular site inspections to identify and correct unsafe conditions or acts.

- Investigate all incidents, near misses, and accidents, reporting findings to management.

- Provide on-the-job safety instruction and ensure proper use of PPE.

5.3 Employee Responsibilities

Employees are responsible for adhering to all safety rules, utilizing provided safety equipment, and reporting any unsafe conditions or practices to their supervisor.

- Comply with all company safety policies and procedures, including those outlined in WAC 296-155-105.

- Use all safety equipment and PPE as required.

- Report all accidents, injuries, near misses, and unsafe conditions immediately.

- Participate in safety training and safety meetings.

5.4 Subcontractor Responsibilities

Subcontractors are responsible for ensuring their employees comply with all MH Construction safety policies and applicable regulatory requirements while on company job sites.

- Adhere to all MH Construction safety policies and procedures.

- Provide their employees with appropriate safety training and PPE.

- Coordinate all safety-related activities with MH Construction site supervision.

6. PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

6.1 Accident Prevention Program (APP)

MH Construction maintains a comprehensive Accident Prevention Program (APP) in accordance with WAC 296-155-110. This program is designed to identify, evaluate, and control workplace hazards.

1. The APP is reviewed and updated annually, or as significant changes occur in operations or regulations.

2. All new employees receive an orientation on the APP during their onboarding process.

3. The APP includes provisions for regular safety meetings and employee participation.

6.2 Hazard Identification and Control

A systematic approach to hazard identification and control is implemented across all job sites to ensure an injury-free workplace. Per 29 CFR 1926.20(b)(2), frequent and regular inspections of the job sites, materials, and equipment shall be made by competent persons designated by the employers.

1. Daily job hazard analyses (JHAs) are conducted by competent persons before work commences.

2. All identified hazards are documented, and control measures are implemented immediately.

3. Control measures follow the hierarchy of controls: elimination, substitution, engineering controls, administrative controls, and personal protective equipment (PPE).

6.3 Safety Meetings

Regular safety meetings are held to discuss potential hazards, review incidents, and reinforce safe work practices. Per WAC 296-155-110(4), safety meetings shall be held at least monthly.

1. Toolbox talks are conducted weekly on all active job sites.

2. Formal safety committee meetings are held monthly, involving representatives from management and labor.

3. Meeting minutes are recorded and maintained for review.

6.4 Incident Reporting and Investigation

All incidents, including accidents, injuries, and near misses, must be reported and investigated promptly to prevent recurrence. Per WAC 296-155-100(3), management shall investigate every injury, illness, and near miss.

1. Employees must report all incidents to their supervisor immediately, regardless of severity.

2. Supervisors initiate an investigation within 24 hours, documenting findings and corrective actions.

3. Serious incidents are reported to regulatory agencies as required by law.

6.5 Emergency Preparedness

Emergency procedures are established and communicated to all personnel to ensure a coordinated response to unforeseen events.

1. Emergency action plans (EAPs) are developed for each job site, including evacuation routes and assembly points.

2. Drills are conducted periodically to ensure employee familiarity with emergency procedures.

3. First aid and emergency contact information are prominently displayed.

6.6 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)

Appropriate PPE is provided and required for use based on identified hazards. Per 29 CFR 1926.20(b)(3), the employer shall permit only those employees qualified by training or experience to operate equipment and machinery.

1. Hazard assessments are conducted to determine the necessary PPE for each task.

2. Employees are trained on the proper use, care, and maintenance of PPE.

3. PPE is regularly inspected and replaced as needed.

7. TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

All employees, supervisors, and subcontractors receive comprehensive safety training relevant to their roles and tasks. Per WAC 296-155-100(4), management shall provide safety training to all employees.

- New employee safety orientation covering general site hazards and company policies.

- Task-specific training for hazardous operations or equipment.

- Annual refresher training on key safety topics.

- Documentation of all training sessions, including attendees, topics, and dates, is maintained by the Safety Officer.

8. INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

Compliance with safety policies is monitored through regular inspections and audits. Enforcement actions are taken to ensure adherence to safety standards.

- Daily site inspections by supervisors and foremen.

- Weekly comprehensive safety audits by the Safety Officer.

- Unsafe conditions or acts are immediately corrected, and appropriate disciplinary action is taken for non-compliance.

9. RECORDKEEPING

Accurate and up-to-date records are maintained for all safety-related activities. Per 29 CFR 1926.20(b)(1), the employer shall initiate and maintain such programs as may be necessary to comply therewith.

- Accident and incident reports (minimum 5 years retention).

- Safety meeting minutes (minimum 3 years retention).

- Training records (duration of employment plus 3 years).

- Inspection and audit reports (minimum 3 years retention).

- All records are maintained by the Safety Officer and are accessible upon request.

10. VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

Violations of safety policies will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract.

- First Offense: Verbal warning and retraining.

- Second Offense: Written warning and mandatory safety review.

- Third Offense: Suspension without pay or contract termination, depending on severity and recurrence.

- Severe violations, or those causing serious injury or property damage, may result in immediate termination.

11. REFERENCES

- OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.20(b) General Safety and Health Provisions

- WISHA: WAC 296-155-100 Management's Responsibility

- WISHA: WAC 296-155-105 Employee's Responsibility

- WISHA: WAC 296-155-110 Accident Prevention Program

- AGC: SHIP Best Practices Handbook Ch. 1

- AGC: CSEA Program Standards

sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 02 | Drug Free Work Place

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

1. Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.20(b)

OSHA

Drug-Free Workplace Act 41 U.S.C. 8101-8106

Drug-Free Workplace Act

OSHA

DOT 49 CFR Part 40

Department of Transportation

WISHA

WAC 296-155-110

Accident Prevention Program

WISHA

WAC 296-800-11035

Drug-Free Workplace

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 2

Safety and Health Manual

2. Purpose

This policy establishes a clear stance against drug and alcohol abuse to ensure a safe and productive work environment. It aims to protect all employees, subcontractors, and visitors from hazards associated with impaired judgment and performance. By implementing this policy, MH Construction, Inc. mitigates risks, maintains operational integrity, and complies with federal and state regulations.

3. Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors while on any MH Construction job site, company property, or operating company vehicles or equipment. Adherence is mandatory for all personnel engaged in company operations.

4. Definitions

Term

Definition

Drug-Free Workplace

A work environment where the unlawful manufacture, distribution, dispensation, possession, or use of a controlled substance is prohibited.

Controlled Substance

A drug or chemical whose manufacture, possession, or use is regulated by the government under the Controlled Substances Act.

Impairment

A state in which an individual’s ability to perform job functions safely and effectively is diminished due to the influence of drugs or alcohol.

Reasonable Suspicion

Belief based on specific, articulable facts and reasonable inferences drawn from those facts that an employee is under the influence of drugs or alcohol.

Safety-Sensitive Position

Any position where a lapse in judgment or inattentiveness could result in injury, death, or significant property damage.

5. Responsibilities

5.1 Company/Management Responsibilities

  • - Establish, implement, and maintain a comprehensive Drug-Free Workplace Policy.
    - Provide training and education on the policy to all employees and supervisors.
    - Ensure compliance with all federal, state, and local drug and alcohol testing regulations.
    - Provide resources for employee assistance programs (EAP) for substance abuse.
    - Conduct investigations into alleged violations of this policy.

5.2 Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • - Enforce the Drug-Free Workplace Policy consistently and fairly.
    - Observe and document behaviors that may indicate drug or alcohol use.
    - Initiate reasonable suspicion testing procedures when appropriate.
    - Ensure employees under their supervision receive required training.
    - Maintain confidentiality regarding all drug and alcohol-related incidents.

5.3 Employee Responsibilities

  • - Adhere to all aspects of the Drug-Free Workplace Policy.
    - Report to work fit for duty and free from the influence of drugs or alcohol.
    - Cooperate with all drug and alcohol testing procedures.
    - Seek assistance for substance abuse problems through available resources.
    - Report any policy violations observed on the job site.

5.4 Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • - Ensure their employees comply with MH Construction, Inc.'s Drug-Free Workplace Policy while on MHC job sites.
    - Implement and enforce their own drug and alcohol policies that meet or exceed MHC standards.
    - Provide documentation of compliance upon request.
    - Remove any employee from an MHC job site who violates this policy.

6. Procedures / Requirements

6.1 Prohibited Conduct

The following conduct is strictly prohibited: the unlawful manufacture, distribution, dispensation, possession, or use of controlled substances, and the consumption of alcohol, on company property, job sites, or while conducting company business. Violation of this policy will result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination. (Refer to OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.20(b) for general safety and health provisions).

6.2 Drug and Alcohol Testing

MH Construction, Inc. reserves the right to conduct drug and alcohol testing under various circumstances, including pre-employment, reasonable suspicion, post-accident, return-to-duty, and follow-up testing. All testing will be conducted in accordance with federal and state regulations, including DOT 49 CFR Part 40 for safety-sensitive positions. (Refer to WISHA: WAC 296-800-11035 for Drug-Free Workplace requirements).

6.3 Reasonable Suspicion Testing Procedures

Supervisors trained in reasonable suspicion identification will document specific, articulable observations concerning an employee's appearance, behavior, speech, or body odors. These observations will be reviewed with another supervisor or management representative. If reasonable suspicion is confirmed, the employee will be immediately removed from duty and transported for testing. Failure to submit to testing will be considered a refusal and will result in disciplinary action.

6.4 Post-Accident Testing

Employees involved in an accident that results in a fatality, injury requiring medical treatment beyond first aid, or significant property damage will be subject to drug and alcohol testing. Testing will occur as soon as practicable following the incident. (Refer to WISHA: WAC 296-155-110 for Accident Prevention Program guidelines, which includes provisions for incident investigation).

6.5 Return-to-Duty and Follow-Up Testing

Employees who have violated this policy and are permitted to return to work must undergo a return-to-duty drug and alcohol test with a negative result. They will also be subject to unannounced follow-up testing for a period determined by a Substance Abuse Professional (SAP), in accordance with DOT 49 CFR Part 40, if applicable.

6.6 Confidentiality

All information related to drug and alcohol testing and policy violations will be treated with strict confidentiality, shared only on a need-to-know basis, and in accordance with applicable privacy laws.

6.7 Employee Assistance Program (EAP)

MH Construction, Inc. encourages employees with substance abuse problems to seek help through the company's Employee Assistance Program or other appropriate resources. Seeking assistance will not be used as a basis for disciplinary action, provided the employee complies with all treatment recommendations and policy requirements.

7. Training Requirements

All employees will receive initial and periodic training on the Drug-Free Workplace Policy, including the effects of drugs and alcohol on health and safety, and available assistance programs. Supervisors will receive additional training on reasonable suspicion identification and documentation. Training records, including dates, attendees, and topics covered, will be maintained by the Safety Officer. (Refer to OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.21 for safety training and education requirements).

8. Inspection and Enforcement

Compliance with this policy will be monitored through regular job site inspections, supervisor observations, and incident investigations. Any observed violations will be immediately addressed. Corrective actions will be implemented promptly, and follow-up will ensure sustained compliance. The Safety Officer is responsible for overseeing enforcement activities.

9. Recordkeeping

Records related to drug and alcohol testing, training, policy violations, and disciplinary actions will be maintained by the HR Department and/or Safety Officer for a minimum of five years, or as required by federal and state regulations. These records will be kept confidential and separate from personnel files.

10. Violation Consequences

Violations of this Drug-Free Workplace Policy will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including immediate termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and severity of the violation, prior infractions, and compliance with any required rehabilitation programs. MH Construction, Inc. reserves the right to take immediate and severe disciplinary action for serious violations.

11. References

OSHA: 29 CFR - 1926.20(b)

OSHA: Drug-Free Workplace Act 41 U.S.C. 8101-8106 - Drug-Free Workplace Act

OSHA: DOT 49 CFR Part 40 - Department of Transportation

WISHA: WAC 296-155-110 - Accident Prevention Program

WISHA: WAC 296-800-11035 - Drug-Free Workplace

AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 2 - Safety and Health Manual

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 03 | Program Policy and Requirements Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey ────────────────────────────────────────────────── Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926. 20 General Safety OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH Construction, Inc.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 03 | Program Policy and Requirements

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

──────────────────────────────────────────────────

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.20 General Safety

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.21(b) Safety Training and Education

WISHA

WAC

296-155-100

WISHA

WAC

296-155-105

WISHA

WAC

296-155-110

WISHA

WAC

296-155-115 Safety Bulletin Board

AGC

SHIP Best Practices Handbook

AGC

AGC CSEA Program Requirements


Purpose

This policy establishes the framework for a safe and healthy work environment at MH Construction, Inc. It aims to prevent workplace injuries, illnesses, and fatalities by outlining clear guidelines and responsibilities for all personnel involved in company operations.

Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors on MH Construction job sites. Adherence to these requirements is mandatory to ensure compliance with regulatory standards and company safety objectives.

Definitions

Term

Definition

Competent Person

One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Hazard

A condition or situation that has the potential for harm or adverse health effects on people, property, or the environment.

MISH Program

MH Construction Industrial Safety & Health Program Manual, a comprehensive document outlining the company's safety policies, procedures, and requirements.

OSHA

Occupational Safety and Health Administration, a federal agency that sets and enforces protective workplace safety and health standards.

WISHA

Washington Industrial Safety and Health Act, the state-level occupational safety and health program in Washington, often more stringent than federal OSHA.


Responsibilities

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Establish and maintain a safe and healthy work environment.
  • Provide necessary resources for safety programs.
  • Ensure compliance with all applicable safety regulations.
  • Conduct regular safety audits and reviews.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Enforce safety policies and procedures on job sites.
  • Conduct regular safety inspections and toolbox talks.
  • Ensure employees are properly trained and equipped.
  • Investigate all incidents and near-misses.

Employee Responsibilities

  • Adhere to all safety rules and procedures.
  • Report hazards, incidents, and near-misses immediately.
  • Use personal protective equipment (PPE) correctly.
  • Participate in safety training programs.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Comply with MH Construction's safety policies and all applicable regulations.
  • Provide their employees with necessary safety training and equipment.
  • Coordinate safety efforts with MH Construction site management.
  • Report all incidents involving their personnel to MH Construction.

Procedures / Requirements

7.1 General Safety and Health Provisions

MH Construction will initiate and maintain accident prevention programs to ensure safe working conditions. These programs will include frequent and regular inspections of job sites, materials, and equipment by competent persons. Per 29 CFR 1926.20 General Safety, all employers must comply with the safety and health standards.

7.2 Safety Training and Education

All employees will receive training to recognize and avoid unsafe conditions and to understand the regulations applicable to their work environment. This training will be conducted by competent persons. Per 29 CFR 1926.21(b) Safety Training and Education, employers must instruct each employee in the recognition and avoidance of unsafe conditions.

7.3 Accident Prevention Program (APP)

MH Construction maintains a written Accident Prevention Program (APP) as required by WAC 296-155-100. This program is designed to effectively implement the requirements of the WISHA safety and health standards and to address the specific hazards of our operations.

7.4 Safety Meetings

Regular safety meetings will be held with all employees to discuss safety performance, review incidents, and provide ongoing training. These meetings will be documented. Per WAC 296-155-105, employers must hold safety meetings at least monthly for all employees.

7.5 Safety and Health Committee

A safety and health committee will be established and maintained in accordance with WAC 296-155-110. This committee will assist in the development and implementation of the APP, conduct inspections, and review incident reports.

7.6 Safety Bulletin Board

A safety bulletin board will be maintained at each job site, displaying required posters, emergency contact information, and safety-related notices. Per WAC 296-155-115 Safety Bulletin Board, specific information must be posted for employee access.

7.7 AGC Best Practices

MH Construction incorporates best practices from the AGC SHIP Best Practices Handbook to enhance our safety program beyond minimum regulatory requirements, focusing on proactive hazard identification and risk mitigation.

Training Requirements

All new employees will receive initial safety orientation. Ongoing training will be provided annually or as needed for new hazards or equipment. Training records, including dates, topics, and attendees, will be maintained by the Safety Officer. Specific training per 29 CFR 1926.21(b) and WAC 296-155-100 will be documented.

Inspection and Enforcement

Job sites will undergo frequent and regular safety inspections by competent persons. Any identified deficiencies will be documented, and corrective actions will be implemented promptly. Non-compliance with safety policies will result in disciplinary action as outlined in the Violation Consequences section.

Recordkeeping

All safety-related documents, including training records, inspection reports, incident investigations, and safety meeting minutes, will be retained for a minimum of five years. The Safety Officer is responsible for maintaining these records.

Violation Consequences

Violations of safety policies will be subject to progressive disciplinary action, ranging from verbal warnings for minor infractions to termination for serious or repeated offenses. The severity of the consequence will depend on the nature and impact of the violation.

References

  • - OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.20 General Safety
  • - OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.21(b) Safety Training and Education
  • - WISHA: WAC 296-155-100
  • - WISHA: WAC 296-155-105
  • - WISHA: WAC 296-155-110
  • - WISHA: WAC 296-155-115 Safety Bulletin Board
  • - AGC: SHIP Best Practices Handbook
  • - AGC: AGC CSEA Program Requirements

sections.title: safety and health orientation
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 04 | Safety and Health Orientation Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 04 | Safety and Health Orientation

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey


────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.21(b)(2) Employer must instruct each employee in recognition and avoidance of unsafe conditions

N/A

WISHA

WAC 296-155-110(3)

Accident Prevention Program Training

WISHA

WAC 296-155-115

Safety Bulletin Board

AGC

Jobsite Safety Handbook

N/A

AGC

AGC SHIP Ch. 3

N/A

Purpose

This policy establishes the framework for a comprehensive Safety and Health Orientation program at MH Construction, Inc. It aims to ensure all personnel are aware of potential hazards and the measures required to maintain a safe working environment, thereby preventing accidents and promoting compliance with regulatory standards.

Scope

This program applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, temporary workers, and visitors present on any MH Construction job site or facility. Adherence to these guidelines is mandatory for all individuals to ensure a consistent and high standard of safety across all operations.

Definitions

Term

Definition

Competent Person

An individual designated by MH Construction, Inc. who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Hazard Communication

The process of informing employees about chemical hazards in the workplace and the necessary precautions to take. This includes Safety Data Sheets (SDS) and proper labeling.

Job Hazard Analysis (JHA)

A procedure which helps integrate accepted safety and health principles and practices into a particular task or job operation. In a JHA, each basic step of the job is examined to identify potential hazards and to determine the safest way to do the job.

Orientation

The formal process of introducing new or transferred employees, as well as subcontractors and visitors, to the safety policies, procedures, and site-specific hazards of MH Construction, Inc. before they commence work.

Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)

Specialized clothing or equipment worn by employees for protection against health and safety hazards. Examples include hard hats, safety glasses, high-visibility vests, and steel-toed boots.

Responsibilities

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Develop, implement, and maintain a comprehensive Safety and Health Orientation program.
  • Provide necessary resources, including training materials and qualified personnel, to conduct effective orientations.
  • Ensure compliance with all applicable OSHA, WISHA, and AGC safety regulations and standards.
  • Regularly review and update the MISH Program to reflect current regulations and best practices.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Conduct site-specific safety orientations for all new employees, subcontractors, and visitors before they begin work.
  • Ensure all personnel under their supervision understand and adhere to MH Construction's safety policies and procedures.
  • Verify that all required training is completed and documented.
  • Identify and correct unsafe conditions or acts promptly, and report all incidents and near-misses.

Employee Responsibilities

  • Attend and actively participate in all required safety orientations and training sessions.
  • Comply with all MH Construction safety rules, policies, and procedures, as well as all applicable regulatory requirements.
  • Report any unsafe conditions, hazards, or incidents to their supervisor immediately.
  • Use all assigned Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) correctly and maintain it in good condition.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Ensure their employees receive comprehensive safety orientation relevant to the scope of work and job site hazards.
  • Comply with all MH Construction safety policies and procedures, and all applicable OSHA and WISHA regulations.
  • Provide their employees with appropriate PPE and ensure its correct use and maintenance.
  • Coordinate with MH Construction supervisors regarding site-specific hazards and emergency procedures.

Procedures / Requirements

7.1 General Orientation Requirements

All new employees, transferred employees, subcontractors, and visitors must receive a safety and health orientation prior to commencing work on any MH Construction, Inc. job site. This orientation will cover general safety policies and site-specific hazards. Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.21(b)(2), employers must instruct each employee in the recognition and avoidance of unsafe conditions and the regulations applicable to their work environment to control or eliminate any hazards or other exposure to illness or injury.

7.2 Content of General Orientation

The general orientation will include, but not be limited to, the following topics:

  1. MH Construction's safety philosophy and commitment to an injury-free workplace.
  2. Overview of the MISH Program and its importance.
  3. Emergency procedures, including evacuation routes, assembly points, and first aid locations.
  4. Hazard communication program, including access to Safety Data Sheets (SDS) and understanding of chemical labels.
  5. Reporting procedures for incidents, near-misses, and unsafe conditions.
  6. General site rules, including personal protective equipment (PPE) requirements, housekeeping, and prohibited activities.
  7. Drug and alcohol policy.
  8. Fire prevention and protection.
  9. Electrical safety awareness.
  10. Fall protection basics.

7.3 Site-Specific Orientation

In addition to the general orientation, a site-specific orientation must be conducted for all personnel entering a new job site. This orientation will address hazards unique to that particular site. Per WAC 296-155-110(3), employers must ensure that all employees are trained in the specific hazards of their job and the job site.

  1. Identification of site-specific hazards (e.g., excavations, confined spaces, overhead power lines, traffic patterns).
  2. Emergency contact information and procedures specific to the site.
  3. Location of first aid stations, fire extinguishers, and emergency exits.
  4. Site-specific communication protocols and reporting requirements.
  5. Traffic control plans and pedestrian safety routes.
  6. Specific equipment and machinery hazards present on site.

7.4 Documentation of Orientation

All orientations, both general and site-specific, must be documented. This documentation will include the date of orientation, topics covered, and signatures of attendees, acknowledging their understanding of the information presented. Records will be maintained by the Safety Officer or designated personnel.

7.5 Refresher Orientations

Refresher orientations will be conducted periodically, or when there are significant changes to job tasks, equipment, or site conditions, or when an employee demonstrates a lack of understanding or adherence to safety procedures. This ensures continuous awareness and compliance with evolving safety standards.

7.6 Safety Bulletin Board

Each job site will maintain a Safety Bulletin Board, prominently displayed and easily accessible to all employees. This board will contain essential safety information, including emergency contacts, safety policies, and relevant regulatory posters. Per WAC 296-155-115, a safety bulletin board or a similar system must be used to disseminate safety information.

Training Requirements

All MH Construction, Inc. employees, supervisors, and subcontractors must undergo initial safety and health orientation as described in Section 7.1. Training frequency will be determined by job role, exposure to hazards, and regulatory requirements. Documentation of all training, including dates, topics, and attendees, will be maintained by the Safety Officer. Applicable OSHA and WISHA training mandates, such as those outlined in 29 CFR 1926.21(b)(2) and WAC 296-155-110(3), will be strictly adhered to.

Inspection and Enforcement

Compliance with the Safety and Health Orientation program will be monitored through regular site inspections conducted by supervisors, foremen, and the Safety Officer. Inspections will occur at least weekly, or more frequently based on site-specific hazards and activities. Any non-compliance or unsafe conditions identified will result in immediate corrective action, documented and tracked to ensure resolution. Failure to comply with safety procedures will be addressed through the progressive discipline policy outlined in Section 11.

Recordkeeping

The following records related to Safety and Health Orientation will be maintained:

  • Orientation attendance logs, including dates, topics covered, and attendee signatures.
  • Training certificates and qualifications for specialized safety training.
  • Records of safety meetings and toolbox talks.
  • Documentation of corrective actions taken for identified non-compliance.

All records will be retained for a minimum of five (5) years, or as required by specific regulatory standards, and will be managed by the Safety Officer.

Violation Consequences

Violation of MH Construction, Inc. safety policies, including failure to adhere to orientation requirements, will result in progressive disciplinary action. This may include verbal warnings, written warnings, suspension, and ultimately, termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and frequency of the violation, as well as its potential impact on safety.

References

OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.21(b)(2) Employer must instruct each employee in recognition and avoidance of unsafe conditions N/A

sections.title: pre job safety planning
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 05 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Matt Ramsey ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 1.  Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926. 20(b)(1) Initiation and maintenance of programs OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 05

3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Matt Ramsey

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.20(b)(1) Initiation and maintenance of programs

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.21(b)

WISHA

WAC 296-155-110(3)

Accident Prevention Program Planning

WISHA

WAC 296-155-035

General Requirements

AGC

JHA Guidelines

AGC

AGC SHIP Best Practices Handbook Ch. 4

2. Purpose

This policy establishes the framework for proactive safety planning prior to the commencement of any work activities. It aims to identify and mitigate potential hazards, ensuring a safe working environment for all personnel and preventing accidents and injuries on MH Construction job sites.

3. Scope

This program applies to all MH Construction employees, subcontractors, vendors, and visitors engaged in or present on any MH Construction project site. Adherence to these guidelines is mandatory to maintain a consistent standard of safety across all operations.

4. Definitions

Term

Definition

Job Hazard Analysis (JHA)

A systematic process to identify potential hazards of a job or task, recommend corrective actions, and develop safe work procedures.

Competent Person

One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Pre-job Safety Meeting

A meeting conducted before the start of a job or specific task to discuss potential hazards, safety procedures, and assign responsibilities.

Site-Specific Safety Plan (SSSP)

A comprehensive document outlining the specific safety policies, procedures, and responsibilities for a particular construction project.

Near Miss

An unplanned event that did not result in injury, illness, or damage but had the potential to do so.

5. Responsibilities

5.1 Company/Management

  • - Provide necessary resources for safety planning and implementation.
    - Ensure all projects have adequate safety oversight and support.
    - Establish and maintain a culture of safety throughout the organization.
    - Review and approve Site-Specific Safety Plans (SSSPs).

5.2 Supervisor/Foreman

  • - Conduct and document Job Hazard Analyses (JHAs) for all tasks.
    - Lead pre-job safety meetings and toolbox talks.
    - Ensure all personnel understand and follow safety procedures.
    - Implement corrective actions for identified hazards.
    - Verify subcontractor compliance with safety requirements.

5.3 Employee

  • - Participate actively in safety planning and meetings.
    - Understand and adhere to all safety policies and procedures.
    - Report all hazards, incidents, and near misses immediately.
    - Use appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE).

5.4 Subcontractor

  • - Develop and implement their own safety programs compliant with OSHA, WISHA, and MHC requirements.
    - Provide competent personnel for all tasks.
    - Coordinate safety planning with MH Construction supervisors.
    - Ensure their employees are trained and follow all safety protocols.

6. Procedures / Requirements

6.1 Pre-Job Hazard Assessment

  1. 1. Before commencing any new project or task, a comprehensive hazard assessment shall be conducted by a Competent Person. This assessment will identify potential safety and health risks associated with the work.
    2. The assessment shall consider all aspects of the job, including but not limited to, equipment, materials, work environment, and personnel. Per 29 CFR 1926.20(b)(1), employers must initiate and maintain accident prevention programs.

6.2 Job Hazard Analysis (JHA) Development

  1. 1. For each distinct task, a Job Hazard Analysis (JHA) must be developed. The JHA will break down the task into steps, identify hazards at each step, and prescribe control measures.
    2. JHAs must be reviewed and approved by the project supervisor and communicated to all affected employees prior to task execution. Refer to AGC JHA Guidelines for best practices.

6.3 Site-Specific Safety Plan (SSSP)

  1. 1. A Site-Specific Safety Plan (SSSP) shall be developed for each project, detailing the unique safety requirements, emergency procedures, and roles for that site.
    2. The SSSP must be accessible on-site and reviewed with all personnel. This aligns with WAC 296-155-110(3) regarding Accident Prevention Program Planning.

6.4 Pre-Job Safety Meetings

  1. 1. Prior to the start of any new project or significant phase, a pre-job safety meeting shall be held with all involved personnel, including subcontractors.
    2. The meeting will cover the SSSP, relevant JHAs, emergency contacts, and site-specific hazards. Per 29 CFR 1926.21(b), employers are responsible for instructing employees in the recognition and avoidance of unsafe conditions.

6.5 Subcontractor Safety Integration

  1. 1. All subcontractors must submit their safety programs and relevant JHAs for review and approval by MH Construction prior to mobilization.
    2. Subcontractors are required to participate in MH Construction's safety meetings and adhere to the project's SSSP. This is critical for compliance with WAC 296-155-035 General Requirements.

6.6 Emergency Preparedness

  1. 1. Emergency response procedures, including evacuation routes, assembly points, and first aid locations, shall be clearly defined in the SSSP and communicated during pre-job meetings.
    2. Emergency contact information for all key personnel and local emergency services must be readily available on site.

7. Training Requirements

All employees, supervisors, and foremen involved in pre-job safety planning activities must receive initial and annual refresher training on hazard recognition, JHA development, and SSSP implementation. Training documentation, including dates, topics, and attendees, shall be maintained by the Safety Officer. Specific training mandates from OSHA 29 CFR 1926.21(b) and WAC 296-155-110(3) must be met.

8. Inspection and Enforcement

Compliance with pre-job safety planning procedures will be monitored through regular site inspections and audits conducted by the Safety Officer or designated Competent Person. Any non-compliance or deficiencies identified will result in immediate corrective action, documented and tracked to completion. Failure to adhere to these procedures may lead to disciplinary action as outlined in the Violation Consequences section.

9. Recordkeeping

The following records related to pre-job safety planning shall be maintained for a minimum of five years: Job Hazard Analyses (JHAs), Site-Specific Safety Plans (SSSPs), pre-job safety meeting minutes, and hazard assessment reports. The Safety Officer is responsible for the secure retention and accessibility of these documents.

10. Violation Consequences

Any violation of the Pre-job Safety Planning policy will be subject to MH Construction's progressive disciplinary policy. This may include verbal warnings, written warnings, suspension, and ultimately, termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and frequency of the violation, as well as its potential impact on safety.

11. References

  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.20(b)(1) - Initiation and maintenance of programs
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.21(b) - Safety training and education
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-110(3) - Accident Prevention Program Planning
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-035 - General Requirements
  • AGC: JHA Guidelines
  • AGC: SHIP Best Practices Handbook Ch. 4

sections.title: emergency response
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 06 | Emergency Response Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey REGULATORY AUTHORITY Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926. 35 Employee Emergency Action Plans OSHA 29 CFR 1910.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 06 | Emergency Response

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

REGULATORY AUTHORITY

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.35

Employee Emergency Action Plans

OSHA

29 CFR 1910.38

Emergency Action Plans

WISHA

WAC 296-155-140

Sanitation Emergency provisions

WISHA

WAC 296-800-310

Emergency Procedures

WISHA

WAC 296-155-120

First-Aid Training

AGC

Ch. 4

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 4 Emergency Preparedness

PURPOSE

This policy establishes clear guidelines for emergency response procedures, ensuring the safety and well-being of all personnel on MH Construction job sites. It aims to mitigate risks associated with unforeseen emergencies and protect against potential injuries or property damage.

SCOPE

This Emergency Response policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors present on any MH Construction job site. Adherence to these procedures is mandatory to maintain a safe working environment.

DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Emergency Action Plan (EAP)

A written plan required by OSHA outlining procedures for employees to follow in emergency situations.

First Aid

Immediate care given to an injured or ill person to prevent further injury or to preserve life.

Evacuation

The organized, rapid, and safe movement of people from an area of danger to a safe location.

Assembly Point

A designated safe location where all personnel are to gather after an evacuation.

Incident Commander

The individual responsible for managing all incident operations at the emergency scene.

RESPONSIBILITIES

  • Company/Management Responsibilities:
  • Develop, implement, and maintain a comprehensive Emergency Action Plan (EAP) for each project. (Per 29 CFR 1910.38)
  • Ensure adequate resources, including first-aid supplies and emergency equipment, are available on site.
  • Provide training and information to all employees regarding emergency procedures.
  • Conduct regular drills and exercises to test the effectiveness of emergency plans.
  • Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities:
  • Ensure all personnel under their supervision are aware of and understand the project-specific EAP.
  • Designate and train employees to assist in safe and orderly emergency evacuation. (Per 29 CFR 1926.35)
  • Verify that first-aid trained personnel are available on site as required. (Per WAC 296-155-120)
  • Lead and account for all personnel during emergency evacuations.
  • Employee Responsibilities:
  • Familiarize themselves with the project-specific EAP and emergency procedures.
  • Know the location of emergency exits, assembly points, and first-aid stations.
  • Report any potential emergency hazards or unsafe conditions immediately.
  • Follow all instructions from supervisors and emergency personnel during an incident.
  • Subcontractor Responsibilities:
  • Develop and implement their own emergency procedures that align with MH Construction's EAP.
  • Ensure their employees are trained in and adhere to all emergency response protocols.
  • Coordinate emergency response efforts with MH Construction site management.

PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

  1. 7.1 Emergency Action Plan (EAP) Development:
  2. A written EAP will be developed for each project, specific to the site's hazards and layout. This plan will include procedures for reporting emergencies, evacuation, shutdown operations, and rescue/medical duties. (Per 29 CFR 1926.35, 29 CFR 1910.38)
  3. 7.2 Evacuation Procedures:
  4. Upon activation of an alarm or notification of an emergency, all personnel must immediately evacuate the affected area and proceed to the designated assembly point. Supervisors will ensure a headcount is taken at the assembly point. (Per 29 CFR 1926.35)
  5. 7.3 First Aid and Medical Attention:
  6. Adequate first-aid supplies will be readily available on all job sites. Personnel trained in first aid and CPR will be identified and accessible. In case of serious injury, emergency medical services (911) will be contacted immediately. (Per WAC 296-155-120)
  7. 7.4 Fire Prevention and Response:
  8. Fire extinguishers will be strategically located and regularly inspected. Employees will be trained on the proper use of fire extinguishers and evacuation routes in case of fire. (Per WAC 296-800-310)
  9. 7.5 Hazardous Material Spills:
  10. Procedures for handling and containing hazardous material spills will be included in the EAP. Only trained personnel with appropriate PPE will attempt to contain spills. Emergency services will be notified for significant spills.
  11. 7.6 Communication During Emergencies:
  12. A clear communication plan will be established to inform all personnel of emergency status and instructions. This may include alarms, two-way radios, or designated runners. (Per 29 CFR 1910.38)
  13. 7.7 Emergency Provisions for Sanitation:
  14. In the event of an emergency that disrupts normal sanitation facilities, alternative provisions will be made to ensure employee health and hygiene. (Per WAC 296-155-140)
  15. 7.8 Shelter-in-Place Procedures:
  16. In situations where evacuation is not the safest option (e.g., chemical release outside the site), shelter-in-place procedures will be implemented. Designated safe areas will be identified, and personnel will be instructed to seal off ventilation and await further instructions.

TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

All MH Construction employees will receive initial and annual refresher training on the project-specific Emergency Action Plan (EAP). Training will cover evacuation routes, assembly points, reporting procedures, and the location and use of emergency equipment. Documentation of all training will be maintained by the Safety Officer. (Per 29 CFR 1910.38, WAC 296-800-310)

Supervisors and designated personnel will receive additional training in first aid, CPR, and advanced emergency response techniques. (Per WAC 296-155-120)

INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

The Safety Officer and project management will conduct regular inspections to ensure compliance with all emergency response procedures and the availability of necessary equipment. Any deficiencies will be documented, and corrective actions will be implemented promptly. Emergency drills will be conducted periodically to evaluate the effectiveness of the EAP and identify areas for improvement.

RECORDKEEPING

The following records will be maintained by the Safety Officer for a minimum of three years:

  • Emergency Action Plans (EAPs) for each project.
  • Records of all emergency response training, including dates, attendees, and topics covered.
  • Documentation of emergency drills, including observations and corrective actions.
  • Incident reports related to emergencies and their outcomes.

VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

Failure to comply with the Emergency Response policy and procedures may result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and impact of the violation.

REFERENCES

  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.35 Employee Emergency Action Plans
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1910.38 Emergency Action Plans
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-140 Sanitation Emergency provisions
  • WISHA: WAC 296-800-310 Emergency Procedures
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-120 First-Aid Training
  • AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 4 Emergency Preparedness

sections.title: safety bulletin boards
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 07 | Safety Bulletin Boards Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey 1.  REGULATORY AUTHORITY Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1903. 2 Posting of Notice OSHA 29 CFR 1903.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 07 | Safety Bulletin Boards

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey



1. REGULATORY AUTHORITY

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29

CFR 1903.2 Posting of Notice

OSHA

29

CFR 1903.16 Posting of Citations

WISHA

WAC

296-155-115 Safety Bulletin Board required postings

WISHA

WAC

296-800-350 Posting Requirements

AGC

Jobsite Safety Handbook

2. PURPOSE

This policy establishes guidelines for the proper use and maintenance of safety bulletin boards to ensure critical safety information is effectively communicated. It protects employees, subcontractors, and visitors by providing readily accessible information regarding workplace hazards, emergency procedures, and regulatory requirements.

3. SCOPE

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors on MH Construction job sites.

4. DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Safety Bulletin Board

A designated physical or digital display area used to post mandatory safety notices, warnings, and other pertinent information.

Regulatory Citations

Specific references to OSHA, WISHA, or other governmental safety standards that mandate certain practices or postings.

Emergency Procedures

Established protocols to be followed in the event of an unforeseen incident, such as fire, injury, or natural disaster.

Jobsite Safety Handbook

A comprehensive guide provided by the Associated General Contractors (AGC) detailing best practices and compliance requirements for construction site safety.

Posting Requirements

Mandatory display of specific notices, permits, or summaries as stipulated by regulatory bodies like OSHA and WISHA.

5. RESPONSIBILITIES

5.1 Company/Management

  • The Company/Management is responsible for:
  • Providing and maintaining adequate safety bulletin boards.
  • Ensuring compliance with all federal and state posting requirements.
  • Allocating resources for the creation and distribution of safety information.

5.2 Supervisor/Foreman

  • Supervisors/Foremen are responsible for:
  • Regularly checking bulletin boards for current and relevant information.
  • Ensuring all mandatory postings are present and legible.
  • Communicating key safety messages from the bulletin boards to their teams.

5.3 Employee

  • Employees are responsible for:
  • Reviewing information posted on safety bulletin boards regularly.
  • Reporting any missing or outdated postings to their supervisor.
  • Adhering to all safety policies and procedures communicated via bulletin boards.

5.4 Subcontractor

  • Subcontractors are responsible for:
  • Familiarizing themselves and their employees with information on MH Construction safety bulletin boards.
  • Complying with all safety directives and regulatory postings.
  • Maintaining their own required safety postings as per their contractual obligations.

6. PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

6.1 General Posting Requirements

  1. Safety bulletin boards shall be prominently displayed in a conspicuous location accessible to all employees, subcontractors, and visitors. Per 29 CFR 1903.2, the OSHA "Job Safety and Health: It\'s The Law!" poster must be displayed. Additionally, per WAC 296-155-115, specific Washington State required postings must be present.

6.2 Mandatory Postings

  1. The following items are mandatory postings on all MH Construction safety bulletin boards:
  • OSHA Poster (Job Safety and Health: It\'s The Law!)
  • WISHA Poster (Your Rights as a Worker)
  • Emergency contact information and procedures.
  • Company safety policy statement.
  • Workers\' Compensation information.
  • Safety data sheets (SDS) for hazardous materials present on site, or clear instructions on where to access them.
  • Current project-specific safety plan.

6.3 Posting of Citations and Notices

  1. Any OSHA citations or notices of unsafe conditions received by MH Construction must be posted immediately at or near the place of the violation for three working days or until the violation is abated, whichever is longer. Per 29 CFR 1903.16, these must remain posted for the specified period.

6.4 Maintenance and Updates

  1. Bulletin boards shall be kept neat, orderly, and up-to-date. Outdated or irrelevant information must be removed promptly. Supervisors are responsible for ensuring the accuracy and currency of all posted materials. Per WAC 296-800-350, employers must ensure that required notices are maintained in good condition.

6.5 Accessibility

  1. Safety bulletin boards must be located in areas easily accessible and visible to all personnel, such as break rooms, main entrances, or designated safety stations. They should be protected from weather and damage to ensure legibility.

6.6 Digital Bulletin Boards

  1. Where digital bulletin boards are utilized, they must meet the same accessibility and content requirements as physical boards. Digital displays must be regularly monitored for functionality and content accuracy, ensuring all mandatory postings are clearly visible and easily navigable.

6.7 Language Requirements

  1. Where a significant portion of the workforce speaks a language other than English, critical safety information and mandatory postings should be provided in those languages in addition to English, to ensure effective communication.

7. TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

All new employees and subcontractors shall be oriented to the location and content of safety bulletin boards during their initial site-specific safety orientation. Training shall cover the importance of reviewing posted information, identifying mandatory postings, and understanding emergency procedures. Documentation of this training shall be maintained by the Safety Officer. Applicable OSHA and WISHA training mandates related to hazard communication and emergency action plans are reinforced through these postings.

8. INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

Supervisors and the Safety Officer will conduct weekly inspections of all safety bulletin boards to ensure compliance with posting requirements. Any deficiencies will be noted, and corrective actions will be implemented immediately. Non-compliance with posting requirements or failure to maintain bulletin boards will result in corrective action, which may include disciplinary measures for responsible parties.

9. RECORDKEEPING

Records of bulletin board inspections, including dates, findings, and corrective actions, shall be maintained by the Safety Officer for a minimum of three years. Documentation of employee and subcontractor orientation regarding bulletin board content will also be retained as part of their training records.

10. VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

Failure to comply with the Safety Bulletin Board policy may result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. Violations may also lead to regulatory fines and penalties for MH Construction, Inc. All personnel are expected to adhere strictly to these requirements to maintain a safe working environment.

11. REFERENCES

OSHA: 29 CFR 1903.2 Posting of Notice

OSHA: 29 CFR 1903.16 Posting of Citations

WISHA: WAC 296-155-115 Safety Bulletin Board required postings

WISHA: WAC 296-800-350 Posting Requirements

AGC: Jobsite Safety Handbook

sections.title: event reporting of incidents accidents and near misses
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.
Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)
MISH 08 | Event Reporting of Incidents, Accidents, And Near Misses
Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey


────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1904

Recording and Reporting Occupational Injuries and Illnesses

OSHA

29 CFR 1904.39

Reporting Fatalities and Hospitalizations

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.20

(b)(3)

WISHA

WAC 296-27 Recording and Reporting Occupational Injuries and Illnesses

WISHA

WAC 296-155-110

(5)

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 5 Incident Reporting


Purpose

This policy establishes guidelines for the comprehensive reporting and investigation of all incidents, accidents, and near misses to ensure a safe working environment. It aims to identify root causes, implement corrective actions, and prevent recurrence, thereby protecting employees, subcontractors, and company assets from harm.

Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors present on any MH Construction job site or property.

Definitions

Term

Definition

Incident

An unplanned event that did not result in injury, illness, or damage, but had the potential to do so.

Accident

An unplanned event that results in injury, illness, or property damage.

Near Miss

An unplanned event that did not result in injury, illness, or damage, but had the potential to do so. (Often used interchangeably with Incident)

First Aid Case

Any one-time treatment and subsequent observation of minor scratches, cuts, burns, splinters, etc., which do not ordinarily require medical care. Such treatment and observation are considered first aid even though provided by a physician or registered professional personnel.

Recordable Injury/Illness

An injury or illness that meets the OSHA criteria for recording on the OSHA 300 Log, including fatalities, lost workdays, restricted work/transfer to another job, medical treatment beyond first aid, or other diagnosed significant injuries/illnesses.


Responsibilities

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Provide a safe working environment and necessary resources for incident reporting and investigation.
  • Ensure all incidents, accidents, and near misses are thoroughly investigated and documented.
  • Implement corrective actions to prevent recurrence of incidents.
  • Maintain accurate records of all incident reports and investigations.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Ensure immediate first aid or medical attention for injured employees.
  • Secure the incident scene to prevent further injury or property damage.
  • Initiate incident investigations promptly and thoroughly.
  • Complete and submit all required incident reports within specified timeframes.
  • Communicate findings and corrective actions to affected employees.

Employee Responsibilities

  • Report all incidents, accidents, and near misses to their supervisor immediately, regardless of severity.
  • Cooperate fully with all incident investigations.
  • Adhere to all safety policies and procedures to prevent incidents.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Comply with all MH Construction safety policies and procedures.
  • Report all incidents, accidents, and near misses involving their employees or operations to MH Construction management immediately.
  • Participate in incident investigations as required by MH Construction.

Procedures / Requirements

  1. Immediate Reporting: All employees, subcontractors, and visitors must report any incident, accident, or near miss to their immediate supervisor or MH Construction management as soon as possible, but no later than the end of the shift during which the event occurred. For serious incidents (e.g., those requiring hospitalization or resulting in fatality), reporting must be immediate. Per OSHA 29 CFR 1904.39, fatalities must be reported within 8 hours, and inpatient hospitalizations, amputations, or losses of an eye must be reported within 24 hours.
  2. Thorough Investigation: All reported incidents, accidents, and near misses will be thoroughly investigated by the immediate supervisor, Safety Officer, or designated investigation team. The investigation will aim to identify root causes, contributing factors, and necessary corrective actions. Per WAC 296-27, employers must investigate all incidents that result in or had the potential to result in serious injury or illness.
  3. Detailed Documentation: All incidents must be documented using the MH Construction Incident Report Form. This form must include: date and time of incident, location, individuals involved, witnesses, description of the event, type of injury/damage, immediate actions taken, and recommended corrective actions. Per OSHA 29 CFR 1904, employers are required to keep records of work-related injuries and illnesses.
  4. Medical Attention: For any injury, appropriate first aid or medical treatment must be sought immediately. Supervisors must ensure injured employees receive prompt medical care. All first aid cases should also be documented internally.
  5. Implementation of Corrective Actions: Based on investigation findings, effective corrective actions will be developed and implemented to prevent recurrence. These actions may include changes to procedures, equipment modifications, additional training, or disciplinary actions. The effectiveness of corrective actions will be monitored.
  6. Communication: Investigation findings and implemented corrective actions will be communicated to all relevant employees and subcontractors to ensure awareness and promote a proactive safety culture. This aligns with the principles outlined in AGC Safety and Health Manual Ch. 5 Incident Reporting.
  7. OSHA 300 Log: MH Construction will maintain an OSHA 300 Log for recording all recordable work-related injuries and illnesses, as required by 29 CFR 1904. The OSHA 300A Summary of Work-Related Injuries and Illnesses will be posted annually from February 1 to April 30.
  8. Washington State Requirements: In addition to federal OSHA requirements, MH Construction will comply with all applicable WISHA regulations, including WAC 296-27 for recording and reporting occupational injuries and illnesses, and WAC 296-155-110(5) regarding accident prevention programs. Where WISHA standards are more stringent than OSHA, the WISHA standard will take precedence.

Training Requirements

All employees and supervisors will receive training on this Incident Reporting policy, including procedures for reporting, investigation, and documentation. Training will be conducted upon initial hire and annually thereafter, or as needed due to policy changes or identified deficiencies. Training records, including dates, attendees, and topics covered, will be maintained by the Safety Officer. Per 29 CFR 1926.20(b)(3), employers must instruct each employee in the recognition and avoidance of unsafe conditions and the regulations applicable to his work environment to control or eliminate any hazards or other exposure to illness or injury.

Inspection and Enforcement

Compliance with this policy will be monitored through regular site inspections, safety audits, and review of incident reports. The Safety Officer is responsible for overseeing compliance. Any non-compliance or deficiencies identified will result in immediate corrective action. Enforcement will follow MH Construction's progressive discipline policy.

Recordkeeping

The Safety Officer is responsible for maintaining all incident reports, investigation findings, corrective action plans, and OSHA 300 Logs. Records will be retained for a minimum of five (5) years from the date of the incident, as required by 29 CFR 1904.33. Training records will be maintained for the duration of employment plus three years.

Violation Consequences

Failure to comply with the provisions of this Incident Reporting policy may result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract, depending on the severity and frequency of the violation. MH Construction is committed to maintaining a safe workplace, and adherence to safety policies is a condition of employment and engagement.

References

  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1904 Recording and Reporting Occupational Injuries and Illnesses
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1904.39 Reporting Fatalities and Hospitalizations
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.20 (b)(3)
  • WISHA: WAC 296-27 Recording and Reporting Occupational Injuries and Illnesses
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-110 (5)
  • AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 5 Incident Reporting

sections.title: safety and health meetings inspections
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 09 | Safety and Health Meetings/Inspections Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 1.  Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 09 | Safety and Health Meetings/Inspections

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.20(b)(2) Frequent and regular inspections of jobsites

WISHA

WAC 296-155-110(4)

Accident Prevention Program Inspections and Meetings

AGC

SHIP Best Practices Handbook Ch. 3; AGC Toolbox Talks Program

2. Purpose

This policy establishes guidelines for regular safety and health meetings and inspections to proactively identify and mitigate workplace hazards. It aims to ensure a safe working environment for all personnel by promoting continuous hazard recognition and control, thereby preventing accidents and occupational illnesses.

3. Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors present on MH Construction job sites.

4. Definitions

Term

Definition

Jobsite Inspection

A systematic examination of a work area to identify potential hazards, unsafe conditions, and non-compliant practices.

Safety Meeting

A formal gathering of personnel to discuss safety performance, review incidents, communicate new policies, and provide safety training.

Hazard

A condition or practice that has the potential to cause injury, illness, or property damage.

Competent Person

One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Corrective Action

Measures taken to eliminate the causes of an existing nonconformity or other undesirable situation.

5. Responsibilities

5.1. Company/Management

  • Provide necessary resources (time, personnel, equipment) for effective safety meetings and jobsite inspections.
  • Establish and maintain a comprehensive Accident Prevention Program (APP) as required by WISHA WAC 296-155-110.
  • Ensure that all personnel involved in inspections are adequately trained and competent.

5.2. Supervisor/Foreman

  • Conduct frequent and regular inspections of jobsites to identify and correct hazards, as per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.20(b)(2).
  • Lead and document regular safety meetings, including toolbox talks, with their crews.
  • Ensure immediate corrective action is taken for identified hazards and communicate resolutions to affected employees.
  • Train employees on safe work practices and the proper use of equipment.

5.3. Employees

  • Actively participate in safety meetings and jobsite inspections.
  • Report all unsafe conditions, hazards, and incidents to their supervisor immediately.
  • Comply with all safety rules, procedures, and instructions.

5.4. Subcontractors

  • Adhere to MH Construction's safety policies and procedures while on MHC jobsites.
  • Conduct their own safety meetings and inspections, coordinating findings with MHC site supervision.
  • Ensure their employees are trained and follow safe work practices.

6. Procedures / Requirements

6.1. Frequency of Inspections

  1. Jobsite inspections shall be conducted frequently and regularly by a competent person, as required by OSHA 29 CFR 1926.20(b)(2). The frequency will be determined by the nature of the work, hazards present, and project phase, but no less than weekly for active construction sites.

6.2. Inspection Personnel

  1. Inspections shall be conducted by supervisors, foremen, or designated competent persons who have the authority to correct hazards immediately.
  2. The safety officer will conduct periodic comprehensive inspections.

6.3. Inspection Process

  1. Inspectors will use a standardized checklist to ensure all areas and potential hazards are covered. This includes, but is not limited to, fall protection, scaffolding, electrical safety, excavation safety, and personal protective equipment (PPE).
  2. Any identified hazards must be documented, including the location, description, responsible party for correction, and target completion date.
  3. Immediate corrective action must be taken for imminent danger situations. For other hazards, a reasonable timeline for correction will be established and followed up.

6.4. Safety Meetings (Toolbox Talks)

  1. Supervisors shall conduct regular safety meetings (e.g., daily huddles, weekly toolbox talks) with their crews to discuss current jobsite hazards, safe work procedures, and review recent incidents. These meetings fulfill part of the Accident Prevention Program requirements under WISHA WAC 296-155-110(4).
  2. Topics for safety meetings should be relevant to the ongoing work and potential hazards, utilizing resources such as the AGC Toolbox Talks Program.

6.5. Accident Prevention Program (APP) Meetings

  1. Formal APP meetings, involving management and employee representatives, will be held periodically (e.g., monthly or quarterly) to review overall safety performance, discuss inspection findings, accident trends, and update safety policies. This is a key component of WISHA WAC 296-155-110(4).

6.6. Documentation of Meetings and Inspections

  1. All safety meetings and jobsite inspections must be documented. Documentation will include attendees, topics discussed, hazards identified, corrective actions taken, and completion dates.

7. Training Requirements

All employees, supervisors, and competent persons involved in safety meetings and inspections must receive adequate training to perform their roles effectively. This includes training on hazard recognition, OSHA and WISHA standards, and MH Construction's specific safety procedures.

  • Supervisors and competent persons must be trained in hazard identification and correction, and understanding of applicable OSHA (e.g., 29 CFR 1926) and WISHA (e.g., WAC 296-155) regulations.
  • New employees will receive initial safety orientation covering the importance of safety meetings and hazard reporting.
  • Training records, including dates, topics, and attendees, will be maintained by the Safety Officer.

8. Inspection and Enforcement

Compliance with this policy will be monitored through ongoing jobsite inspections, safety audits, and review of meeting documentation. The Safety Officer and site supervisors are responsible for ensuring adherence to all safety protocols.

  • Identified non-compliance or hazards during inspections will result in immediate corrective action. For serious violations, work may be stopped until the hazard is abated.
  • Supervisors are empowered to enforce safety rules and implement disciplinary actions as outlined in the company's violation consequences policy.

9. Recordkeeping

Accurate and complete records of all safety meetings and jobsite inspections are crucial for demonstrating compliance and continuous improvement.

  • All inspection checklists, meeting minutes, attendance sheets, and documentation of corrective actions will be maintained.
  • Records will be retained for a minimum of five (5) years, or longer if required by specific regulatory standards or project contracts.
  • The Safety Officer is responsible for the organization, maintenance, and accessibility of all safety records.

10. Violation Consequences

Failure to comply with the requirements of this Safety and Health Meetings/Inspections policy may result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract, consistent with MH Construction's progressive disciplinary policy.

  • Minor infractions will typically result in a verbal warning and retraining.
  • Repeated violations or serious safety breaches may lead to written warnings, suspension, or termination.
  • Subcontractors found in non-compliance may face penalties, removal from the jobsite, or termination of their contract.

11. References

  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.20(b)(2) Frequent and regular inspections of jobsites
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-110(4) Accident Prevention Program Inspections and Meetings
  • AGC: SHIP Best Practices Handbook Ch. 3; AGC Toolbox Talks Program
