Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 10 | Personal Protective Equipment Revision N/A | Effective: N/A | Safety Officer: N/A REGULATORY AUTHORITY Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart E OSHA 29 CFR 1926. 95 PPE Criteria OSHA 29 CFR 1926. 96 Occupational Foot Protection OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.
Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)
MISH 10 | Personal Protective Equipment
Revision N/A | Effective: N/A | Safety Officer: N/A

REGULATORY AUTHORITY

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926 Subpart E

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.95

PPE Criteria

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.96

Occupational Foot Protection

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.100

Head Protection

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.102

Eye and Face Protection

WISHA

WAC 296-155-200

General PPE Requirements

WISHA

WAC 296-155-205

Head Protection

WISHA

WAC 296-155-210

Hearing Protection

WISHA

WAC 296-155-212

Foot Protection

WISHA

WAC 296-155-215

Eye and Face Protection

AGC

Safety

and Health Manual Ch. 6 PPE

PURPOSE

This policy establishes guidelines for the selection, use, and maintenance of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) to safeguard MH Construction employees, subcontractors, and visitors from workplace hazards. It aims to prevent injuries, illnesses, and fatalities by ensuring appropriate protection against identified risks.

SCOPE

This policy applies to all MH Construction employees, subcontractors, and visitors present on any MH Construction job site. Adherence to these requirements is mandatory to maintain a safe working environment.

DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

PPE

Personal Protective Equipment; specialized clothing or equipment worn by employees for protection against health and safety hazards.

Hazard Assessment

An evaluation process to identify potential workplace hazards and determine the appropriate PPE required.

Competent Person

One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Donning

The act of putting on PPE.

Doffing

The act of taking off PPE.

RESPONSIBILITIES

Company/Management Responsibilities

• Provide appropriate PPE at no cost to employees. (Per 29 CFR 1926.95(a))

• Ensure hazard assessments are conducted to identify necessary PPE. (Per 29 CFR 1926.95(a))

• Establish and maintain a comprehensive PPE program, including selection, maintenance, and training.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

• Enforce the proper use and maintenance of PPE on job sites.

• Conduct regular inspections to ensure PPE is in good condition and being used correctly.

• Provide initial and refresher training on PPE to employees under their supervision.

Employee Responsibilities

• Properly wear and maintain assigned PPE. (Per WAC 296-155-200(2))

• Inspect PPE before each use and report any defects to their supervisor.

• Attend all required PPE training sessions.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

• Ensure their employees comply with all MH Construction PPE policies and regulatory requirements.

• Provide their employees with appropriate PPE and training.

PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

7.1 Hazard Assessment and PPE Selection

1. A competent person shall conduct a hazard assessment for each job site and task to identify potential hazards requiring PPE. (Per 29 CFR 1926.95(a))

2. The assessment will consider impact, penetration, compression, chemical, heat, harmful dust, light radiation, and biological hazards.

3. Based on the assessment, appropriate PPE will be selected that meets or exceeds ANSI standards where applicable. (Per 29 CFR 1926.95(b))

7.2 Head Protection

1. Employees working in areas where there is a possible danger of head injury from impact, falling objects, or electrical shock shall wear protective helmets. (Per 29 CFR 1926.100(a))

2. Helmets shall meet the requirements of ANSI Z89.1-2009. (Per WAC 296-155-205(1))

7.3 Eye and Face Protection

1. Eye and face protection shall be provided where there is a reasonable probability of injury from physical, chemical, or radiation agents. (Per 29 CFR 1926.102(a)(1))

2. Protection shall meet the requirements of ANSI Z87.1-2010. (Per WAC 296-155-215(1))

7.4 Foot Protection

1. Employees shall wear protective footwear when working in areas where there is a danger of foot injuries from falling or rolling objects, or objects piercing the sole. (Per 29 CFR 1926.96(a))

2. Footwear shall meet the requirements of ANSI Z41-1991. (Per WAC 296-155-212(1))

7.5 Hand Protection

1. Appropriate hand protection shall be worn when employees are exposed to hazards such as those from skin absorption of harmful substances, severe cuts or lacerations, severe abrasions, punctures, chemical burns, thermal burns, and harmful temperature extremes.

2. Glove selection will be based on the hazards identified during the job hazard analysis.

7.6 Hearing Protection

1. Hearing protection shall be provided and used when noise levels exceed permissible exposure limits. (Per WAC 296-155-210(1))

2. Hearing protectors must be selected based on the noise environment and individual fit.

TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

All employees required to use PPE shall be trained to know: when PPE is necessary; what PPE is necessary; how to properly don, doff, adjust, and wear PPE; the limitations of the PPE; and the proper care, maintenance, useful life, and disposal of the PPE. (Per 29 CFR 1926.95(f)) Training shall be provided prior to initial assignment and when changes in workplace or PPE necessitate new training. Retraining will be conducted as needed. All training will be documented.

INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

Supervisors will conduct regular, documented inspections of job sites and employee PPE usage to ensure compliance. Deficiencies will be addressed immediately. Non-compliance with PPE requirements will result in corrective action as outlined in the Violation Consequences section.

RECORDKEEPING

Records of hazard assessments, PPE training, and PPE issuance will be maintained by the Safety Officer for a minimum of three years. These records are essential for demonstrating compliance and continuous improvement.

VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

Failure to comply with this PPE policy may result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment. Subcontractors found in non-compliance may face removal from the job site and potential termination of contract. The progressive discipline policy is as follows: 1st Offense - Verbal Warning; 2nd Offense - Written Warning; 3rd Offense - Suspension; 4th Offense - Termination.

REFERENCES

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1926 Subpart E

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.95 PPE Criteria

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.96 Occupational Foot Protection

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.100 Head Protection

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.102 Eye and Face Protection

• WISHA: WAC 296-155-200 General PPE Requirements

• WISHA: WAC 296-155-205 Head Protection

• WISHA: WAC 296-155-210 Hearing Protection

• WISHA: WAC 296-155-212 Foot Protection

• WISHA: WAC 296-155-215 Eye and Face Protection

• AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 6 PPE

sections.title: fall protection
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 11 — Fall Protection Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey ________________________________________________________________________________ 1.  Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title / Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart M Fall Protection — Construction OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 11 — Fall Protection

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

________________________________________________________________________________

1. Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title / Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926 Subpart M

Fall Protection — Construction

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.500

Scope, Application, and Definitions

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.501

Duty to Have Fall Protection

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.502

Fall Protection Systems Criteria and Practices

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.503

Training Requirements

WISHA

WAC 296-880

Unified Safety Standards for Fall Protection (replaces WAC 296-155 Part C-1)

WISHA

WAC 296-880-010 through 296-880-100

Fall Protection Requirements — Construction

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 7

Fall Protection

2. Purpose

The purpose of this section is to establish requirements for the prevention of fall-related injuries and fatalities on all MH Construction, Inc. job sites. Falls are the leading cause of death in the construction industry, accounting for approximately one-third of all construction fatalities annually (OSHA). This program ensures compliance with 29 CFR 1926 Subpart M and WAC 296-880 and provides a systematic approach to identifying, evaluating, and controlling fall hazards.

3. Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction employees, subcontractors, vendors, and visitors on any MHC-controlled job site where fall hazards of 6 feet or greater exist in construction operations, or 4 feet in general industry operations. Per 29 CFR 1926.501(b)(1), each employee on a walking/working surface with an unprotected side or edge which is 6 feet or more above a lower level must be protected from falling by the use of guardrail systems, safety net systems, or personal fall arrest systems.

4. Definitions

Term

Definition

Anchorage

A secure point of attachment for lifelines, lanyards, or deceleration devices capable of supporting at least 5,000 lbs per attached employee (29 CFR 1926.502(d)(15)).

Body Harness

Straps which may be secured about the employee in a manner that will distribute the fall arrest forces over at least the thighs, pelvis, waist, chest, and shoulders (29 CFR 1926.500(b)).

Competent Person

One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Guardrail System

A barrier erected to prevent employees from falling to lower levels. Top rail at 42 inches (+/- 3 inches), mid-rail at 21 inches, capable of withstanding 200 lbs of force (29 CFR 1926.502(b)).

Leading Edge

The edge of a floor, roof, or formwork for a floor or other walking/working surface which changes location as additional floor, roof, decking, or formwork sections are placed.

Personal Fall Arrest System (PFAS)

A system used to arrest an employee in a fall from a working level, consisting of an anchorage, connectors, body harness, and may include a lanyard, deceleration device, lifeline, or combinations of these.

Safety Net System

A system used to arrest falling employees or materials, installed as close as practicable under the walking/working surface on which employees are working, but in no case more than 30 feet below (29 CFR 1926.502(c)).

5. Responsibilities

5.1 Company/Management:

  • Provide and maintain all fall protection equipment in safe working condition.
  • Ensure a written Fall Protection Work Plan is developed for all work at heights of 6 feet or more.
  • Ensure all supervisors and employees receive fall protection training per 29 CFR 1926.503.
  • Conduct periodic audits of fall protection compliance on all job sites.

5.2 Supervisors/Foremen:

  • Identify all fall hazards before work begins and implement appropriate controls.
  • Ensure all employees under their supervision use required fall protection at all times.
  • Inspect fall protection equipment before each use and remove defective equipment from service.
  • Enforce the Fall Protection Work Plan and document any deviations.
  • Conduct pre-task safety briefings that include fall hazard identification per WAC 296-880.

5.3 Employees:

  • Use fall protection equipment as instructed and trained.
  • Inspect personal fall arrest equipment before each use.
  • Report any defective equipment or unguarded fall hazards to their supervisor immediately.
  • Never remove, alter, or bypass fall protection systems.
  • Attend all required fall protection training.

6. Procedures and Requirements

6.1 Fall Hazard Assessment

Prior to beginning any work where fall hazards exist, the competent person must conduct a fall hazard assessment. Per WAC 296-880-020, the assessment must identify all fall hazards, determine the appropriate fall protection method, and document the findings in the site-specific Fall Protection Work Plan. The assessment must be updated whenever site conditions change.

6.2 Trigger Heights

Per 29 CFR 1926.501 and WAC 296-880, fall protection is required at the following heights:

Work Activity

Trigger Height / Requirement

General construction

6 feet or more above a lower level

Excavations

6 feet or more in depth

Formwork and reinforcing steel

6 feet or more

Ramps, runways, and walkways

6 feet or more

Overhand bricklaying

6 feet or more

Roofing work on low-slope roofs

6 feet or more — guardrails, safety nets, or PFAS required

Steep roof work

6 feet or more — PFAS, safety nets, or warning line + safety monitor

Leading edges

6 feet or more — PFAS, safety nets, or guardrails

6.3 Guardrail Systems

Per 29 CFR 1926.502(b) and WAC 296-880, guardrail systems must meet the following criteria: Top rail height of 42 inches (+/- 3 inches) above the walking/working level; midrail at 21 inches; capable of withstanding a force of at least 200 lbs applied in any outward or downward direction; top rails and midrails must be smooth-surfaced to prevent injury and snagging of clothing.

6.4 Personal Fall Arrest Systems (PFAS)

Per 29 CFR 1926.502(d) and WAC 296-880, PFAS must: (1) Limit maximum arresting force to 1,800 lbs; (2) Be rigged so the employee cannot free fall more than 6 feet or contact a lower level; (3) Bring the employee to a complete stop and limit maximum deceleration distance to 3.5 feet; (4) Have sufficient strength to withstand twice the potential impact energy of an employee free falling 6 feet or the free fall distance permitted by the system, whichever is less. Full-body harnesses are required — body belts are prohibited as the sole means of fall arrest.

6.5 Safety Net Systems

Per 29 CFR 1926.502(c), safety nets must be installed as close as practicable under the walking/working surface, but no more than 30 feet below. Nets must extend outward from the outermost projection of the work surface as follows: Up to 5 feet below — extend 8 feet; 5-10 feet below — extend 10 feet; more than 10 feet below — extend 13 feet. Safety nets must be drop-tested after installation and at 6-month intervals.

6.6 Covers for Floor and Roof Openings

Per 29 CFR 1926.502(i) and WAC 296-880, covers for floor holes and openings must: (1) Be capable of supporting at least twice the weight of employees, equipment, and materials that may be imposed on the cover; (2) Be secured to prevent accidental displacement; (3) Be color-coded or marked with the word "HOLE" or "COVER" to provide warning of the hazard.

6.7 Positioning Device Systems

Per 29 CFR 1926.502(e), positioning device systems must be rigged such that an employee cannot free fall more than 2 feet. Connectors must be drop-forged, pressed, or formed steel or equivalent materials. The system must be inspected prior to each use for wear, damage, and other deterioration.

6.8 Warning Line Systems

Per 29 CFR 1926.502(f), warning line systems used on low-slope roofs must consist of ropes, wires, or chains with a minimum tensile strength of 500 lbs, erected on stanchions no more than 6 feet apart. The warning line must be flagged at not more than 6-foot intervals with high-visibility material and erected at a height of not less than 34 inches nor more than 39 inches above the walking/working surface.

6.9 Controlled Access Zones (CAZ)

Per 29 CFR 1926.502(g), CAZs may be used for leading edge work, precast concrete erection, and residential construction as an alternative to PFAS when PFAS is infeasible. The CAZ must be defined by a control line of rope, wire, or tape with a minimum breaking strength of 200 lbs, erected at a height between 39 and 45 inches, and located between 6 and 25 feet from the unprotected edge.

6.10 Fall Protection Plan (Alternative to Conventional Systems)

Per 29 CFR 1926.502(k), a written fall protection plan may be used in lieu of conventional fall protection systems only when the employer can demonstrate that it is infeasible or creates a greater hazard. The plan must be prepared by a qualified person, be site-specific, and include: identification of each location where conventional fall protection cannot be used; description of the measures to be taken; and the name of the competent person responsible for implementation.

7. Training Requirements

Per 29 CFR 1926.503 and WAC 296-880, all employees exposed to fall hazards must be trained by a competent person before initial assignment and when changes in the workplace render previous training obsolete. Training must cover:

  • The nature of fall hazards in the work area
  • The correct procedures for erecting, maintaining, disassembling, and inspecting fall protection systems
  • The use and operation of guardrail systems, personal fall arrest systems, safety net systems, warning line systems, and controlled access zones
  • The role of each employee in the safety monitoring system when used
  • The limitations on the use of mechanical equipment during the performance of roofing work on low-slope roofs
  • The correct procedures for equipment and materials handling and storage
  • The role of employees in fall protection plans

Retraining is required when the employer has reason to believe that an employee who has already been trained does not have the understanding and skill required. Training must be documented with employee name, date, and trainer signature.

8. Inspection and Enforcement

The competent person must inspect all fall protection equipment before each use. Supervisors must conduct daily walk-through inspections of all fall protection systems in use. MHC Safety Officer will conduct periodic unannounced audits. Any fall protection system found to be defective must be immediately removed from service and tagged "DO NOT USE." Non-compliance will result in immediate work stoppage until the hazard is corrected.

9. Recordkeeping

The following records must be maintained for a minimum of 3 years: Fall Protection Work Plans (site-specific); Fall protection training records (employee name, date, trainer, topics covered); Equipment inspection logs; Incident reports involving falls or near-misses. Records are maintained by the MHC Safety Officer and stored in Procore.

10. Violation Consequences

Violations of this fall protection policy will result in progressive disciplinary action: First violation — verbal warning and immediate correction; Second violation — written warning; Third violation — suspension without pay; Fourth violation — termination. Willful disregard of fall protection requirements resulting in imminent danger will result in immediate removal from the job site and may result in termination.

11. References

  • 29 CFR 1926 Subpart M — Fall Protection
  • 29 CFR 1926.500 — Scope, Application, and Definitions
  • 29 CFR 1926.501 — Duty to Have Fall Protection
  • 29 CFR 1926.502 — Fall Protection Systems Criteria and Practices
  • 29 CFR 1926.503 — Training Requirements
  • WAC 296-880 — Unified Safety Standards for Fall Protection
  • AGC Safety and Health Manual, Chapter 7 — Fall Protection
  • ANSI/ASSE Z359 — Fall Protection Code

sections.title: flammable and combustible liquids
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 12 | Flammable and Combustible Liquids Revision 3 | **Effective:** 04/07/2026 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey | **Revision:** 3 | **Effective:** 04/07/2026 1.  REGULATORY AUTHORITY Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart F OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 12 | Flammable and Combustible Liquids

Revision 3 | **Effective:** 04/07/2026 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey | **Revision:** 3 | **Effective:** 04/07/2026

1. REGULATORY AUTHORITY

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1926 Subpart F

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.152 Flammable Liquids

WISHA

WAC 296-155-270

Flammable Liquids

WISHA

WAC 296-155-280

Temporary Heating Devices

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch.

8 Fire Safety

2. PURPOSE

This policy establishes guidelines for the safe handling, storage, and use of flammable and combustible liquids to prevent fires, explosions, and exposure-related injuries. It aims to protect all personnel and property on MH Construction job sites from hazards associated with these materials.

3. SCOPE

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors on MH Construction job sites where flammable and combustible liquids are present or used.

4. DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Flammable Liquid

Any liquid having a flashpoint below 100°F (37.8°C), except any mixture having components with flashpoints of 100°F (37.8°C) or higher, the total of which make up 99 percent or more of the total volume of the mixture.

Combustible Liquid

Any liquid having a flashpoint at or above 100°F (37.8°C).

Flashpoint

The minimum temperature at which a liquid gives off vapor in sufficient concentration to form an ignitable mixture with air near the surface of the liquid.

Approved Container

A container designed, constructed, and maintained to safely store and dispense flammable or combustible liquids, meeting OSHA and WISHA standards.

Ignition Source

Any item or condition that can produce a spark, flame, or heat sufficient to ignite flammable or combustible vapors, including but not limited to open flames, sparks, hot surfaces, and static electricity.

5. RESPONSIBILITIES

5.1 Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Provide necessary resources and equipment for safe handling and storage of flammable and combustible liquids.
  • Ensure compliance with all applicable OSHA and WISHA regulations regarding flammable and combustible liquids.
  • Develop, implement, and maintain this MISH program section.

5.2 Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Ensure all employees under their supervision are trained on the hazards and safe handling procedures for flammable and combustible liquids.
  • Monitor work areas to ensure proper storage, use, and disposal of these materials, and enforce compliance with this policy.
  • Conduct regular inspections of storage areas and equipment.

5.3 Employee Responsibilities

  • Adhere to all safety procedures and instructions regarding flammable and combustible liquids.
  • Use appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) as required.
  • Report any spills, leaks, or unsafe conditions immediately to their supervisor.

5.4 Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Comply with all MH Construction policies and procedures, as well as all applicable OSHA and WISHA regulations, concerning flammable and combustible liquids.
  • Ensure their employees are properly trained and equipped to handle these materials safely.

6. PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

6.1 Storage of Flammable and Combustible Liquids

All flammable and combustible liquids must be stored in approved containers and cabinets. Per 29 CFR 1926.152(a)(1) and WAC 296-155-270(1)(a), not more than 25 gallons of flammable liquids shall be stored in a room outside of an approved storage cabinet. For quantities greater than 25 gallons, an approved storage cabinet must be used. Storage areas must be clearly marked and free from ignition sources.

6.2 Handling and Use

When handling flammable and combustible liquids, employees must use appropriate PPE, including gloves and eye protection. Per 29 CFR 1926.152(d)(1) and WAC 296-155-270(4)(a), liquids shall be drawn from or transferred into containers within a building only through a closed piping system, from safety cans, by means of a device drawing through the top, or from a container or portable tank by gravity through an approved self-closing valve. Open flames, smoking, and other ignition sources are strictly prohibited in areas where these liquids are being used or transferred.

6.3 Dispensing Operations

Dispensing of flammable liquids must be conducted in well-ventilated areas. Per 29 CFR 1926.152(e)(1) and WAC 296-155-270(5)(a), when transferring flammable liquids from one container to another, both containers must be electrically bonded and grounded to prevent static electricity buildup. This is crucial for preventing sparks that could ignite vapors.

6.4 Fire Prevention and Control

Adequate fire extinguishers must be readily available in areas where flammable and combustible liquids are stored or used. Employees must be trained in the proper use of fire extinguishers. Per 29 CFR 1926.152(f)(1) and WAC 296-155-270(6)(a), at least one portable fire extinguisher, having a rating of not less than 20-B units, shall be located outside of, but not more than 10 feet from, the door opening into any room used for storage of more than 60 gallons of flammable liquids.

6.5 Spill Response

In the event of a spill, employees must follow established spill response procedures. Spill kits containing absorbent materials must be accessible. Per WAC 296-155-270(10), spills of flammable or combustible liquids shall be cleaned up promptly and properly disposed of. Large spills requires immediate evacuation and notification of emergency services.

6.6 Waste Disposal

Waste flammable and combustible liquids must be disposed of in accordance with all federal, state, and local regulations. They must be stored in approved, labeled containers awaiting proper disposal. Mixing of different types of waste liquids is prohibited unless specifically approved.

6.7 Temporary Heating Devices

When temporary heating devices are used, especially those fueled by flammable or combustible liquids, they must comply with WAC 296-155-280. This includes proper ventilation, clearance from combustible materials, and ensuring devices are in good working order. Fueling operations for these devices must follow all guidelines for handling flammable liquids.

7. TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

All employees who handle or are exposed to flammable and combustible liquids must receive initial and annual refresher training. Training will cover the hazards of these materials, safe handling procedures, emergency response, and proper use of PPE. Documentation of training, including dates and attendees, will be maintained by the Safety Officer. Per 29 CFR 1926.21(b)(2) and WAC 296-155-100, employees must be instructed in the recognition and avoidance of unsafe conditions and the regulations applicable to their work environment to control or eliminate any hazards or other exposure to illness or injury.

8. INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

Supervisors will conduct daily visual inspections of areas where flammable and combustible liquids are stored or used. Formal inspections will be conducted monthly by the Safety Officer. Any non-compliance or unsafe conditions will result in immediate corrective action. Repeated violations may lead to disciplinary action as outlined in the company's progressive discipline policy.

9. RECORDKEEPING

Records of all training sessions, safety data sheets (SDS) for all flammable and combustible liquids, inspection reports, and incident reports related to these materials will be maintained by the Safety Officer for a minimum of five years. SDS will be readily accessible to all employees.

10. VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

Violations of this policy will be subject to MH Construction's progressive disciplinary action policy, up to and including termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and frequency of the violation, and the potential for harm.

11. REFERENCES

  • 29 CFR 1926 Subpart F - Fire Protection and Prevention
  • 29 CFR 1926.152 - Flammable Liquids
  • WAC 296-155-270 - Flammable Liquids
  • WAC 296-155-280 - Temporary Heating Devices
  • AGC Safety and Health Manual Ch. 8 - Fire Safety

sections.title: fire prevention
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 13 | Fire Prevention Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey 1.  Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart F OSHA 29 CFR 1926. 150 Fire Protection OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 13 | Fire Prevention

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey





1. Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926 Subpart F

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.150

Fire Protection

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.151

Fire Prevention

WISHA

WAC 296-155-260

Fire Protection

WISHA

WAC 296-155-265

Fire Prevention

WISHA

WAC 296-155-250

Definitions

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 8 Fire Prevention

2. Purpose

This policy establishes comprehensive guidelines for fire prevention and protection on all MH Construction, Inc. job sites. Its purpose is to safeguard personnel, property, and the environment from the hazards of fire, ensuring compliance with all applicable federal and state regulations.

By implementing these measures, we aim to minimize fire risks, provide effective response protocols, and promote a culture of safety among all site personnel.

3. Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, vendors, and visitors present on any MH Construction, Inc. job site. Adherence to these guidelines is mandatory for all individuals involved in project operations.

4. Definitions

Term

Definition

Combustible Material

Any material that can ignite and burn, creating a fire hazard. This includes wood, paper, fabrics, and certain liquids.

Flammable Liquid

Any liquid having a flashpoint below 100°F (37.8°C), such as gasoline, propane, and certain solvents. These liquids pose a significant fire and explosion risk.

Fire Watch

A designated person or persons responsible for monitoring hot work operations (e.g., welding, cutting) and immediately extinguishing any fires that may occur.

Hot Work

Any operation producing flames, sparks, or heat, including welding, cutting, grinding, and torch operations, which can ignite combustible materials.

Ignition Source

Anything that can cause a fire, including open flames, sparks, hot surfaces, electrical equipment, and smoking materials.

5. Responsibilities

5.1 Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Provide a safe work environment free from recognized fire hazards.
  • Ensure adequate fire protection equipment is available and maintained.
  • Establish and enforce fire prevention policies and procedures.
  • Provide necessary training and resources for fire safety.

5.2 Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Implement and enforce fire prevention plans on site.
  • Conduct regular inspections to identify and mitigate fire hazards.
  • Ensure all personnel are aware of fire safety procedures and emergency exits.
  • Oversee hot work operations and ensure proper permits and fire watch are in place.

5.3 Employee Responsibilities

  • Adhere to all fire prevention policies and procedures.
  • Report any fire hazards or unsafe conditions immediately to a supervisor.
  • Know the location of fire extinguishers and emergency exits.
  • Participate in all required fire safety training.

5.4 Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Comply with all MH Construction, Inc. fire prevention policies and procedures.
  • Ensure their employees receive appropriate fire safety training.
  • Provide and maintain their own fire protection equipment as required by their scope of work and this policy.

6. Procedures / Requirements

6.1 General Housekeeping and Storage

  1. Maintain clean and orderly work areas, free from accumulations of combustible waste materials. Per 29 CFR 1926.25(c).
  2. Store combustible materials in designated areas, away from ignition sources and at least 10 feet from buildings or structures. Per 29 CFR 1926.150(c)(1)(vi).
  3. Ensure proper ventilation in areas where flammable or combustible materials are stored or used. Per WAC 296-155-265(1).

6.2 Flammable and Combustible Liquids

  1. Store flammable liquids in approved, labeled containers and cabinets. Per 29 CFR 1926.152(a)(1).
  2. Dispense flammable liquids only in well-ventilated areas, away from ignition sources. Per 29 CFR 1926.152(e)(1).
  3. Limit the quantity of flammable liquids stored in work areas to the amount needed for one day's operation. Per WAC 296-155-265(2).

6.3 Hot Work Operations

  1. Obtain a hot work permit from the site supervisor before commencing any hot work. Per 29 CFR 1926.352(b).
  2. Ensure a fire watch is present during and for at least 30 minutes after hot work operations, equipped with appropriate fire extinguishing equipment. Per 29 CFR 1926.352(e).
  3. Clear the hot work area of all combustible materials within a 35-foot radius, or protect them with fire-resistant coverings. Per WAC 296-155-265(4).

6.4 Fire Extinguishers

  1. Provide appropriate type and number of fire extinguishers based on the hazards present. Per 29 CFR 1926.150(c)(1)(i).
  2. Ensure fire extinguishers are readily accessible, inspected monthly, and maintained annually. Per 29 CFR 1926.150(c)(1)(viii).
  3. Train employees on the proper use of fire extinguishers. Per WAC 296-155-260(4).

6.5 Electrical Safety

  1. Ensure all temporary electrical wiring and equipment are installed and maintained in accordance with NFPA 70E and 29 CFR 1926 Subpart K. Per 29 CFR 1926.403.
  2. Avoid overloading electrical circuits and use ground-fault circuit interrupters (GFCIs) for all temporary power. Per WAC 296-155-265(5).
  3. Report any damaged electrical cords, tools, or equipment immediately for repair or replacement.

6.6 Emergency Egress and Assembly Points

  1. Maintain clear and unobstructed access to all emergency exits and escape routes. Per 29 CFR 1926.34.
  2. Establish and clearly mark designated assembly points for all personnel in case of a fire or emergency.
  3. Conduct regular drills to ensure all personnel are familiar with emergency evacuation procedures.

7. Training Requirements

All MH Construction, Inc. employees and relevant subcontractors must receive initial and periodic training on fire prevention and protection. Training will cover:

  • Recognition of fire hazards.
  • Proper storage and handling of flammable and combustible materials.
  • Use of fire extinguishing equipment (e.g., fire extinguishers). Per 29 CFR 1926.150(c)(1)(viii) and WAC 296-155-260(4).
  • Emergency evacuation procedures and assembly points.
  • Hot work permit procedures and fire watch responsibilities.

Training frequency will be determined by job function and regulatory requirements, but at a minimum, annually. Documentation of all training sessions, including attendees, topics covered, and dates, will be maintained by the Safety Officer.

8. Inspection and Enforcement

Site supervisors will conduct daily informal inspections and weekly formal inspections to ensure compliance with this fire prevention policy. Any identified deficiencies will be documented, and corrective actions will be implemented promptly. Non-compliance will be addressed through the company's disciplinary action policy.

9. Recordkeeping

The following records will be maintained by the Safety Officer for a minimum of three (3) years:

  • Fire prevention plans.
  • Hot work permits.
  • Fire extinguisher inspection and maintenance logs.
  • Fire safety training records.
  • Incident reports related to fire or near-misses.

10. Violation Consequences

Violations of this Fire Prevention policy will result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract, depending on the severity and frequency of the violation. MH Construction, Inc. is committed to maintaining a safe work environment, and non-compliance with safety regulations will not be tolerated. Progressive disciplinary actions will be applied as follows:

  • First Offense: Verbal warning and retraining.
  • Second Offense: Written warning and additional training.
  • Third Offense: Suspension without pay or contract termination.
  • Severe Violations: Immediate termination or contract termination, especially if gross negligence or willful disregard for safety is demonstrated.

11. References

  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926 Subpart F
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.150 Fire Protection
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.151 Fire Prevention
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-260 Fire Protection
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-265 Fire Prevention
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-250 Definitions
  • AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 8 Fire Prevention

sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.
Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)
MISH 14 | Welding, Cutting, and Heating Operations
Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey


Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29

CFR 1926 Subpart J

OSHA

29

CFR 1926.350 Gas Welding and Cutting

OSHA

29

CFR 1926.351 Arc Welding and Cutting

OSHA

29

CFR 1926.352 Fire Prevention

OSHA

29

CFR 1926.353 Ventilation and Protection

OSHA

29

CFR 1926.354 Welding on Preservative Coatings

WISHA

WAC

296-155-400 Gas Welding and Cutting

WISHA

WAC

296-155-405 Arc Welding and Cutting

WISHA

WAC

296-155-407 Protective Clothing

WISHA

WAC

296-155-410 Fire Prevention

WISHA

WAC

296-155-415 Ventilation and Protection

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 9 Welding and Cutting

Purpose

This policy establishes guidelines for safe welding, cutting, and heating operations to prevent injuries, fires, and exposure to hazardous fumes. It aims to protect all personnel from the inherent risks associated with these activities on MH Construction job sites.

Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors engaged in or exposed to welding, cutting, and heating operations on any MH Construction job site.

Definitions

Term

Definition

Hot Work

Any operation involving open flames, producing heat and/or sparks, including welding, cutting, grinding, and brazing.

Fire Watch

A designated person responsible for observing hot work operations and surrounding areas for fires, equipped with appropriate fire extinguishing equipment.

Confined Space

A space that is large enough for an employee to enter and perform assigned work, has limited or restricted means for entry or exit, and is not designed for continuous employee occupancy.

PPE

Personal Protective Equipment, including but not limited to welding helmets, gloves, flame-resistant clothing, and safety glasses.

Flashback Arrestor

A device designed to prevent a flame from traveling back into the gas supply hose and regulator.

Responsibilities

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Provide necessary resources, equipment, and training to ensure safe welding, cutting, and heating operations.
  • Establish and enforce this MISH program and related safety policies.
  • Conduct periodic reviews of hot work procedures and equipment.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Ensure all hot work operations are conducted in accordance with this policy and applicable regulations (e.g., 29 CFR 1926.352).
  • Issue hot work permits when required and ensure fire watch personnel are properly trained and equipped.
  • Verify that all personnel involved in hot work have received appropriate training and use required PPE.

Employee Responsibilities

  • Adhere to all hot work procedures, safety rules, and wear appropriate PPE (e.g., WAC 296-155-407).
  • Report any unsafe conditions, equipment malfunctions, or incidents immediately to their supervisor.
  • Participate in all required training and understand the hazards associated with welding, cutting, and heating.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Comply with all MH Construction hot work policies and procedures, as well as all applicable OSHA and WISHA regulations.
  • Provide their employees with necessary training, PPE, and safe equipment for hot work operations.
  • Coordinate all hot work activities with the MH Construction site supervisor.

Procedures / Requirements

7.1 General Safety Precautions

  1. Before commencing any hot work, a thorough hazard assessment must be conducted to identify potential fire hazards, combustible materials, and atmospheric contaminants. All combustible materials within 35 feet must be removed or protected with fire-resistant covers (Per 29 CFR 1926.352(a)).
  2. Ensure adequate ventilation is present to prevent the accumulation of toxic fumes or gases. Mechanical ventilation must be used in confined spaces or areas with limited natural airflow (Per 29 CFR 1926.353(a) and WAC 296-155-415).
  3. Appropriate Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) must be worn at all times, including welding helmets with proper shade lenses, flame-resistant clothing, gauntlet gloves, and safety glasses (Per WAC 296-155-407).

7.2 Fire Prevention and Protection

  1. A trained fire watch must be present during and for at least 30 minutes after hot work operations, equipped with suitable fire extinguishing equipment (Per 29 CFR 1926.352(e) and WAC 296-155-410).
  2. Fire extinguishers (minimum 20-B rating) or charged hose lines must be readily available in the immediate vicinity of hot work (Per 29 CFR 1926.352(d)).
  3. Hot work permits are required for all operations outside designated hot work areas. Permits must be approved by the site supervisor and clearly outline precautions.

7.3 Gas Welding and Cutting

  1. Oxygen and fuel gas cylinders must be stored, handled, and used in an upright position and secured to prevent tipping. Valve protection caps must be in place when cylinders are not in use or connected (Per 29 CFR 1926.350(a) and WAC 296-155-400).
  2. Flashback arrestors must be installed on both the oxygen and fuel gas regulators to prevent reverse flow and flashbacks (Per WAC 296-155-400(10)).
  3. Hoses and connections must be inspected daily for leaks, cuts, or damage. Defective equipment must be immediately removed from service.

7.4 Arc Welding and Cutting

  1. Arc welding machines must be properly grounded. The work lead must be attached to the workpiece or to the work table (Per 29 CFR 1926.351(b) and WAC 296-155-405).
  2. Electrode holders, cables, and other equipment must be in good repair. Frayed or damaged cables must not be used.
  3. When not in use, electrode holders must be placed where they cannot make electrical contact with persons, conducting objects, or flammable materials.

7.5 Welding on Preservative Coatings

  1. Welding, cutting, or heating operations on surfaces covered with preservative coatings (e.g., paints, galvanizing) must only be performed after ensuring adequate ventilation and respiratory protection are in place to prevent exposure to toxic fumes (Per 29 CFR 1926.354).
  2. The coating must be removed from the area to be welded or heated, or specific precautions taken to protect workers from hazardous fumes.

7.6 Confined Spaces

  1. Hot work in confined spaces requires a specific confined space entry permit and continuous atmospheric monitoring. Ventilation must be provided to maintain safe oxygen levels and prevent accumulation of hazardous fumes (Per WAC 296-155-415(4)).
  2. All cylinders and welding machines must remain outside the confined space.

Training Requirements

All employees involved in welding, cutting, and heating operations, as well as those acting as fire watch, must receive comprehensive training on the hazards, safe operating procedures, emergency response, and proper use of PPE. Training must be documented and refreshed periodically, as required by OSHA and WISHA standards. Specific training on gas welding and cutting (29 CFR 1926.350, WAC 296-155-400) and arc welding and cutting (29 CFR 1926.351, WAC 296-155-405) is mandatory.

Inspection and Enforcement

Supervisors will conduct regular inspections of hot work operations, equipment, and work areas to ensure compliance with this policy and regulatory requirements. Any non-compliance will result in immediate corrective action, which may include stopping work, retraining, or disciplinary measures. Equipment found to be unsafe will be tagged out and removed from service until repaired or replaced.

Recordkeeping

The following records must be maintained by the Safety Officer or designated personnel:

  • Hot work permits (retained for the duration of the project).
  • Employee training records (retained for the duration of employment).
  • Equipment inspection and maintenance logs (retained for 3 years).
  • Incident and accident reports related to hot work (retained indefinitely).

Violation Consequences

Failure to comply with this policy and associated safety procedures will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. Violations may also lead to immediate removal from the job site and reporting to relevant regulatory authorities.

References

  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926 Subpart J
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.350 Gas Welding and Cutting
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.351 Arc Welding and Cutting
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.352 Fire Prevention
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.353 Ventilation and Protection
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.354 Welding on Preservative Coatings

  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-405 Arc Welding and Cutting
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-407 Protective Clothing
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-410 Fire Prevention
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-415 Ventilation and Protection
  • AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 9 Welding and Cutting

sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 15 | Lock and Tag Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey —————————————————————————————————————————————————— Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1910. 147 Control of Hazardous Energy (Lockout/Tagout) OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 15 | Lock and Tag

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

——————————————————————————————————————————————————

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1910.147

Control of Hazardous Energy (Lockout/Tagout)

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.417

Lockout and Tagging of Circuits

WISHA

WAC 296-803

Lockout/Tagout

WISHA

WAC 296-155-429

Lockout and Tagging of Circuits

AGC

Ch. 10

LOTO (Safety and Health Manual)

Purpose

This policy establishes minimum performance requirements for the control of hazardous energy during servicing and maintenance of machines and equipment. It aims to prevent unexpected energization, startup, or release of stored energy that could cause injury to employees. Adherence to these procedures protects personnel from serious harm or fatality.

Scope

This program applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors who may be exposed to hazardous energy sources during the servicing and maintenance of machines, equipment, or systems on any MH Construction job site or facility.

Definitions

Term

Definition

Affected Employee

An employee whose job requires him/her to operate or use a machine or equipment on which servicing or maintenance is being performed under lockout or tagout, or whose job requires him/her to work in an area in which such servicing or maintenance is being performed.

Authorized Employee

A person who locks out or tags out machines or equipment in order to perform servicing or maintenance on that machine or equipment.

Energized

Connected to an energy source or containing residual or stored energy.

Energy Isolating Device

A mechanical device that physically prevents the transmission or release of energy, including but not limited to manually operated electrical circuit breakers, disconnect switches, line valves, and blocks.

Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)

The placement of a lockout device or tagout device on an energy isolating device, in accordance with an established procedure, to ensure that the energy isolating device and the equipment being controlled cannot be operated until the lockout or tagout device is removed.

Responsibilities

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Develop, implement, and maintain the Lockout/Tagout program.
  • Provide necessary lockout/tagout devices and equipment.
  • Ensure adequate training is provided to all affected and authorized employees.
  • Conduct periodic inspections of the energy control procedures.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Ensure employees understand and follow LOTO procedures.
  • Identify all energy sources for equipment under their supervision.
  • Verify that all energy sources are properly isolated and de-energized before work begins.
  • Provide specific training and guidance to employees under their direction.

Employee Responsibilities

  • Comply with all LOTO procedures and instructions.
  • Never remove another employee's lock or tag.
  • Report any unsafe conditions or deviations from LOTO procedures.
  • Participate in all required LOTO training.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Adhere to MH Construction's LOTO program or provide their own equivalent program for review and approval.
  • Ensure their employees are trained and follow LOTO procedures.
  • Coordinate all LOTO activities with MH Construction's site supervisor.

Procedures / Requirements

7.1 General Requirements

MH Construction, Inc. shall establish a program consisting of energy control procedures, employee training, and periodic inspections to ensure that before any employee performs any servicing or maintenance on a machine or equipment where the unexpected energization or startup of the machine or equipment, or release of stored energy could cause injury, the machine or equipment shall be isolated from the energy source and rendered inoperative. Per 29 CFR 1910.147(c)(1) and WAC 296-803-200.

7.2 Lockout/Tagout Devices

  1. Lockout devices and tagout devices shall be singularly identified, be the only devices used for controlling energy, not be used for other purposes, and meet the following requirements:
  • Durable: Capable of withstanding the environment to which they are exposed for the maximum period of time that exposure is expected.
  • Standardized: Lockout and tagout devices shall be standardized within the facility in at least one of the following criteria: color, shape, or size; and in the case of tagout devices, print and format.
  • Substantial: Lockout devices shall be substantial enough to prevent removal without the use of excessive force or unusual techniques. Tagout devices shall be substantial enough to prevent inadvertent or accidental removal.
  • Identifiable: Lockout devices and tagout devices shall indicate the identity of the employee applying the device.

7.3 Energy Control Procedure Development

Specific energy control procedures shall be developed, documented, and utilized for each machine or equipment. These procedures shall clearly and specifically outline the scope, purpose, authorization, rules, and techniques to be utilized for the control of hazardous energy, and the means to enforce compliance. Per 29 CFR 1910.147(c)(4) and WAC 296-803-300.

7.4 Application of Control

  1. The established procedure for the application of energy control (lockout/tagout) shall include the following steps:
  • Notify all affected employees that servicing or maintenance is required and the machine or equipment must be shut down and locked out/tagged out.
  • The authorized employee shall know the type and magnitude of the energy that the machine or equipment utilizes, the hazards of the energy, and the methods to control the energy.
  • If the machine or equipment is operating, shut it down by the normal stopping procedure (e.g., depress stop button, open switch, etc.).
  • De-activate the energy isolating device(s) so that the machine or equipment is isolated from the energy source(s).
  • Lock out and/or tag out the energy isolating device(s) with assigned individual lockout/tagout devices.
  • Stored or residual energy (e.g., capacitors, springs, elevated machine members, rotating flywheels, hydraulic systems, air, gas, steam, or water pressure, etc.) must be safely relieved, disconnected, restrained, or otherwise rendered safe.
  • After ensuring that no personnel are exposed, the authorized employee shall verify that the isolation and de-energization of the machine or equipment have been accomplished by attempting to operate the machine or equipment.

7.5 Release from Lockout/Tagout

  1. Before lockout or tagout devices are removed and energy is restored to the machine or equipment, the following steps shall be taken:
  • Inspect the work area to ensure that non-essential items have been removed and that machine or equipment components are operationally intact.
  • Ensure that all employees have been safely positioned or removed from the area.
  • Verify that controls are in neutral or off position.
  • Remove the lockout/tagout devices by the authorized employee who applied them.
  • Notify affected employees that the lockout/tagout devices have been removed and the equipment is ready for use.

7.6 Group Lockout/Tagout

When servicing and/or maintenance is performed by a crew, craft, department, or other group, they shall utilize a procedure which affords a level of protection equivalent to that provided by the implementation of a personal lockout or tagout device. Per 29 CFR 1910.147(f)(3) and WAC 296-803-600.

7.7 Shift or Personnel Changes

Specific procedures shall be utilized during shift or personnel changes to ensure the continuity of lockout or tagout protection, including the orderly transfer of lockout or tagout devices between off-going and on-coming employees, to minimize exposure to hazards from the unexpected energization or startup of the machine or equipment, or the release of stored energy. Per 29 CFR 1910.147(f)(4) and WAC 296-803-600.

Training Requirements

All authorized employees shall receive training in the recognition of hazardous energy sources, the type and magnitude of the energy available in the workplace, and the methods and means necessary for energy isolation and control. All affected employees shall be instructed in the purpose and use of the energy control procedure. Retraining shall be provided for all authorized and affected employees whenever there is a change in job assignments, machines, equipment or processes that present a new hazard, or when there is a change in the energy control procedures. Training shall be documented and maintained by MH Construction, Inc. Per 29 CFR 1910.147(g) and WAC 296-803-700.

Inspection and Enforcement

Periodic inspections of the energy control procedure shall be performed at least annually to ensure that the procedure and the requirements of this standard are being followed. The inspection shall be performed by an authorized employee other than the one(s) utilizing the energy control procedure being inspected. Any deviations or inadequacies identified during the inspection shall be corrected immediately. Enforcement of this policy will follow MH Construction's progressive disciplinary action policy.

Recordkeeping

MH Construction, Inc. shall maintain records of all employee training, including dates, attendees, and topics covered. Records of periodic inspections of energy control procedures, including the equipment inspected, date of inspection, and employees involved, shall also be maintained. These records shall be retained for a minimum of three years and be readily available for review by regulatory authorities. The Safety Officer is responsible for maintaining these records.

Violation Consequences

Failure to comply with the Lockout/Tagout program and procedures will result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment. Subcontractors who fail to comply will be removed from the job site and may face contract termination. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and gravity of the violation, as well as any previous infractions.

References

OSHA: 29 CFR 1910.147 Control of Hazardous Energy (Lockout/Tagout)

OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.417 Lockout and Tagging of Circuits

WISHA: WAC 296-803 Lockout/Tagout

WISHA: WAC 296-155-429 Lockout and Tagging of Circuits

AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 10 LOTO

MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 16 | Confined Space Entry

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29

CFR 1926 Subpart AA

OSHA

29

CFR 1926.1200 Scope

OSHA

29

CFR 1926.1203 General Requirements

OSHA

29

CFR 1926.1204 Permit-Required Confined Spaces

WISHA

WAC

296-155-203 Confined Spaces

WISHA

WAC

296-155-20301 Definitions

WISHA

WAC

296-809 Confined Spaces General Industry adopted by reference

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 11 Confined Space

Purpose

This policy establishes clear guidelines and procedures for confined space entry at MH Construction, Inc. It aims to prevent injuries, illnesses, and fatalities associated with confined space hazards, ensuring a safe working environment for all personnel.

Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors who may be required to enter or work near confined spaces on any MH Construction job site.

Definitions

Term

Definition

Confined Space

A space that is large enough and so configured that an employee can bodily enter and perform assigned work; has limited or restricted means for entry or exit; and is not designed for continuous employee occupancy.

Permit-Required Confined Space (PRCS)

A confined space that has one or more of the following characteristics: contains or has a potential to contain a hazardous atmosphere; contains a material that has the potential for engulfing an entrant; has an internal configuration such that an entrant could be trapped or asphyxiated by inwardly converging walls or by a floor which slopes downward and tapers to a smaller cross-section; or contains any other recognized serious safety or health hazard.

Authorized Entrant

An employee who is authorized by the employer to enter a permit space.

Attendant

An individual stationed outside one or more permit spaces who monitors the authorized entrants and who performs all attendant's duties assigned in the employer's permit space program.

Entry Supervisor

The person responsible for determining if acceptable entry conditions are present at a permit space where entry is planned, for authorizing entry and overseeing entry operations, and for terminating entry as required by this section.

Responsibilities

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Develop, implement, and maintain a comprehensive Confined Space Entry Program.
  • Provide necessary equipment, training, and resources to ensure safe confined space operations.
  • Ensure compliance with all applicable OSHA (29 CFR 1926 Subpart AA) and WISHA (WAC 296-155-203, WAC 296-809) regulations.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Identify and evaluate all confined spaces on job sites.
  • Ensure all personnel involved in confined space entry are properly trained and equipped.
  • Issue and manage confined space entry permits, ensuring all conditions are met before entry.
  • Monitor confined space operations and ensure adherence to safety procedures.

Employee Responsibilities

  • Comply with all confined space entry procedures and safety rules.
  • Attend and actively participate in all required confined space training.
  • Report any unsafe conditions or practices related to confined spaces to their supervisor immediately.
  • Use all provided safety equipment correctly.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Adhere to MH Construction, Inc.'s Confined Space Entry Program and all applicable regulatory requirements.
  • Provide their employees with appropriate training and equipment for confined space entry.
  • Coordinate all confined space entry activities with MH Construction, Inc. site supervision.

Procedures / Requirements

  1. 7.1 Confined Space Identification and Evaluation All potential confined spaces on a job site must be identified and evaluated by a competent person. This evaluation will determine if the space is a permit-required confined space (PRCS) as defined by OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1200 and WISHA WAC 296-155-20301.
  2. 7.2 Permit-Required Confined Space Entry Program MH Construction, Inc. will implement a written PRCS program in accordance with OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1203 and WISHA WAC 296-155-203. This program will include procedures for identifying, evaluating, and controlling hazards, as well as for safe entry and rescue operations.
  3. 7.3 Entry Permit System A confined space entry permit must be completed and authorized by an Entry Supervisor before any entry into a PRCS. The permit will specify the space to be entered, the purpose of entry, date and authorized duration of entry, authorized entrants, attendants, and entry supervisor, hazards of the permit space, measures used to control hazards, acceptable entry conditions, results of initial and periodic atmospheric monitoring, rescue and emergency services, and communication procedures. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1204)
  4. 7.4 Atmospheric Monitoring Before entry and periodically during entry, the atmosphere within the confined space must be tested for oxygen content, flammable gases and vapors, and potential toxic air contaminants. Continuous monitoring is required where there is a potential for hazardous atmospheric conditions to arise. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1203(b)(4) and WISHA WAC 296-155-203(4))
  5. 7.5 Isolation and Hazard Control Prior to entry, all energy sources must be isolated (lockout/tagout), and any other identified hazards (e.g., engulfment, mechanical, electrical) must be eliminated or controlled. This includes purging, inerting, flushing, or ventilating the space as necessary. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1203(b)(5))
  6. 7.6 Rescue and Emergency Services MH Construction, Inc. will ensure that rescue and emergency services are available and capable of responding in a timely manner. This includes providing appropriate rescue equipment and ensuring personnel are trained in rescue procedures. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1203(b)(9) and WISHA WAC 296-155-203(9))
  7. 7.7 Communication Procedures Effective communication must be maintained between authorized entrants, attendants, and the entry supervisor throughout the entry operation. This may include two-way radios, visual signals, or other approved methods. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1203(b)(7))
  8. 7.8 Attendant Requirements An attendant must be stationed outside the permit space for the duration of the entry. The attendant's duties include monitoring entrants, maintaining communication, ordering evacuation if necessary, and summoning rescue services. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1203(f))
  9. 7.9 Hot Work in Confined Spaces Any hot work (e.g., welding, cutting) performed within a confined space requires additional precautions, including a hot work permit, continuous atmospheric monitoring, and appropriate fire suppression equipment. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.352 and WISHA WAC 296-155-408)
  10. 7.10 Training and Competency All personnel involved in confined space entry, including entrants, attendants, and entry supervisors, must receive comprehensive training to ensure they understand the hazards, procedures, and their roles. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1207 and WISHA WAC 296-155-203(10))

Training Requirements

All employees whose work involves confined spaces, including authorized entrants, attendants, and entry supervisors, must receive training prior to their initial assignment and whenever there is a change in job duties, permit space operations, or when deficiencies in their knowledge or use of the permit space program are noted. Training will cover hazards, safe work practices, emergency procedures, and proper use of equipment. Training records, including employee names, dates of training, and means used to verify proficiency, will be maintained. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1207 and WISHA WAC 296-155-203(10))

Inspection and Enforcement

Compliance with this Confined Space Entry Program will be monitored through regular site inspections, permit audits, and safety observations. Any non-compliance will be addressed immediately through corrective actions, which may include retraining, disciplinary action, or removal from confined space duties. The Safety Officer is responsible for overseeing the inspection and enforcement process.

Recordkeeping

The following records will be maintained by MH Construction, Inc. for a minimum of five years, or as required by specific regulations:

  • Confined Space Entry Permits (completed permits)
  • Atmospheric monitoring results
  • Training records for all personnel involved in confined space entry
  • Equipment inspection and maintenance records
  • Rescue drills and emergency response evaluations

Violation Consequences

Violation of this Confined Space Entry Program or any related safety procedures will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and gravity of the violation, potential for harm, and previous safety record. All incidents will be thoroughly investigated.

References

29 CFR 1926 Subpart AA

29 CFR 1926.1200 Scope

29 CFR 1926.1203 General Requirements

29 CFR 1926.1204 Permit-Required Confined Spaces

WAC 296-155-203 Confined Spaces

WAC 296-155-20301 Definitions

WAC 296-809 Confined Spaces General Industry adopted by reference

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 11 Confined Space
