sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 17 | Use and Care of Ladders

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926 Subpart X

Ladders

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.1050

Scope

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.1053

Ladders

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.1060

Training Requirements

WISHA

WAC 296-876

Ladders Portable and Fixed

WISHA

WAC 296-155-475

Stairways Scope

WISHA

WAC 296-155-477

Stairways

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 12

Ladders and Stairways

1. Purpose

The purpose of this policy is to establish minimum safety requirements for the use, care, and maintenance of ladders and stairways on all MH Construction, Inc. projects. This policy protects employees from fall hazards, structural failures, and other injuries associated with improper ladder use, ensuring compliance with OSHA and WISHA standards.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, vendors, and visitors operating on or visiting any MH Construction job site or facility where ladders or stairways are utilized.

3. Definitions

Term

Definition

Competent Person

One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Extension Ladder

A non-self-supporting portable ladder adjustable in length. It consists of two or more sections traveling in guides or brackets so arranged as to permit length adjustment.

Stepladder

A self-supporting portable ladder, nonadjustable in length, having flat steps and a hinged back.

Fixed Ladder

A ladder that cannot be readily moved or carried because it is an integral part of a building or structure.

Maximum Intended Load

The total load of all persons, equipment, tools, materials, transmitted loads, and other loads reasonably anticipated to be applied to a ladder or stairway at any one time.

4. Responsibilities

4.1 Company/Management

  • Provide appropriate, ANSI-approved ladders for the specific tasks required.
  • Ensure comprehensive training programs are developed and implemented per 29 CFR 1926.1060.
  • Maintain records of ladder inspections and training.

4.2 Supervisor/Foreman

  • Act as or designate a Competent Person to inspect ladders periodically and after any incident that could affect their safe use.
  • Ensure employees use the correct ladder for the job and follow all safety procedures.
  • Remove defective ladders from service immediately and tag them "Do Not Use".

4.3 Employee

  • Inspect ladders visually before each use.
  • Use ladders only for their designed purpose and within their load ratings.
  • Report any damaged or defective ladders to the Supervisor/Foreman immediately.

4.4 Subcontractor

  • Comply with all requirements of this MISH section, OSHA, and WISHA standards.
  • Provide their own ANSI-approved ladders and ensure their employees are properly trained.

5. Procedures and Requirements

5.1 General Ladder Safety

  1. All ladders used on MH Construction sites must meet or exceed ANSI standards and be rated for the intended load (minimum Type I, 250 lbs, or Type IA, 300 lbs).
  2. Ladders must be maintained free of oil, grease, and other slipping hazards per 29 CFR 1926.1053(b)(1).
  3. Ladders shall not be loaded beyond the maximum intended load for which they were built, nor beyond their manufacturer's rated capacity.
  4. Ladders must only be used on stable and level surfaces unless secured to prevent accidental displacement.

5.2 Portable Ladders (Stepladders and Extension Ladders)

  1. When portable ladders are used for access to an upper landing surface, the ladder side rails must extend at least 3 feet (.9 m) above the upper landing surface to which the ladder is used to gain access (29 CFR 1926.1053(b)(1)).
  2. Non-self-supporting ladders must be used at an angle such that the horizontal distance from the top support to the foot of the ladder is approximately one-quarter of the working length of the ladder (the 4:1 rule).
  3. Stepladders must not be used as single ladders or in a partially closed position.
  4. Employees shall not stand on the top step or top cap of a stepladder (29 CFR 1926.1053(b)(13)).

5.3 Safe Climbing Practices

  1. Employees must maintain three points of contact (two hands and a foot, or two feet and a hand) at all times when ascending or descending a ladder.
  2. Employees must face the ladder when climbing up or down (29 CFR 1926.1053(b)(20)).
  3. Employees shall not carry any object or load that could cause them to lose balance and fall (29 CFR 1926.1053(b)(22)). Tools should be carried in a tool belt or hoisted up after the employee has reached the working level.

5.4 Electrical Hazards

  1. Ladders with conductive side rails (e.g., aluminum) shall not be used where the employee or the ladder could contact exposed energized electrical equipment (29 CFR 1926.1053(b)(12)).
  2. Fiberglass ladders are required for all electrical work or when working in proximity to electrical hazards.

5.5 Defective Ladders

  1. Ladders with structural defects, such as broken or missing rungs, cleats, or steps, broken or split rails, corroded components, or other faulty or defective components, must immediately be marked in a manner that readily identifies them as defective, or be tagged with "Do Not Use" or similar language, and shall be withdrawn from service until repaired (29 CFR 1926.1053(b)(16)).
  2. Repairs must restore the ladder to a condition meeting its original design criteria before it is returned to use.

5.6 Washington State Specific Requirements (WISHA)

  1. Per WAC 296-876, employers must ensure that portable ladders are used only on stable and level surfaces unless they are secured or provided with slip-resistant feet.
  2. When ladders are placed in areas such as passageways, doorways, or driveways, or where they can be displaced by workplace activities or traffic, they must be secured to prevent accidental displacement, or a barricade must be used to keep the activities or traffic away from the ladder.
  3. WAC 296-155-475 and 477 dictate specific requirements for stairways used during construction, including the provision of handrails and the elimination of hazardous projections.

5.7 Stairways

  1. Stairways that will not be a permanent part of the structure on which construction work is being performed must have landings of not less than 30 inches in the direction of travel and extend at least 22 inches in width at every 12 feet or less of vertical rise.
  2. Stairs must be installed between 30 degrees and 50 degrees from horizontal.
  3. Stairways having four or more risers or rising more than 30 inches, whichever is less, must be equipped with at least one handrail and one stairrail system along each unprotected side or edge.

6. Training Requirements

Per 29 CFR 1926.1060, MH Construction shall provide a training program for each employee using ladders and stairways. The program shall enable each employee to recognize hazards related to ladders and stairways and shall train each employee in the procedures to be followed to minimize these hazards.

Training must be conducted by a Competent Person and cover:

  • The nature of fall hazards in the work area.
  • The correct procedures for erecting, maintaining, and disassembling the fall protection systems to be used.
  • The proper construction, use, placement, and care in handling of all stairways and ladders.
  • The maximum intended load-carrying capacities of ladders used.

Retraining shall be provided for each employee as necessary so that the employee maintains the understanding and knowledge acquired through compliance with this section.

7. Inspection and Enforcement

Ladders shall be inspected by a Competent Person for visible defects on a periodic basis and after any occurrence that could affect their safe use. Employees must conduct a visual inspection prior to each use. The Safety Officer or designated representative will conduct random site audits to ensure compliance with this policy. Non-compliant equipment will be immediately removed from service.

8. Recordkeeping

The Safety Officer shall maintain records of all ladder and stairway safety training, including the names of employees trained, dates of training, and the signature of the person who conducted the training or the employer. These records shall be retained for the duration of the employee's employment plus three years. Inspection logs for ladders should be maintained on-site for the duration of the project.

9. Violation Consequences

Failure to comply with this policy will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. Disciplinary steps typically include:

  1. First Offense: Verbal warning and immediate retraining.
  2. Second Offense: Written warning documented in the employee's personnel file.
  3. Third Offense: Suspension without pay or termination, depending on the severity of the violation and potential for injury.

10. References

  • OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart X - Stairways and Ladders
  • OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1050 - Scope, application, and definitions applicable to this subpart
  • OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1053 - Ladders
  • OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1060 - Training requirements
  • WISHA WAC 296-876 - Ladders, Portable and Fixed
  • WISHA WAC 296-155-475 - Scope and application (Stairways)
  • WISHA WAC 296-155-477 - Stairways
  • AGC Safety and Health Manual Chapter 12 - Ladders and Stairways

sections.title: motor vehicle safety program
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 18 | Motor Vehicle Safety Program Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey ────────────────────────────────────────────────── Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart O OSHA 29 CFR 1926. 600 Equipment General OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 18 | Motor Vehicle Safety Program

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

──────────────────────────────────────────────────

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926 Subpart O

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.600 Equipment General

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.601 Motor Vehicles

WISHA

WAC 296-155-600 Definitions

WISHA

WAC 296-155-610 Motor Vehicles on Construction Sites

WISHA

WAC 296-155-615 Material Handling Equipment

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 13 Motor Vehicles and Equipment

PURPOSE

This policy establishes guidelines and procedures to ensure the safe operation of motor vehicles and related equipment on MH Construction job sites. Its primary purpose is to prevent accidents, injuries, and property damage associated with vehicle use, thereby protecting employees, subcontractors, and the public.

SCOPE

This program applies to all MH Construction employees, subcontractors, and visitors operating or working near motor vehicles and equipment on company-controlled job sites, including company-owned, rented, or personal vehicles used for company business.

DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Motor Vehicle

Any vehicle that is self-propelled and used for transportation or work on a construction site, including trucks, cars, forklifts, loaders, and other heavy equipment.

Operator

An individual authorized and trained to operate a specific motor vehicle or piece of equipment.

Spotter

A designated person who assists equipment operators by providing visual guidance to ensure safe movement, especially in blind spots or congested areas.

Blind Spot

Areas around a vehicle or equipment where the operator's vision is obstructed, requiring additional vigilance or assistance from a spotter.

Competent Person

One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

RESPONSIBILITIES

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • • Provide safe vehicles and equipment, ensuring regular maintenance and inspections.
  • • Ensure operators are trained and competent for the equipment they operate.
  • • Establish and enforce clear motor vehicle safety policies and procedures.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • • Ensure all personnel under their supervision adhere to motor vehicle safety policies.
  • • Verify that operators are qualified and authorized to operate specific equipment.
  • • Conduct daily pre-shift inspections of vehicles and equipment.
  • • Ensure proper use of spotters and communication protocols in congested areas.

Employee Responsibilities

  • • Operate vehicles and equipment safely and only if authorized and trained.
  • • Conduct pre-operational checks and report any defects immediately.
  • • Wear seatbelts and appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE).
  • • Adhere to all traffic laws, site speed limits, and company safety rules.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • • Ensure their employees comply with all MH Construction motor vehicle safety policies and procedures.
  • • Provide properly maintained and inspected vehicles and equipment.
  • • Verify that their operators are trained, certified, and competent.

PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

  1. 7.1 Vehicle and Equipment Maintenance: All motor vehicles and equipment shall be maintained in a safe operating condition. Regular preventative maintenance schedules will be followed. Any vehicle found to have defects affecting safe operation shall be immediately removed from service until repaired. (Per 29 CFR 1926.600(a)(6))
  2. 7.2 Operator Qualifications: Only trained and authorized personnel shall operate motor vehicles and equipment. Operators must possess valid licenses and any required certifications for the specific type of equipment. (Per WAC 296-155-610(1))
  3. 7.3 Pre-Operational Inspections: Before each shift, operators shall conduct a thorough pre-operational inspection of their assigned vehicle or equipment. This includes checking tires, brakes, lights, fluid levels, horn, and safety devices. Any deficiencies must be reported to a supervisor immediately. (Per 29 CFR 1926.600(a)(5))
  4. 7.4 Seatbelts and PPE: All occupants of motor vehicles on job sites must wear seatbelts where provided. Operators shall wear appropriate Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) as required by job site conditions and equipment type. (Per WAC 296-155-610(2))
  5. 7.5 Safe Operating Practices: Operators must adhere to site-specific speed limits, maintain safe following distances, and exercise extreme caution when operating in congested areas, near pedestrians, or in adverse weather conditions. Backing operations require a spotter unless the operator has a clear view of the path of travel. (Per 29 CFR 1926.601(b)(4) and WAC 296-155-610(3))
  6. 7.6 Material Handling Equipment: Forklifts, loaders, and similar material handling equipment must be operated in accordance with manufacturer's instructions and specific WISHA requirements for industrial trucks. Loads must be stable and within the equipment's rated capacity. (Per WAC 296-155-615)
  7. 7.7 Traffic Control and Pedestrian Safety: Designated traffic routes shall be established on job sites. Pedestrian walkways must be clearly marked and separated from vehicle traffic where feasible. Operators must yield to pedestrians and maintain constant vigilance for personnel in their work area. (Per 29 CFR 1926.201(a)(1))
  8. 7.8 Securing Loads: All loads transported on or off site must be properly secured to prevent shifting or falling during transit. This includes proper chaining, strapping, and covering as necessary. (Per WAC 296-155-610(4))
  9. 7.9 Parking and Idling: Vehicles and equipment must be parked in designated areas, with brakes set and keys removed when unattended. Idling should be minimized to reduce emissions and noise. (Per WAC 296-155-610(5))
  10. 7.10 Emergency Procedures: Operators must be familiar with emergency shutdown procedures, fire extinguisher locations, and communication protocols in case of an accident or equipment malfunction. All incidents must be reported immediately.

TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

All employees who operate motor vehicles or equipment on MH Construction job sites must receive initial and refresher training covering safe operating procedures, pre-operational inspections, hazard recognition, and emergency protocols. Training will be documented and conducted by a competent person. Specific training mandates include: (Per 29 CFR 1926.21(b)(2) and WAC 296-155-610(1))

  • • Initial training for all new operators.
  • • Refresher training as needed, or at least every three years.
  • • Training on specific equipment types before operation.
  • • Documentation of all training sessions, including dates, topics, and attendees.

INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

Compliance with this Motor Vehicle Safety Program will be monitored through regular site inspections, supervisor observations, and incident investigations. Unsafe acts or conditions will be addressed immediately. Corrective actions will be implemented, and employees will be held accountable for adherence to these policies.

RECORDKEEPING

The following records shall be maintained by MH Construction:

  • • Vehicle and equipment maintenance logs.
  • • Operator training records and certifications.
  • • Pre-operational inspection checklists.
  • • Incident and accident investigation reports related to motor vehicle operations.

Records will be retained for a minimum of five years, or as required by specific regulatory standards, and will be managed by the Safety Officer.

VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

Violations of this Motor Vehicle Safety Program will result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. The disciplinary process will be progressive, taking into account the severity of the violation and prior infractions. Immediate action may be taken for severe violations that pose imminent danger.

REFERENCES

  • 29 CFR 1926 Subpart O
  • 29 CFR 1926.600 Equipment General
  • 29 CFR 1926.601 Motor Vehicles
  • WAC 296-155-600 Definitions
  • WAC 296-155-610 Motor Vehicles on Construction Sites
  • WAC 296-155-615 Material Handling Equipment
  • Safety and Health Manual Ch. 13 Motor Vehicles and Equipment

sections.title: equipment maintenance and inspection
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.
Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)
MISH 19 | Equipment Maintenance and Inspection
Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

________________________________________________________________________________

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926 Subpart O

Motor Vehicles, Mechanized Equipment, and Marine Operations

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.600(a)

Equipment Condition

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.602

Material Handling Equipment

WISHA

WAC 296-155-605

Equipment

WISHA

WAC 296-155-610

Motor Vehicles on Construction Sites

WISHA

WAC 296-155-615

Material Handling Equipment

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 13

Equipment Maintenance and Inspection

1. Purpose

The purpose of this Equipment Maintenance and Inspection policy is to establish minimum requirements for the safe operation, inspection, and maintenance of all motorized and mechanized equipment used on MH Construction, Inc. (MHC) projects. This policy ensures that all equipment is maintained in safe operating condition, protecting employees, subcontractors, and the public from hazards associated with equipment failure or malfunction.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, vendors, and visitors operating or working near motorized and mechanized equipment on MHC job sites. It covers all company-owned, leased, rented, and subcontractor-provided equipment, including but not limited to earthmoving equipment, material handling equipment, aerial lifts, and motor vehicles.

3. Definitions

Term

Definition

Competent Person

One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Qualified Person

One who, by possession of a recognized degree, certificate, or professional standing, or who by extensive knowledge, training, and experience, has successfully demonstrated their ability to solve or resolve problems relating to the subject matter, the work, or the project.

Earthmoving Equipment

Scrapers, loaders, crawler or wheel tractors, bulldozers, off-highway trucks, graders, agricultural and industrial tractors, and similar equipment.

Material Handling Equipment

Equipment used for the movement, storage, control and protection of materials, goods and products throughout the process of manufacturing, distribution, consumption and disposal, including forklifts and telehandlers.

Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)

Specific practices and procedures to safeguard employees from the unexpected energization or startup of machinery and equipment, or the release of hazardous energy during service or maintenance activities.

4. Responsibilities

4.1 Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Provide resources and support to ensure all equipment is maintained in safe operating condition.
  • Ensure that only trained and authorized personnel operate and maintain equipment.
  • Establish and maintain a comprehensive preventative maintenance program for all company-owned equipment.
  • Review and update this policy as necessary to comply with changing regulations and industry best practices.

4.2 Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Ensure that daily pre-shift inspections are conducted and documented for all equipment on their site.
  • Remove from service any equipment found to be defective or unsafe until repaired.
  • Verify that operators possess the required training, certifications, and licenses for the equipment they operate.
  • Coordinate with the Safety Officer and maintenance personnel to schedule necessary repairs and preventative maintenance.

4.3 Employee Responsibilities

  • Conduct thorough pre-shift inspections of equipment prior to use.
  • Operate equipment only if trained, authorized, and in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions.
  • Immediately report any equipment defects, malfunctions, or safety concerns to their supervisor.
  • Never operate equipment that has been tagged out of service.

4.4 Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Comply with all requirements of this policy and applicable OSHA/WISHA regulations regarding equipment maintenance and inspection.
  • Provide documentation of equipment inspections, operator training, and maintenance records upon request by MHC management.
  • Ensure their equipment is maintained in safe operating condition and removed from service if defective.

5. Procedures / Requirements

5.1 General Equipment Condition

  1. Per 29 CFR 1926.600(a) and WAC 296-155-605, all equipment left unattended at night, adjacent to a highway in normal use, or adjacent to construction areas where work is in progress, shall have appropriate lights or reflectors, or barricades equipped with appropriate lights or reflectors, to identify the location of the equipment.
  2. A safety tire rack, cage, or equivalent protection shall be provided and used when inflating, mounting, or dismounting tires installed on split rims, or rims equipped with locking rings or similar devices.
  3. Heavy machinery, equipment, or parts thereof, which are suspended or held aloft by use of slings, hoists, or jacks shall be substantially blocked or cribbed to prevent falling or shifting before employees are permitted to work under or between them.
  4. Bulldozer and scraper blades, end-loader buckets, dump bodies, and similar equipment, shall be either fully lowered or blocked when being repaired or when not in use. All controls shall be in a neutral position, with the motors stopped and brakes set, unless work being performed dictates otherwise.

5.2 Pre-Shift Inspections

  1. Prior to the start of each shift, the operator must conduct a visual and operational inspection of the equipment they will be using.
  2. The inspection must be documented using the MHC Daily Equipment Inspection Checklist or an approved digital equivalent.
  3. The inspection shall include, but is not limited to: fluid levels (oil, coolant, hydraulic), belts, hoses, tires/tracks, brakes (service and parking), steering, horn, backup alarm, lights, mirrors, seatbelts, ROPS/FOPS, and all operating controls.
  4. If any safety-critical defect is identified during the inspection, the equipment must be immediately removed from service, tagged with a "Do Not Operate" tag, and reported to the supervisor.

5.3 Motor Vehicles on Construction Sites

  1. Per WAC 296-155-610, all vehicles shall have a service brake system, an emergency brake system, and a parking brake system. These systems may use common components, and shall be maintained in operable condition.
  2. Whenever visibility conditions warrant additional light, all vehicles, or combinations of vehicles, in use shall be equipped with at least two headlights and two taillights in operable condition.
  3. All vehicles, or combination of vehicles, shall have brake lights in operable condition regardless of light conditions.
  4. All vehicles shall be equipped with an adequate audible warning device at the operator's station and in an operable condition.
  5. No employer shall use any motor vehicle equipment having an obstructed view to the rear unless the vehicle has a reverse signal alarm audible above the surrounding noise level or the vehicle is backed up only when an observer signals that it is safe to do so.

5.4 Material Handling Equipment

  1. Per 29 CFR 1926.602 and WAC 296-155-615, earthmoving equipment such as scrapers, loaders, crawler or wheel tractors, bulldozers, off-highway trucks, graders, agricultural and industrial tractors, and similar equipment shall be equipped with seat belts.
  2. Seat belts shall meet the requirements of the Society of Automotive Engineers, J386-1969, Seat Belts for Construction Equipment.
  3. All earthmoving equipment shall have a rollover protective structure (ROPS) that meets the requirements of WAC 296-155 Part V.
  4. Bi-directional machines, such as rollers, compacters, front-end loaders, bulldozers, and similar equipment, shall be equipped with a horn, distinguishable from the surrounding noise level, which shall be operated as needed when the machine is moving in either direction.

5.5 Preventative Maintenance Program

  1. MHC shall maintain a preventative maintenance program for all company-owned equipment based on the manufacturer's recommendations.
  2. Maintenance schedules shall be tracked using the company's fleet management software or designated maintenance logs.
  3. Only qualified mechanics or authorized service providers shall perform maintenance and repairs on equipment.
  4. All replacement parts must meet or exceed the original equipment manufacturer (OEM) specifications.

5.6 Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) during Maintenance

  1. Before any maintenance, inspection, or repair work is performed where the unexpected energization, start-up, or release of stored energy could cause injury, the equipment must be isolated and rendered inoperative.
  2. Employees performing the maintenance must apply their personal lockout/tagout devices in accordance with the MHC Lockout/Tagout Program.
  3. Stored energy (such as hydraulic pressure, pneumatic pressure, or gravity) must be dissipated or restrained by methods such as grounding, repositioning, blocking, or bleeding down.

5.7 Modifications and Alterations

  1. No modifications or additions which affect the capacity or safe operation of the equipment shall be made without the manufacturer's written approval.
  2. If such modifications or changes are made, the capacity, operation, and maintenance instruction plates, tags, or decals shall be changed accordingly.
  3. In no case shall the original safety factor of the equipment be reduced.

5.8 Washington State Specific Requirements (WISHA)

  1. In accordance with WAC 296-155-605, all equipment must be inspected by a competent person before each use and during use to ensure it is in safe operating condition.
  2. Any equipment found to be unsafe must be tagged out of service and not used until repaired by a qualified person.
  3. Operators must be protected from falling objects by overhead protection (FOPS) when operating equipment in areas where such hazards exist.

6. Training Requirements

All employees who operate, inspect, or maintain equipment must receive appropriate training prior to being assigned such duties. Training shall include:

  • Classroom or online instruction covering the specific equipment type, operating characteristics, and hazards.
  • Practical, hands-on evaluation by a competent person demonstrating the employee's ability to safely operate the equipment.
  • Review of the manufacturer's operator manual and this MISH policy.
  • Specific training on conducting pre-shift inspections and identifying defects.

Refresher training and evaluation shall be conducted at least every three years, or sooner if the operator is observed operating unsafely, is involved in an accident or near-miss, or is assigned to a different type of equipment.

7. Inspection and Enforcement

Compliance with this policy will be monitored through:

  • Daily review of pre-shift inspection checklists by site supervisors.
  • Weekly site safety walks conducted by the Safety Officer or designated competent person.
  • Periodic audits of maintenance records and equipment condition by MHC management.

Any equipment found operating with safety-critical defects or without a documented pre-shift inspection will be immediately shut down. The supervisor will investigate the root cause and implement corrective actions before the equipment is returned to service.

8. Recordkeeping

The following records shall be maintained by the Safety Officer or designated personnel:

  • Daily pre-shift inspection checklists: Retained for the duration of the project or at least 90 days, whichever is longer.
  • Preventative maintenance and repair records: Retained for the life of the equipment.
  • Operator training and evaluation records: Retained for the duration of employment plus three years.
  • Manufacturer's manuals: Kept with the equipment or readily available on site.

9. Violation Consequences

Failure to comply with this Equipment Maintenance and Inspection policy, including operating defective equipment, failing to conduct pre-shift inspections, or bypassing safety devices, will result in disciplinary action up to and including termination of employment or contract. MHC utilizes a progressive discipline policy:

  1. First Offense: Verbal warning and documented retraining.
  2. Second Offense: Written warning and potential suspension from equipment operation duties.
  3. Third Offense: Termination of employment or removal of the subcontractor from the project.

Severe violations, such as intentionally disabling safety devices (e.g., backup alarms) or operating equipment in a reckless manner that endangers life, may result in immediate termination without prior warnings.

10. References

  • OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart O - Motor Vehicles, Mechanized Equipment, and Marine Operations
  • OSHA 29 CFR 1926.600 - Equipment
  • OSHA 29 CFR 1926.602 - Material Handling Equipment
  • WISHA WAC 296-155-605 - Equipment
  • WISHA WAC 296-155-610 - Motor Vehicles on Construction Sites
  • WISHA WAC 296-155-615 - Material Handling Equipment
  • AGC Safety and Health Manual Chapter 13
  • MHC Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) Program

sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 20 | Aerial Lifts and Elevating Work Platforms Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey —————————————————————————————————————————————————— Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 20 | Aerial Lifts and Elevating Work Platforms

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

——————————————————————————————————————————————————

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29

CFR 1926.453 Aerial Lifts

OSHA

29

CFR 1926 Subpart L Scaffolds for scissor lifts

OSHA

ANSI/SIA

A92 Series

WISHA

WAC

296-880 Unified Safety Standards for Fall Protection AWPs

WISHA

WAC

296-155-53400 Cranes General Requirements for boom lifts

AGC

Safety

and Health Manual Ch. 14 Aerial Work Platforms

1. PURPOSE

This policy establishes guidelines for the safe operation and maintenance of aerial lifts and elevating work platforms. It aims to prevent accidents, injuries, and fatalities associated with their use, ensuring a secure working environment for all personnel.

2. SCOPE

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors involved in operations utilizing aerial lifts and elevating work platforms on any MH Construction job site.

3. DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Aerial Lift

Any vehicle-mounted device telescoping, articulating, rotating, or any combination thereof, which is used to position personnel.

Elevating Work Platform

A mobile work platform that can be elevated to various heights, including scissor lifts.

Competent Person

One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Operator

An individual trained and authorized to operate an aerial lift or elevating work platform.

Fall Protection

Any equipment or system designed to prevent falls or reduce the severity of injuries from falls, such as harnesses and lanyards.

4. RESPONSIBILITIES

  • Company/Management Responsibilities:
  • Ensure all aerial lifts and elevating work platforms are maintained in safe operating condition.
  • Provide appropriate training and resources for safe operation.
  • Conduct regular safety audits and inspections.
  • Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities:
  • Verify operators are trained and authorized.
  • Ensure pre-shift inspections are completed.
  • Monitor work site conditions for hazards.
  • Employee Responsibilities:
  • Operate equipment only if trained and authorized.
  • Conduct pre-use inspections and report defects.
  • Follow all safety procedures and wear required PPE.
  • Subcontractor Responsibilities:
  • Adhere to all MH Construction safety policies and procedures.
  • Ensure their employees are properly trained and certified.
  • Provide safe equipment and maintain it in good working order.

5. PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

  1. 5.1 Operator Training and Authorization
  2. Only trained and authorized personnel shall operate aerial lifts and elevating work platforms. Training must include classroom instruction, practical demonstration, and evaluation of operator proficiency. (Per 29 CFR 1926.453(b)(2) and WAC 296-880-200)
  3. 5.2 Pre-Operation Inspection
  4. A thorough visual inspection and functional test must be performed by the operator before each shift. This includes checking controls, safety devices, tires, outriggers, and fluid levels. Any defects must be reported immediately and the equipment taken out of service. (Per 29 CFR 1926.453(b)(1) and WAC 296-880-300)
  5. 5.3 Fall Protection
  6. Operators and occupants of aerial lifts must use a body belt/harness with a lanyard attached to the boom or basket. Fall protection is required at all times when working from an aerial lift. (Per 29 CFR 1926.453(b)(2)(v) and WAC 296-880-400)
  7. 5.4 Work Site Preparation
  8. Before operating, the work area must be inspected for hazards such as uneven terrain, overhead power lines, unstable surfaces, and pedestrian traffic. Barricades and warning signs must be used as necessary. (Per 29 CFR 1926.453(b)(2)(viii) and WAC 296-155-53400)
  9. 5.5 Safe Operating Practices
  10. Operators must maintain a safe distance from power lines, never exceed load capacity, and avoid sudden movements. Do not move the lift with the boom extended and personnel in the basket unless specifically designed for such operation. (Per 29 CFR 1926.453(b)(2)(viii) and WAC 296-155-53400)
  11. 5.6 Maintenance and Repair
  12. All maintenance and repairs must be performed by qualified personnel according to manufacturer specifications. Records of all maintenance must be kept. (Per ANSI/SIA A92 Series)

6. TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

All employees who operate, maintain, or work near aerial lifts and elevating work platforms must receive comprehensive training. This training will cover safe operating procedures, hazard recognition, fall protection, and emergency procedures. Training must be conducted by a competent person and documented. Retraining is required every three years or when new equipment is introduced, or unsafe operation is observed. (Per 29 CFR 1926.453(b)(2) and WAC 296-880-200)

7. INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

MH Construction will conduct regular scheduled and unscheduled inspections of aerial lifts and elevating work platforms, as well as work practices. Supervisors are responsible for daily oversight. Non-compliance with this policy will result in immediate corrective action, which may include retraining, disciplinary action, or removal from duty. (Per AGC Safety and Health Manual Ch. 14)

8. RECORDKEEPING

Records of operator training, equipment inspections, maintenance, and repair must be maintained for the lifespan of the equipment or as required by regulatory standards. These records will be kept by the Safety Officer at the main office. (Per 29 CFR 1926.453 and WAC 296-880)

9. VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

Violations of this policy will be subject to MH Construction's progressive disciplinary policy. Depending on the severity and frequency of the violation, consequences may range from verbal warnings and retraining to suspension or termination of employment. Subcontractors found in violation may face contract termination.

10. REFERENCES

  • 29 CFR 1926.453 Aerial Lifts
  • 29 CFR 1926 Subpart L Scaffolds for scissor lifts
  • ANSI/SIA A92 Series
  • WAC 296-880 Unified Safety Standards for Fall Protection AWPs
  • WAC 296-155-53400 Cranes General Requirements for boom lifts
  • Safety and Health Manual Ch. 14 Aerial Work Platforms

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 21 | Crane Suspended Work Platforms Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey ―――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――― Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart CC OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 21 | Crane Suspended Work Platforms

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926 Subpart CC

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.1431

Hoisting Personnel

WISHA

WAC 296-155-547

Personnel Lifting Platforms

WISHA

Design and Installation Requirements

Lifting Personnel

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 15 Cranes and Rigging

PURPOSE

This policy establishes guidelines for the safe use of crane suspended work platforms to protect personnel from fall hazards and other risks associated with elevated work. It ensures compliance with applicable federal and state regulations, minimizing the potential for accidents and injuries on MH Construction job sites.

SCOPE

This policy applies to all MH Construction employees, subcontractors, and visitors involved in or exposed to operations utilizing crane suspended work platforms on MH Construction job sites.

DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Crane Suspended Work Platform

A platform designed to be suspended from a crane and used to hoist personnel to an elevated work position.

Competent Person

One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Qualified Person

One who, by possession of a recognized degree, certificate, or professional standing, or who by extensive knowledge, training, and experience, has successfully demonstrated the ability to solve or resolve problems relating to the subject matter, the work, or the project.

Fall Protection System

Equipment and procedures designed to prevent workers from falling from elevated work surfaces, including guardrails, safety nets, and personal fall arrest systems.

Load Capacity

The maximum weight that a crane or work platform is designed to safely lift or support.

RESPONSIBILITIES

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Provide safe equipment and resources for crane suspended work platform operations.
  • Ensure all personnel involved in crane suspended work platform operations are adequately trained and qualified.
  • Establish and enforce clear safety policies and procedures.
  • Conduct regular safety audits and inspections.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Ensure all crane suspended work platform operations are conducted in accordance with this policy and regulatory requirements.
  • Verify that all personnel are trained, competent, and authorized for their assigned tasks.
  • Conduct pre-shift inspections of equipment and work areas.
  • Stop any unsafe work practices immediately.

Employee Responsibilities

  • Adhere to all safety policies, procedures, and instructions related to crane suspended work platforms.
  • Use all provided safety equipment correctly, including personal fall arrest systems.
  • Report any unsafe conditions, equipment defects, or hazards to their supervisor immediately.
  • Participate in all required training programs.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Comply with all MH Construction safety policies and procedures, as well as all applicable OSHA and WISHA regulations.
  • Ensure their employees are properly trained and equipped for crane suspended work platform operations.
  • Coordinate all crane suspended work platform activities with MH Construction site management.

PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

7.1 General Requirements

All crane suspended work platform operations must be planned and supervised by a competent person. The crane and platform must be inspected prior to each use. Per 29 CFR 1926.1431(e)(1), employees must be instructed on the hazards of the work and the procedures to be followed.

7.2 Crane and Platform Inspection

Before each shift and prior to lifting personnel, a competent person must inspect the crane and the work platform. This inspection must include, but is not limited to, checking for structural integrity, proper functioning of controls, wire rope condition, and all safety devices. Per WAC 296-155-551, detailed inspection records must be maintained.

7.3 Fall Protection

Each employee on a crane suspended work platform must be protected by a personal fall arrest system. The system must be attached to an anchorage point on the platform or to the crane's load line above the headache ball, ensuring that the lanyard length limits free fall to no more than 6 feet. Per 29 CFR 1926.1431(g)(2), fall arrest systems must meet the requirements of 29 CFR 1926.502.

7.4 Platform Design and Construction

Work platforms must be designed by a qualified person and constructed to safely support the intended load, including personnel, tools, and materials. They must have a minimum design factor of four for the platform and its suspension system. Guardrails, mid-rails, and toe boards are required. Per WAC 296-155-548, specific design and construction criteria must be met.

7.5 Hoisting Personnel

Personnel hoisting with a crane suspended work platform must only be performed when no other safe means of access is feasible. The crane operator must be qualified and experienced in hoisting personnel. The platform must be hoisted smoothly and slowly, without sudden movements. Per 29 CFR 1926.1431(e)(2), a trial lift with the unoccupied platform must be conducted immediately prior to hoisting personnel.

7.6 Communication

Continuous communication must be maintained between the crane operator and a designated signal person, as well as with personnel on the platform. Two-way radio communication is preferred. Hand signals must conform to ASME B30.5. Per WAC 296-155-553(1)(c), a reliable means of communication is mandatory.

7.7 Weather Conditions

Crane suspended work platform operations must be suspended during adverse weather conditions, such as high winds, heavy rain, lightning, or any other condition that could endanger personnel. A competent person must make the determination to cease operations.

7.8 Emergency Procedures

Emergency rescue procedures must be established and reviewed with all personnel prior to commencing operations. This includes plans for retrieving personnel from a stalled platform or in the event of a crane malfunction. Rescue equipment must be readily available.

TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

All employees involved in crane suspended work platform operations, including operators, signal persons, and personnel working on the platform, must receive comprehensive training. This training must cover the hazards associated with the work, safe operating procedures, fall protection, emergency procedures, and their specific responsibilities. Training must be conducted by a qualified person and documented. Retraining is required when there are changes in equipment, procedures, or if an employee demonstrates a lack of proficiency. Per 29 CFR 1926.1431(c)(1), operators must be trained and evaluated.

INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

MH Construction management and supervisors will conduct regular, unannounced inspections of crane suspended work platform operations to ensure compliance with this policy and all applicable regulations. Any observed non-compliance will result in immediate corrective action. Repeated or severe violations may lead to disciplinary action as outlined in the 'Violation Consequences' section.

RECORDKEEPING

The following records must be maintained for a minimum of three (3) years, or as required by specific regulations:

  • Crane and platform inspection logs (pre-shift, monthly, annual)
  • Personnel training records (dates, topics, attendees, trainer qualifications)
  • Maintenance and repair records for cranes and platforms
  • Documentation of trial lifts and pre-lift planning
  • Incident/accident reports related to crane suspended work platforms

The Safety Officer, Matt Ramsey, is responsible for ensuring proper recordkeeping.

VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

Violations of this Crane Suspended Work Platforms policy will be subject to MH Construction's progressive disciplinary policy, which may include, but is not limited to, verbal warnings, written warnings, suspension, and termination of employment or contract. Severe violations or those resulting in serious injury or property damage may result in immediate termination and/or legal action.

REFERENCES

WISHA: WAC 296-155-547 Personnel Lifting Platforms

WISHA: WAC 296-155-548 Design and Installation Requirements

WISHA: WAC 296-155-551 Inspections Maintenance and Testing

sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 22 Use and Handling of Scaffolds Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey ―――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――― REGULATORY AUTHORITY Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart L OSHA 29 CFR 1926.

MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 22 Use and Handling of Scaffolds

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――

REGULATORY AUTHORITY

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926 Subpart L

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.450 Scope

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.451 General Requirements

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.452 Additional Requirements

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.454 Training Requirements

WISHA

WAC 296-869 Scaffolds

replaces WAC 296-155 Part J-1

AGC

Safety and Health Manual

Ch. 16 Scaffolding

PURPOSE

This policy establishes guidelines for the safe use and handling of scaffolds to prevent falls, structural collapses, and other related hazards. It aims to protect all personnel on MH Construction job sites from injuries associated with scaffold erection, dismantling, and use, ensuring compliance with regulatory standards.

SCOPE

This policy applies to all MH Construction employees, subcontractors, and visitors who may erect, dismantle, inspect, or work on or near scaffolds on any MH Construction job site.

DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Competent Person

An individual designated by the employer who is capable of identifying existing and foreseeable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Scaffold

Any temporary elevated platform (supported or suspended) and its supporting structure (including points of anchorage), used to support employees or materials.

Guardrail System

A barrier erected to prevent employees from falling to lower levels.

Toeboard

A low protective barrier erected along the edge of a walking-working surface to prevent the fall of materials and equipment.

Planking

The scaffold platforms, decks, or work surfaces.

RESPONSIBILITIES

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • • Provide a safe work environment and ensure all scaffold operations comply with OSHA and WISHA regulations.
  • • Ensure that only qualified and trained personnel are involved in scaffold erection, dismantling, and inspection.
  • • Provide necessary resources, including appropriate equipment and personal protective equipment (PPE).

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • • Designate a Competent Person for all scaffold-related activities.
  • • Ensure daily inspections of scaffolds are conducted by a Competent Person before each shift and after any event that could affect structural integrity.
  • • Verify that all employees working on or near scaffolds have received adequate training.
  • • Implement corrective actions promptly for any identified scaffold hazards.

Employee Responsibilities

  • • Adhere to all scaffold safety procedures and instructions provided by the Competent Person or supervisor.
  • • Inspect scaffolds before use and report any defects or unsafe conditions immediately.
  • • Use appropriate fall protection and other PPE as required.
  • • Never alter or modify scaffolds without authorization from a Competent Person.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • • Ensure their employees comply with all MH Construction scaffold safety policies and applicable OSHA/WISHA regulations.
  • • Provide proof of Competent Person training and scaffold inspection records upon request.
  • • Coordinate all scaffold erection, modification, and dismantling activities with the MH Construction site supervisor.

PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

7.1 General Requirements for All Scaffolds

All scaffolds must be designed, erected, and maintained in accordance with 29 CFR 1926.451 and WAC 296-869. A Competent Person must supervise all scaffold work. Scaffolds must be capable of supporting, without failure, their own weight and at least four times the maximum intended load. Scaffold components must be inspected for visible defects before each use.

7.2 Fall Protection

Each employee on a scaffold more than 10 feet (3.1 m) above a lower level must be protected from falling to that lower level. This protection must be provided by the use of guardrail systems, personal fall arrest systems, or both. Guardrail systems must be installed along all open sides and ends of platforms per 29 CFR 1926.451(g)(4) and WAC 296-869-20040.

7.3 Falling Object Protection

Each employee on a scaffold must be provided with additional protection from falling hand tools, debris, and other small objects through the installation of toeboards, screens, or guardrail systems, or by erecting debris nets, or by implementing a canopy structure, as specified in 29 CFR 1926.451(h) and WAC 296-869-20045.

7.4 Scaffold Access

Safe means of access must be provided for each employee accessing a scaffold. This includes ladders, stair towers, or ramps. Direct access from another surface is permitted only when the scaffold is not more than 14 inches (36 cm) horizontally and not more than 24 inches (61 cm) vertically from the other surface, as per 29 CFR 1926.451(e) and WAC 296-869-20035.

7.5 Scaffold Planking and Decking

Each scaffold platform must be fully planked or decked between the front uprights and the guardrail supports. The space between adjacent planks must not exceed 1 inch (2.5 cm). Planks must extend over their end supports by at least 6 inches but not more than 12 inches, unless cleated or restrained by other means, in accordance with 29 CFR 1926.451(b) and WAC 296-869-20025.

7.6 Electrical Hazards

Scaffolds must not be erected, used, dismantled, altered, or moved such that they or any conductive material handled on them might come closer to exposed energized power lines than the minimum approach distances specified in 29 CFR 1926.451(f)(6) and WAC 296-869-20050.

7.7 Supported Scaffolds

Supported scaffolds must be braced by cross, longitudinal, or diagonal braces, or a combination thereof, to prevent racking or collapse of the scaffold. The poles, legs, or uprights of supported scaffolds must be plumb and braced to prevent swaying and displacement. Base plates and mud sills must be used on firm foundations.

7.8 Suspended Scaffolds

Suspended scaffolds must be supported by outriggers, davits, or other equivalent means. All support devices must be inspected by a Competent Person before each shift. Wire ropes must be inspected for kinks, broken wires, corrosion, and other damage. Only one employee is permitted on a single-point adjustable suspended scaffold.

TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

All employees who work on a scaffold must be trained by a Competent Person in the hazards associated with the type of scaffold being used and the procedures to control or minimize those hazards. Training must cover topics such as the nature of electrical hazards, fall hazards, falling object hazards, proper use of the scaffold, and handling of materials. Retraining is required when there are changes in the workplace or scaffold type, or if an employee's performance indicates a lack of understanding, as per 29 CFR 1926.454 and WAC 296-869-20060.

INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

Scaffolds must be inspected by a Competent Person before each work shift and after any occurrence that could affect their structural integrity. Any scaffold found to be unsafe must be immediately tagged 'DO NOT USE' and removed from service until repaired. MH Construction supervisors will conduct regular site inspections to ensure compliance with this policy. Non-compliance will result in corrective actions as outlined in the Violation Consequences section.

RECORDKEEPING

Records of all scaffold inspections, maintenance, and training must be maintained by the Safety Officer for a minimum of three (3) years. These records include daily inspection logs, training rosters, and documentation of any incidents or corrective actions related to scaffold use.

VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

Violations of this scaffold safety policy will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and frequency of the violation, and the potential for harm. Immediate removal from scaffold-related duties may occur for serious infractions.

REFERENCES

  • • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926 Subpart L -
  • • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.450 Scope -
  • • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.451 General Requirements -
  • • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.452 Additional Requirements -
  • • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.454 Training Requirements -
  • • WISHA: WAC 296-869 Scaffolds - replaces WAC 296-155 Part J-1
  • • AGC: Safety and Health Manual - Ch. 16 Scaffolding

MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

Occupational Noise Exposure

Lighting

WISHA

WAC 296-62

General Occupational Health Standards

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 17

Industrial Hygiene

1. Purpose

The purpose of the MH Construction, Inc. (MHC) Industrial Hygiene Program is to anticipate, recognize, evaluate, and control environmental factors or stresses arising in or from the workplace that may cause sickness, impaired health and well-being, or significant discomfort among workers or citizens of the community. This policy establishes the framework for protecting employees from occupational health hazards, including chemical, physical, biological, and ergonomic stressors, ensuring a safe and healthful work environment.

2. Scope

This program applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, vendors, and visitors present on any MHC job site, facility, or property. It encompasses all operations and activities where potential exposure to industrial hygiene hazards exists.

3. Definitions

Term

Definition

Industrial Hygiene

The science and art devoted to the anticipation, recognition, evaluation, and control of those environmental factors or stresses arising in or from the workplace, which may cause sickness, impaired health and well-being, or significant discomfort among workers or among the citizens of the community.

Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL)

The maximum legal limit for exposure of an employee to a chemical substance or physical agent, as established by OSHA or WISHA.

Threshold Limit Value (TLV)

Airborne concentrations of chemical substances and represent conditions under which it is believed that nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed, day after day, over a working lifetime, without adverse health effects.

Action Level (AL)

An airborne concentration of a specific substance calculated as an 8-hour time-weighted average, which initiates certain required activities such as exposure monitoring and medical surveillance.

Time-Weighted Average (TWA)

The employee's average airborne exposure in any 8-hour work shift of a 40-hour work week which shall not be exceeded.

4. Responsibilities

4.1 Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Provide the necessary resources, including funding, equipment, and personnel, to implement and maintain the Industrial Hygiene Program.
  • Ensure that all operations comply with applicable OSHA and WISHA industrial hygiene standards.
  • Designate a qualified Safety Officer to oversee the program and coordinate industrial hygiene assessments.

4.2 Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Ensure that employees under their supervision are trained in and comply with the requirements of this program.
  • Identify potential health hazards in their work areas and notify the Safety Officer.
  • Enforce the use of required personal protective equipment (PPE) and engineering controls.

4.3 Employee Responsibilities

  • Comply with all procedures and requirements outlined in this Industrial Hygiene Program.
  • Properly use and maintain assigned PPE and engineering controls.
  • Report any suspected health hazards, symptoms of exposure, or defective control equipment to their supervisor immediately.

4.4 Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Comply with all MHC Industrial Hygiene Program requirements, as well as applicable OSHA and WISHA regulations.
  • Provide their own hazard assessments, monitoring, and PPE for their employees, unless otherwise specified in the contract.
  • Coordinate with MHC management regarding any activities that may introduce new health hazards to the job site.

5. Procedures / Requirements

5.1 Hazard Anticipation and Recognition

Prior to the commencement of any new project or operation, the Safety Officer and project management team shall conduct a comprehensive review to anticipate and recognize potential industrial hygiene hazards. This includes reviewing Safety Data Sheets (SDS) for all chemicals to be used, evaluating planned work processes for physical hazards (e.g., noise, vibration), and assessing the work environment for biological or ergonomic stressors. Per WAC 296-62, a hazard assessment must be documented and maintained.

5.2 Exposure Evaluation and Monitoring

When potential exposures to chemical or physical agents are identified, the Safety Officer shall determine the need for exposure monitoring. Initial monitoring shall be conducted to determine if employee exposures exceed the Action Level (AL) or Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL). Per 29 CFR 1910.1000 and WAC 296-155-160, employee exposure to airborne contaminants shall not exceed the specified limits. If initial monitoring indicates exposures above the AL, periodic monitoring shall be implemented in accordance with specific substance standards.

5.3 Hierarchy of Controls

MHC shall implement controls to eliminate or reduce occupational exposures to acceptable levels, following the hierarchy of controls:

  1. Elimination/Substitution: Removing the hazard or substituting a less hazardous material or process.
  2. Engineering Controls: Implementing physical changes to the workplace, such as local exhaust ventilation (per WAC 296-155-170), isolation, or enclosure of the hazard.
  3. Administrative Controls: Modifying work schedules or practices to reduce exposure duration.
  4. Personal Protective Equipment (PPE): Providing and requiring the use of appropriate respirators, hearing protection, or protective clothing when other controls are infeasible or insufficient.

5.4 Respiratory Protection

When engineering and administrative controls are inadequate to maintain exposures below the PEL for gases, vapors, fumes, dusts, and mists (per 29 CFR 1926.55 and WAC 296-155-160), respiratory protection shall be provided and used in accordance with the MHC Respiratory Protection Program. This includes medical evaluations, fit testing, and training for all affected employees.

5.5 Hearing Conservation

Employees exposed to noise levels at or above an 8-hour TWA of 85 decibels (dBA) shall be included in a Hearing Conservation Program, per WAC 296-155-145. This program includes baseline and annual audiometric testing, provision of hearing protection devices, and annual training. Noise exposure monitoring shall be conducted whenever changes in production, process, or controls increase noise exposures.

5.6 Illumination and Lighting

Adequate lighting shall be provided in all work areas to ensure safe working conditions and prevent eye strain or accidents. Per WAC 296-155-165, minimum illumination intensities shall be maintained for specific work areas and tasks. The Safety Officer shall conduct periodic light meter surveys to verify compliance with these standards.

5.7 Ventilation Systems

Where hazardous dusts, fumes, vapors, or gases are generated, local exhaust ventilation systems shall be designed, installed, and maintained to prevent accumulation of toxic or flammable concentrations. Per WAC 296-155-170, ventilation systems shall be inspected regularly to ensure adequate airflow and capture velocity. Employees shall not enter confined spaces or areas with inadequate ventilation without proper testing and authorization.

5.8 Medical Surveillance

Medical surveillance shall be provided for employees exposed to specific hazardous substances at or above the Action Level, or as required by specific OSHA/WISHA standards (e.g., lead, asbestos, silica). Medical examinations shall be performed by a licensed health care professional at no cost to the employee, and at a reasonable time and place.

6. Training Requirements

All employees potentially exposed to industrial hygiene hazards shall receive comprehensive training prior to initial assignment and at least annually thereafter. Training shall include:

  • The nature of the hazards (chemical, physical, biological) present in their work area.
  • The specific OSHA and WISHA standards applicable to their exposures.
  • Methods and observations used to detect the presence or release of hazardous agents.
  • The physical and health hazards of the substances or agents.
  • Measures employees can take to protect themselves, including specific procedures, engineering controls, work practices, and PPE.

All training shall be documented, including the dates of training, content covered, and names/signatures of attendees and instructors. Records shall be maintained by the Safety Officer.

7. Inspection and Enforcement

The Safety Officer and site supervisors shall conduct regular, documented inspections of work areas to ensure compliance with this Industrial Hygiene Program. Inspections shall verify the proper operation of engineering controls, correct use of PPE, and adherence to safe work practices. Any identified deficiencies or hazards shall be corrected immediately. The Safety Officer has the authority to halt any operation deemed to pose an imminent danger to employee health.

8. Recordkeeping

MHC shall maintain accurate records of all industrial hygiene activities, including:

  • Exposure monitoring results: Maintained for at least 30 years.
  • Medical surveillance records: Maintained for the duration of employment plus 30 years.
  • Training records: Maintained for at least 3 years from the date of training.
  • Hazard assessments and inspection reports: Maintained for at least 5 years.

The Safety Officer is responsible for the secure maintenance and retention of these records. Employees or their designated representatives have the right to access their exposure and medical records upon request.

9. Violation Consequences

Compliance with the Industrial Hygiene Program is a condition of employment. Violations of these procedures, including failure to use required PPE or bypassing engineering controls, will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment. Subcontractors found in violation may face work stoppage, financial penalties, or termination of their contract.

10. References

  • OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1000 - Air Contaminants
  • OSHA 29 CFR 1926.55 - Gases Vapors Fumes Dusts and Mists
  • WISHA WAC 296-155-160 - Gases Vapors Fumes Dusts and Mists
  • WISHA WAC 296-155-145 - Occupational Noise Exposure
  • WISHA WAC 296-155-165 - Lighting
  • WISHA WAC 296-155-170 - Ventilation
  • WISHA WAC 296-62 - General Occupational Health Standards
  • AGC Safety and Health Manual Ch. 17 - Industrial Hygiene

sections.title: contractor hazard communication program
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 24 | Contractor Hazard Communication Program Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 1.  REGULATORY AUTHORITY Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926. 59 Hazard Communication OSHA 29 CFR 1910.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 24 | Contractor Hazard Communication Program

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. REGULATORY AUTHORITY

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.59

Hazard Communication

OSHA

29 CFR 1910.1200

Hazard Communication Standard GHS

WISHA

WAC 296-155-180

Hazard Communication

WISHA

WAC 296-901

Hazard Communication GHS

AGC

Ch. 18

HazCom/GHS

2. PURPOSE

This program establishes guidelines for effective hazard communication to ensure that all employees are informed about chemical hazards in the workplace. It aims to prevent injuries, illnesses, and fatalities resulting from exposure to hazardous substances by providing necessary information and training.

3. SCOPE

This program applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors who may be exposed to hazardous chemicals on any MH Construction job site or facility.

4. DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Hazardous Chemical

Any chemical which is classified as a physical hazard or a health hazard, a simple asphyxiant, combustible dust, pyrophoric gas, or hazard not otherwise classified.

Safety Data Sheet (SDS)

Written or printed material concerning a hazardous chemical that is prepared in accordance with 29 CFR 1910.1200(g) and WAC 296-901.

Label

An appropriate group of written, printed or graphic information elements concerning a hazardous chemical that is affixed to, printed on, or attached to the container of a hazardous chemical.

GHS

Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labeling of Chemicals, an international system for standardizing chemical hazard information.

Exposure

That an employee is subjected to a hazardous chemical in the course of employment through any route of entry (inhalation, ingestion, skin contact or absorption), and includes potential (e.g., accidental or possible) exposure.

5. RESPONSIBILITIES

5.1 Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Establish and maintain a comprehensive Hazard Communication Program.
  • Ensure adequate resources are available for program implementation, including training and SDS management.
  • Review and update the program as necessary to reflect changes in regulations or workplace chemicals.

5.2 Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Implement the Hazard Communication Program in their respective work areas.
  • Ensure all employees under their supervision receive appropriate training on hazardous chemicals.
  • Verify that all hazardous chemical containers are properly labeled and Safety Data Sheets (SDS) are accessible.
  • Conduct regular inspections to ensure compliance with HazCom procedures.

5.3 Employee Responsibilities

  • Attend and actively participate in all required hazard communication training.
  • Read and understand labels and SDSs for chemicals they work with.
  • Use appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) as required.
  • Report any unlabeled containers or missing SDSs to their supervisor immediately.

5.4 Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Provide MH Construction with a copy of their Hazard Communication Program and SDSs for all chemicals they bring onto the job site.
  • Ensure their employees are trained on the hazards of chemicals they use and are aware of MH Construction's HazCom program.
  • Comply with all aspects of MH Construction's Hazard Communication Program while on site.

6. PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

6.1 Chemical Inventory

  1. 6.1.1 MH Construction will maintain a comprehensive inventory of all hazardous chemicals present on job sites and in facilities. This inventory will be updated annually or whenever new hazardous chemicals are introduced. (Per 29 CFR 1910.1200(e)(1) and WAC 296-901-14006)

6.2 Safety Data Sheets (SDSs)

  1. 6.2.1 SDSs will be maintained for every hazardous chemical on site and readily accessible to all employees during their work shifts. Electronic access is permissible, provided there are no barriers to access. (Per 29 CFR 1910.1200(g)(1) and WAC 296-901-14014)
  2. 6.2.2 SDSs will be reviewed by the Safety Officer or designated personnel upon receipt to ensure completeness and accuracy.

6.3 Labeling

  1. 6.3.1 All containers of hazardous chemicals must be labeled, tagged, or marked with the identity of the hazardous chemical, appropriate hazard warnings, and the name and address of the chemical manufacturer, importer, or other responsible party. (Per 29 CFR 1910.1200(f)(1) and WAC 296-901-14012)
  2. 6.3.2 Workplace labels must be legible, in English, and prominently displayed. Employees are prohibited from removing or defacing labels.

6.4 Non-Routine Tasks

  1. 6.4.1 Before employees perform non-routine tasks involving hazardous chemicals, they will be informed of the specific hazards, protective measures, and emergency procedures. (Per 29 CFR 1910.1200(f)(4) and WAC 296-901-14012)

6.5 Multi-Employer Workplaces

  1. 6.5.1 MH Construction will exchange information on hazardous chemicals with other employers on multi-employer worksites. This includes providing SDSs, explaining precautionary measures, and outlining the labeling system used. (Per 29 CFR 1926.59(d) and WAC 296-155-180(4))

6.6 Contractor Responsibilities

  1. 6.6.1 Contractors working on MH Construction sites must comply with all aspects of this Hazard Communication Program. They are responsible for providing their employees with appropriate training and PPE, and for ensuring proper labeling and SDS accessibility for chemicals they bring on site.

7. TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

All employees who work with or are potentially exposed to hazardous chemicals will receive initial training and refresher training as needed. (Per 29 CFR 1910.1200(h) and WAC 296-901-14016)

  • Training will cover:
  • The requirements of the Hazard Communication Standard.
  • Any operations in their work area where hazardous chemicals are present.
  • The location and availability of the written Hazard Communication Program, including the list of hazardous chemicals and SDSs.
  • How to detect the presence or release of a hazardous chemical in the work area.
  • The physical and health hazards of the chemicals in the work area.
  • The measures employees can take to protect themselves from these hazards, including specific procedures, work practices, and personal protective equipment.
  • The details of the hazard communication program developed by MH Construction, including an explanation of the labeling system and the SDS format.
  • Training records will be maintained by the Safety Officer.

8. INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

The Safety Officer will conduct periodic inspections to ensure compliance with this program, including proper labeling, SDS accessibility, and employee adherence to safe work practices. Any deficiencies found will be documented, and corrective actions will be implemented promptly. Non-compliance may result in disciplinary action as outlined in Section 10.

9. RECORDKEEPING

  • The following records will be maintained by the Safety Officer:
  • Chemical inventory list (current and historical).
  • Safety Data Sheets (SDSs) for all hazardous chemicals (current and historical).
  • Hazard communication training records, including dates, attendees, and topics covered.
  • Inspection reports and corrective actions.
  • Records will be retained for a minimum of 30 years for training and medical records, and indefinitely for chemical inventories and SDSs.

10. VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

Violations of this Hazard Communication Program will be subject to MH Construction's progressive disciplinary policy, which may include verbal warnings, written warnings, suspension, and termination of employment. Subcontractor non-compliance may result in removal from the job site and termination of contract.

11. REFERENCES

OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.59 Hazard Communication

OSHA: 29 CFR 1910.1200 Hazard Communication Standard GHS

WISHA: WAC 296-155-180 Hazard Communication

WISHA: WAC 296-901 Hazard Communication GHS

AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 18 HazCom/GHS

sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.
