sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 25 | Heat Related Illness Prevention

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey


――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.20

OSHA

OSH Act General Duty Clause Section 5(a)(1)

WISHA

WAC 296-155-110 Accident Prevention Program

WISHA

WAC 296-62-095 Heat Exposure

AGC

19

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 19 Heat Illness Prevention


Purpose

This policy establishes guidelines and procedures to prevent heat-related illnesses among all personnel working on MH Construction job sites. It aims to protect employees, subcontractors, and visitors from the dangers of excessive heat exposure, ensuring a safe and healthy work environment as mandated by regulatory standards.

Scope

This program applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors present on any MH Construction job site where there is a potential for heat exposure.

Definitions

Term

Definition

Heat Stress

The total load of heat on the body from both internal (metabolic) and external (environmental) sources.

Heat Stroke

The most serious heat-related illness, occurring when the body loses its ability to cool itself, leading to a rapid rise in core body temperature.

Heat Exhaustion

A milder form of heat-related illness resulting from the body's loss of water and salt, often characterized by heavy sweating, weakness, and nausea.

Acclimatization

The physiological adaptation of the body to a new climate or environment, particularly one with higher temperatures.

Work/Rest Cycle

Scheduled periods of work followed by periods of rest in a cooler environment to prevent heat stress.


Responsibilities

Company/Management:

  • Provide resources for heat illness prevention, including water, shade, and training.
  • Establish and maintain a comprehensive Heat Related Illness Prevention Program.
  • Ensure compliance with all applicable OSHA, WISHA, and AGC heat illness prevention standards.

Supervisor/Foreman:

  • Implement and enforce the Heat Related Illness Prevention Program on job sites.
  • Monitor weather conditions and adjust work schedules, including work/rest cycles, as needed.
  • Ensure adequate water and shade are available and utilized by all personnel.
  • Train employees on heat illness symptoms, prevention, and emergency procedures.

Employee:

  • Adhere to all heat illness prevention policies and procedures.
  • Drink water frequently, take rest breaks in shade, and report any symptoms of heat illness immediately.
  • Wear appropriate clothing for hot weather conditions.

Subcontractor:

  • Comply with MH Construction's Heat Related Illness Prevention Program and all applicable regulations.
  • Ensure their employees are trained and follow safe work practices to prevent heat illness.

Procedures / Requirements

7.1 Hydration

Adequate hydration is critical. Potable drinking water must be provided free of charge and readily accessible to all employees at all times. Employees are encouraged to drink small amounts of water frequently (e.g., 4-8 ounces every 15-20 minutes) to prevent dehydration. Per WISHA WAC 296-62-095(3)(a), employers must ensure that employees have access to an adequate supply of drinking water.

7.2 Shade and Rest Breaks

When the ambient temperature exceeds 80°F (26.7°C), access to shade must be provided. Employees must be permitted and encouraged to take preventative cool-down rest periods in the shade for a minimum of 10 minutes every two hours. During these breaks, employees should not perform work and should rehydrate. Per WISHA WAC 296-62-095(3)(b), employers must provide shade when the outdoor temperature is 80°F or higher.

7.3 Acclimatization

New employees or those returning from an absence of more than two weeks must undergo a period of acclimatization. This involves gradually increasing work intensity and duration over 7-14 days, with closer supervision and more frequent breaks during the initial days. This helps the body adapt to working in hot environments and reduces the risk of heat illness. Per OSH Act General Duty Clause Section 5(a)(1), employers must provide a workplace free from recognized hazards likely to cause death or serious physical harm.

7.4 Monitoring Weather Conditions

Supervisors must monitor weather forecasts and current conditions, including temperature, humidity, and heat index. Work activities will be adjusted based on these conditions, potentially including rescheduling tasks, increasing rest breaks, or ceasing non-essential work during peak heat hours.

7.5 Emergency Response Procedures

All personnel must be aware of the symptoms of heat-related illnesses and the appropriate first aid response. In case of a suspected heat stroke or severe heat exhaustion, emergency medical services (911) must be contacted immediately. The affected individual should be moved to a cooler area, loosened clothing, and cooled with water or ice packs while awaiting medical help. Per 29 CFR 1926.20, employers are responsible for initiating and maintaining accident prevention programs.

7.6 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) and Clothing

Employees are encouraged to wear light-colored, loose-fitting, breathable clothing. Sun hats and sunglasses are also recommended. Supervisors will ensure that any required PPE does not exacerbate heat stress and will consider alternatives or additional controls if necessary.

7.7 Communication and Observation

Supervisors and employees are responsible for observing co-workers for signs of heat illness. A buddy system is encouraged, especially during high heat conditions or when working alone. Open communication about how individuals are feeling is essential for early detection and intervention.

Training Requirements

All employees and supervisors who may be exposed to heat stress must receive training on heat illness prevention. This training will cover: recognition of heat illness symptoms, first aid procedures, importance of hydration, proper work/rest cycles, and emergency response. Training will be conducted annually and documented. Per WISHA WAC 296-155-110, employers must have an accident prevention program that includes safety training.

Inspection and Enforcement

Supervisors will conduct regular inspections of job sites to ensure compliance with this program, including availability of water and shade, and proper implementation of work/rest cycles. Non-compliance will result in immediate corrective action and may lead to disciplinary measures as outlined in the 'Violation Consequences' section.

Recordkeeping

Records of heat illness training, incident reports, and corrective actions will be maintained by the Safety Officer for a minimum of five years. These records are essential for demonstrating compliance and for continuous improvement of the program.

Violation Consequences

Failure to comply with the Heat Related Illness Prevention Program may result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and frequency of the violation, and its potential impact on safety.

References

OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.20 - Safety and Health Regulations for Construction

OSHA: OSH Act General Duty Clause Section 5(a)(1)

WISHA: WAC 296-155-110 - Accident Prevention Program

WISHA: WAC 296-62-095 - Heat Exposure

AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 19 Heat Illness Prevention

sections.title: excavation trenching and shoring
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 26 | Excavation, Trenching, and Shoring Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 1.  Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart P OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.
Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)
MISH 26 | Excavation, Trenching, and Shoring
Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1926 Subpart P

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.650 Scope

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.651 Specific Excavation Requirements

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.652 Requirements for Protective Systems

WISHA

WAC 296-155-650

Scope and Application

WISHA

WAC 296-155-652

Definitions

WISHA

WAC 296-155-655

General Protection Requirements

WISHA

WAC 296-155-657

Requirements for Protective Systems

WISHA

WAC 296-155-664

Appendices A-F

AGC

AGC: Safety and Health

Manual Ch. 20 Excavation and Trenching

2. Purpose

This policy establishes the minimum safety requirements for excavation, trenching, and shoring operations to prevent injuries, fatalities, and property damage. It ensures compliance with all applicable federal and state regulations, protecting all personnel involved in these high-risk activities.

3. Scope

This program applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors involved in or present at excavation, trenching, and shoring operations on MH Construction job sites.

4. Definitions

Term

Definition

Competent Person

An individual designated by the employer who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Excavation

Any man-made cut, cavity, trench, or depression in an earth surface, formed by earth removal.

Trench

A narrow excavation (in relation to its length) made below the surface of the ground. In general, the depth is greater than the width, but the width of a trench (measured at the bottom) is not greater than 15 feet (4.6 m).

Shoring

A protective system that uses aluminum hydraulic or other types of supports to prevent soil collapse.

Sloping

A method of protecting employees from cave-ins by excavating the sides of an excavation to form one or a series of inclined surfaces and can include benching.

5. Responsibilities

5.1 Company/Management

  • Provide necessary resources and training for safe excavation, trenching, and shoring operations.
  • Ensure compliance with all federal, state, and local regulations.
  • Designate a Competent Person for all excavation sites.

5.2 Supervisor/Foreman

  • Implement and enforce this program on job sites.
  • Ensure daily inspections of excavations by a Competent Person.
  • Stop work immediately if hazardous conditions are identified.

5.3 Employees

  • Adhere to all safety procedures and instructions.
  • Report any unsafe conditions or practices to their supervisor.
  • Use appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE).

5.4 Subcontractors

  • Comply with MH Construction, Inc. safety policies and all applicable regulations.
  • Provide their employees with necessary training and PPE.
  • Coordinate excavation activities with MH Construction, Inc. supervisors.

6. Procedures / Requirements

6.1 General Requirements

  1. All excavations 5 feet or deeper must be protected against cave-ins by sloping, shoring, or shielding. (Per 29 CFR 1926.652 and WAC 296-155-657)
  2. A Competent Person must inspect excavations daily and after any rainstorm or other hazard-increasing occurrence. (Per 29 CFR 1926.651(k)(1) and WAC 296-155-655(1))
  3. Access and egress must be provided for excavations 4 feet or more in depth. Ramps, ladders, or stairs must be within 25 feet of employees. (Per 29 CFR 1926.651(c)(2) and WAC 296-155-655(5))

6.2 Underground Installations

  1. The estimated location of utility installations, such as sewer, telephone, fuel, electric, water, or other underground installations, must be determined prior to opening an excavation. (Per 29 CFR 1926.651(b)(1) and WAC 296-155-655(2))
  2. When excavation approaches the estimated location of underground installations, the exact location must be determined by safe and acceptable means. (Per 29 CFR 1926.651(b)(2) and WAC 296-155-655(2))

6.3 Exposure to Vehicular Traffic

  1. Employees exposed to vehicular traffic must be provided with, and must wear, warning vests or other suitable garments marked with or made of reflective or high-visibility material. (Per 29 CFR 1926.651(d) and WAC 296-155-655(3))

6.4 Protection from Falling Objects

  1. Employees must be protected from excavated or other materials or equipment that could fall or roll into excavations. Protection must be provided by placing and keeping such materials or equipment at least 2 feet from the edge of excavations, or by the use of retaining devices. (Per 29 CFR 1926.651(j)(2) and WAC 296-155-655(10))

6.5 Water Accumulation

  1. Employees are not permitted to work in excavations where there is accumulated water, or in excavations in which water is accumulating, unless precautions have been taken to protect employees from the hazards posed by water accumulation. (Per 29 CFR 1926.651(h)(1) and WAC 296-155-655(11))

6.6 Hazardous Atmospheres

  1. Where oxygen deficiency (less than 19.5 percent) or a hazardous atmosphere exists or could reasonably be expected to exist, such as in excavations in landfill areas or excavations in areas where hazardous substances are stored nearby, the atmospheres in the excavation must be tested before employees enter excavations greater than 4 feet in depth. (Per 29 CFR 1926.651(g)(1) and WAC 296-155-655(12))

7. Training Requirements

All employees involved in excavation, trenching, and shoring operations must receive training on the hazards associated with these activities and the methods to control them. Training must cover recognition of hazardous conditions, safe work practices, and the proper use of protective systems. Training records must be maintained by MH Construction, Inc. and made available upon request. (Per 29 CFR 1926.21(b)(2) and WAC 296-155-005)

8. Inspection and Enforcement

A Competent Person will conduct daily inspections of all excavations, adjacent areas, and protective systems. Inspections will also occur after any event that could increase hazards, such as heavy rainfall or seismic activity. Any identified deficiencies will be corrected immediately. Non-compliance with this program will result in disciplinary action as outlined in the Violation Consequences section.

9. Recordkeeping

Records of Competent Person training, daily excavation inspection logs, and incident reports related to excavation activities will be maintained for a minimum of three years by the Safety Officer. These records will be accessible to employees, their representatives, and regulatory agencies upon request.

10. Violation Consequences

Violations of this Excavation, Trenching, and Shoring program will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and gravity of the violation, as well as the employee's prior safety record.

11. References

  • 29 CFR 1926 Subpart P
  • 29 CFR 1926.650 Scope
  • 29 CFR 1926.651 Specific Excavation Requirements
  • 29 CFR 1926.652 Requirements for Protective Systems
  • WAC 296-155-650 Scope and Application
  • WAC 296-155-652 Definitions
  • WAC 296-155-655 General Protection Requirements
  • WAC 296-155-657 Requirements for Protective Systems
  • WAC 296-155-664 Appendices A-F
  • AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 20 Excavation and Trenching

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 27 | Construction Equipment Modifications and Fabrication Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey —————————————————————————————————————————————————— Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926. 21(b) OSHA 29 CFR 1926.

MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 27 | Construction Equipment Modifications and Fabrication

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

——————————————————————————————————————————————————

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.21(b)

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.602(a) Earthmoving Equipment Modifications

OSHA

ANSI/ASME B30

Series

WISHA

WAC 296-155-605

Equipment Employer Modifications

WISHA

WAC 296-155-610

Motor Vehicles on Construction Sites

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 13

Purpose

This policy establishes guidelines for the modification and fabrication of construction equipment to ensure operational safety and compliance with regulatory standards. It aims to prevent accidents, injuries, and equipment failures resulting from unauthorized or improperly executed alterations, thereby safeguarding personnel and assets on all MH Construction job sites.

Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors involved in the operation, maintenance, modification, or fabrication of construction equipment on any MH Construction job site. Adherence is mandatory to maintain a safe working environment and ensure regulatory compliance.

Definitions

Term

Definition

Construction Equipment

Any machinery, vehicle, or tool used in construction operations, including but not limited to excavators, loaders, cranes, and forklifts.

Modification

Any alteration to the original design, function, or operational parameters of construction equipment.

Fabrication

The process of constructing new equipment or components from raw materials.

Competent Person

An individual capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Qualified Person

One who, by possession of a recognized degree, certificate, or professional standing, or who by extensive knowledge, training, and experience, has successfully demonstrated his ability to solve or resolve problems relating to the subject matter, the work, or the project.

Responsibilities

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • • Provide necessary resources for safe equipment modification and fabrication.
  • • Ensure compliance with all applicable OSHA, WISHA, and AGC standards.
  • • Establish and enforce clear policies and procedures for equipment alterations.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • • Oversee all equipment modification and fabrication activities on site.
  • • Ensure all personnel involved are trained and competent.
  • • Conduct pre-use inspections of modified or fabricated equipment.
  • • Verify that all modifications adhere to manufacturer specifications or engineered designs.

Employee Responsibilities

  • • Adhere to all company policies and procedures regarding equipment.
  • • Report any unsafe conditions or unauthorized modifications immediately.
  • • Do not operate equipment that has been improperly modified or fabricated.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • • Comply with MH Construction’s equipment modification and fabrication policies.
  • • Ensure their equipment meets all regulatory and safety standards.
  • • Provide documentation for any modifications made to their equipment.

Procedures / Requirements

7.1 Authorization for Modifications

All proposed modifications to construction equipment must receive prior written authorization from MH Construction management and a Qualified Person. Unauthorized modifications are strictly prohibited. Per 29 CFR 1926.21(b), employers must instruct each employee in the recognition and avoidance of unsafe conditions and the regulations applicable to his work environment to control or eliminate any hazards or other exposure to illness or injury.

7.2 Design and Engineering

Modifications impacting structural integrity, operational controls, or safety systems must be designed and approved by a Professional Engineer. Detailed drawings and calculations must be maintained. Per WAC 296-155-605, equipment employer modifications must be approved by the manufacturer or a qualified engineer.

7.3 Fabrication Standards

Fabricated components must meet or exceed original equipment manufacturer (OEM) specifications for material strength, welding, and finish. All welding must be performed by certified welders. Per ANSI/ASME B30 Series, specific standards apply to cranes and lifting equipment, ensuring safe design and operation.

7.4 Earthmoving Equipment Modifications

Modifications to earthmoving equipment, including ROPS/FOPS, must comply with 29 CFR 1926.602(a). Any modification that affects the capacity or safe operation of the equipment must be approved by the manufacturer or a qualified engineer. The operator’s manual must be updated to reflect changes.

7.5 Inspection and Testing

Following any modification or fabrication, equipment must be thoroughly inspected and tested by a Competent Person before being returned to service. Documentation of these inspections and tests is required. This includes load testing for lifting equipment if capacity is altered.

7.6 Operator Training

Operators of modified equipment must receive additional training on any new operational characteristics, controls, or limitations. This training must be documented. Per WAC 296-155-610, motor vehicles on construction sites must be operated by trained personnel.

7.7 Documentation and Labeling

All modified or fabricated equipment must be clearly labeled with information regarding the modification, including date, approving authority, and any new operational limits. A permanent record of all modifications must be kept with the equipment’s maintenance file.

Training Requirements

All employees involved in equipment modification, fabrication, or operation of modified equipment must receive specific training relevant to their tasks. Training will cover this policy, specific procedures, potential hazards, and regulatory requirements (OSHA 29 CFR 1926.21(b), WAC 296-155-605). Training frequency will be determined by the Safety Officer based on job role and equipment changes, with documentation maintained by MH Construction management.

Inspection and Enforcement

Compliance with this policy will be monitored through regular site inspections by Supervisors and the Safety Officer. Modified or fabricated equipment will undergo pre-use and periodic inspections. Any non-compliance or unsafe conditions will result in immediate corrective action, including equipment removal from service until deemed safe. Enforcement will follow MH Construction’s disciplinary policy.

Recordkeeping

The following records must be maintained by MH Construction management for a minimum of 7 years: written authorizations for modifications, engineering designs and calculations, inspection and testing reports, and operator training records. These records are crucial for demonstrating compliance and for accident investigation purposes.

Violation Consequences

Violations of this policy will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. Minor infractions may lead to retraining and warnings, while serious violations or those causing injury/damage will result in immediate and severe consequences. All disciplinary actions will be documented.

References

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.21(b) - Safety training and education

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.602(a) - Earthmoving Equipment Modifications

• ANSI/ASME B30 Series - Safety Standard for Cranes, Derricks, Hoists, Hooks, Jacks, and Slings

• WISHA: WAC 296-155-605 - Equipment Employer Modifications

• WISHA: WAC 296-155-610 - Motor Vehicles on Construction Sites

• AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 13 - Construction Equipment

sections.title: housekeeping
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 28 | Housekeeping Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey —————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————— 1.  Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 28 | Housekeeping

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey


——————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————

1. Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.25 Housekeeping

WISHA

WAC 296-155-020

Housekeeping

WISHA

WAC 296-155-335

Disposal of Waste Materials

AGC

Jobsite Safety Handbook Housekeeping

2. Purpose

The purpose of this Housekeeping policy is to establish clear guidelines for maintaining clean, orderly, and safe work environments across all MH Construction job sites. This policy aims to prevent accidents, minimize health hazards, and ensure compliance with regulatory standards by eliminating clutter, spills, and other unsafe conditions.

3. Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction employees, subcontractors, and visitors present on any MH Construction job site. Adherence to these guidelines is mandatory to ensure a consistent standard of safety and operational efficiency.

4. Definitions

Term

Definition

Housekeeping

The systematic maintenance of order and cleanliness in a workplace.

Debris

Scattered pieces of waste or remains, typically found on construction sites.

Work Area

Any location where work is being performed or materials are stored.

Hazard

A source of potential harm or adverse health effect.

PPE

Personal Protective Equipment, such as gloves, safety glasses, and hard hats.

5. Responsibilities

The following responsibilities are assigned to ensure effective housekeeping practices:

5.1 Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Provide necessary resources (e.g., waste receptacles, cleaning supplies, adequate disposal facilities).
  • Establish and communicate clear housekeeping policies and procedures.
  • Ensure regular inspections and audits of job sites.

5.2 Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Enforce housekeeping standards daily.
  • Conduct regular visual inspections of work areas.
  • Ensure proper disposal of waste materials (Per WAC 296-155-335).
  • Address and rectify housekeeping deficiencies promptly.

5.3 Employee Responsibilities

  • Maintain a clean and orderly personal work area.
  • Dispose of waste and debris in designated receptacles.
  • Report any unsafe housekeeping conditions to their supervisor.
  • Participate in general site clean-up efforts.

5.4 Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Adhere to MH Construction’s housekeeping policies.
  • Maintain cleanliness within their assigned work zones.
  • Remove their own waste and debris or utilize designated site facilities.

6. Procedures / Requirements

6.1 General Housekeeping (Per 29 CFR 1926.25 and WAC 296-155-020)

1. All places of employment, passageways, storerooms, and service rooms shall be kept clean and orderly.

2. The floor of every workroom shall be maintained in a clean and, so far as possible, dry condition. Where wet processes are used, drainage shall be maintained, and false floors, platforms, mats, or other dry standing places shall be provided where practicable.

3. To facilitate cleaning, every floor, working place, and passageway shall be kept free from protruding nails, splinters, holes, or loose boards.

4. Combustible scrap and debris shall be removed at regular intervals.

6.2 Waste Material Disposal (Per WAC 296-155-335)

1. Debris and waste materials shall be removed from work areas frequently and regularly.

2. All waste shall be disposed of in designated containers. Containers used for garbage and other oily, flammable, or hazardous wastes shall be equipped with covers.

3. Chutes, whenever used, shall be enclosed. The area where debris is dropped shall be barricaded, and signs shall be posted warning of the hazard.

4. Open fires and burning of waste materials are strictly prohibited on all job sites.

6.3 Material Storage

1. Materials shall be stacked, stored, and secured in a manner that prevents sliding, falling, or collapse.

2. Aisles and passageways shall be kept clear to allow for the free and safe movement of employees and equipment.

3. Flammable and combustible materials shall be stored in approved containers and designated areas, away from ignition sources.

6.4 Spill Prevention and Control

1. Spills of oil, grease, paint, or other liquids shall be cleaned up immediately to prevent slips and falls.

2. Appropriate absorbent materials shall be readily available on site for spill containment and cleanup.

3. Contaminated absorbent materials shall be disposed of according to environmental regulations.

6.5 Tool and Equipment Management

1. Tools and equipment shall be returned to their designated storage areas after use.

2. Damaged or defective tools shall be tagged out of service and reported for repair or replacement.

3. Cords, hoses, and cables shall be coiled or routed to prevent tripping hazards.

6.6 Site Access and Egress

1. Access roads, walkways, and stairs shall be kept clear of obstructions at all times.

2. Emergency exits and fire fighting equipment shall not be blocked.

3. Adequate lighting shall be provided in all work areas, especially in passageways and stairwells.

7. Training Requirements

All employees and supervisors shall receive initial training on the importance of good housekeeping practices, spill response procedures, and proper waste disposal methods. Training shall be documented and refreshed annually or as needed. Specific training on hazardous waste handling will be provided to relevant personnel.

8. Inspection and Enforcement

Supervisors are responsible for daily informal inspections of their work areas. Formal, documented inspections will be conducted weekly by the Safety Officer or designated personnel. Any identified deficiencies will be recorded, and corrective actions will be assigned with deadlines. Non-compliance will be addressed through the company’s disciplinary policy.

9. Recordkeeping

Records of housekeeping inspections, corrective actions, and employee training shall be maintained by the Safety Officer for a minimum of three years. These records will be available for review by regulatory agencies upon request.

10. Violation Consequences

Failure to comply with this Housekeeping policy may result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. Violations will be addressed through a progressive disciplinary process, considering the severity and frequency of the infraction. Immediate action will be taken for conditions posing imminent danger.

11. References

  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.25 Housekeeping
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-020 Housekeeping
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-335 Disposal of Waste Materials
  • AGC: Jobsite Safety Handbook Housekeeping

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 29 | Electrical Safety Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 1. sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 29 | Electrical Safety

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. REGULATORY AUTHORITY

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1926 Subpart K

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.400 Introduction

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.404 Wiring Design and Protection GFCI

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.416 General Requirements

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.417 Lockout and Tagging

WISHA

WAC 296-155-426

Introduction

WISHA

WAC 296-155-428

General Requirements

WISHA

WAC 296-155-429

Lockout and Tagging of Circuits

WISHA

WAC 296-155-444

General Requirements

WISHA

WAC 296-155-447

Wiring Design and Protection

AGC

Health Manual Ch. 21 Electrical Safety

Manual Ch. 21 Electrical Safety

2. PURPOSE

This policy establishes guidelines and procedures to protect all personnel from electrical hazards encountered on MH Construction job sites. It aims to prevent electrical shocks, burns, fires, and explosions, ensuring a safe working environment in compliance with applicable regulatory standards.

3. SCOPE

This policy applies to all MH Construction employees, subcontractors, and visitors present on any MH Construction job site where electrical work or exposure to electrical hazards may occur.

4. DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Competent Person

One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Energized

Connected to an energy source or containing residual or stored energy.

Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)

The placement of a lockout device or tagout device on an energy isolating device, in accordance with an established procedure, to ensure that the energy isolating device and the equipment being controlled cannot be operated until the lockout/tagout device is removed.

Ground-Fault Circuit Interrupter (GFCI)

A device intended for the protection of personnel that functions to de-energize a circuit or portion thereof within an established period of time when a current to ground exceeds some predetermined value that is less than that required to operate the overcurrent protective device of the supply circuit.

Qualified Person

One who has demonstrated skills and knowledge related to the construction and operation of electrical equipment and installations and has received safety training to recognize and avoid the hazards involved.

5. RESPONSIBILITIES

5.1 Company/Management Responsibilities

MH Construction, Inc. management is responsible for:

  • Providing a safe work environment free from recognized electrical hazards.
  • Ensuring compliance with all applicable OSHA, WISHA, and AGC electrical safety standards.
  • Providing necessary electrical safety equipment and personal protective equipment (PPE).
  • Implementing and enforcing this Electrical Safety Program.

5.2 Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

Supervisors and Foremen are responsible for:

  • Conducting regular site inspections to identify and correct electrical hazards.
  • Ensuring all employees under their supervision are trained and adhere to electrical safety procedures.
  • Verifying proper use of lockout/tagout procedures and other energy control measures.
  • Investigating all electrical incidents and near-misses, and implementing corrective actions.

5.3 Employee Responsibilities

All employees are responsible for:

  • Adhering to all electrical safety rules and procedures outlined in this program.
  • Using appropriate PPE and electrical safety equipment as required.
  • Reporting any electrical hazards, defective equipment, or unsafe conditions immediately to their supervisor.
  • Never working on energized electrical equipment unless specifically authorized and trained to do so.

5.4 Subcontractor Responsibilities

Subcontractors are responsible for:

  • Complying with all aspects of this Electrical Safety Program and applicable regulatory standards.
  • Ensuring their employees are properly trained and equipped for electrical work.
  • Coordinating all electrical work with MH Construction site management.

6. PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

6.1 General Electrical Safety Requirements

  1. All electrical installations and work practices shall comply with 29 CFR 1926 Subpart K and WAC 296-155-426 through WAC 296-155-447. Only qualified persons shall install, maintain, or repair electrical equipment. (Per 29 CFR 1926.416 General Requirements; WAC 296-155-428 General Requirements)
  2. Ensure all electrical equipment and tools are in good working order, free from defects, and properly grounded or double-insulated. Damaged equipment must be immediately removed from service and tagged out. (Per 29 CFR 1926.416(a)(1); WAC 296-155-428(1))
  3. Avoid working in wet conditions or with wet hands when operating electrical equipment. Use insulated gloves and protective footwear when necessary.
  4. Maintain adequate clear working space around electrical equipment to permit safe operation and maintenance. Do not store materials in electrical pathways or near panels.

6.2 Ground-Fault Circuit Interrupters (GFCIs)

  1. All 120-volt, single-phase, 15- and 20-ampere receptacle outlets on construction sites, which are not part of the permanent wiring of the building or structure and which are in use by employees, shall have approved ground-fault circuit interrupters for personnel protection. (Per 29 CFR 1926.404(b)(1)(ii); WAC 296-155-447(5))
  2. GFCIs shall be tested regularly as per manufacturer’s instructions and prior to each use to ensure proper functionality. Records of these tests shall be maintained.

6.3 Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) Procedures

  1. All electrical circuits and equipment shall be de-energized and locked out/tagged out before any work is performed on them, unless work on energized equipment is absolutely necessary and performed by qualified personnel under strict safety protocols. (Per 29 CFR 1926.417 Lockout and Tagging; WAC 296-155-429 Lockout and Tagging of Circuits)
  2. A written LOTO procedure shall be developed and utilized for all equipment requiring servicing or maintenance where unexpected energization or startup could occur. This procedure must clearly outline steps for shutdown, isolation, lockout/tagout application, stored energy release, verification of de-energization, and restoration of service.
  3. Each lockout/tagout device shall be standardized within the facility in at least one of the following criteria: Color, shape, or size; and in addition, in the case of tagout devices, print and format shall be standardized. (Per WAC 296-155-429(3)(b))

6.4 Overhead Power Lines

  1. Maintain a minimum clearance distance of 10 feet from overhead power lines for all equipment and personnel. If work must be performed closer than 10 feet, the power company must be contacted to de-energize and ground the lines, or insulating barriers must be installed. (Per 29 CFR 1926.416(a)(3); WAC 296-155-428(3))

6.5 Temporary Wiring

  1. Temporary electrical wiring shall be installed and maintained in a safe manner, protected from damage, and not run through doorways, windows, or across aisles without proper protection. (Per 29 CFR 1926.400(c)(1); WAC 296-155-444(1))
  2. Extension cords shall be of the three-wire type, designed for hard or extra-hard usage, and equipped with an equipment grounding conductor. Cords shall not be spliced or repaired with tape. (Per 29 CFR 1926.404(b)(1)(iii)(C); WAC 296-155-447(5)(c))

6.6 Portable Electric Tools

  1. All portable electric tools shall be either grounded, double-insulated, or powered by a low-voltage isolation transformer. Tools shall be inspected before each use for damage to cords, plugs, and housings. (Per 29 CFR 1926.416(a)(1); WAC 296-155-428(1))

7. TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

  1. All employees whose work involves potential exposure to electrical hazards shall receive training to understand the hazards and the necessary precautions. This includes recognizing and avoiding unsafe conditions, understanding lockout/tagout procedures, and proper use of PPE. (Per 29 CFR 1926.21(b)(2); WAC 296-155-100)
  2. Qualified persons performing work on or near energized parts shall be trained in the skills and techniques necessary to distinguish exposed live parts from other parts of electric equipment, to determine the nominal voltage of exposed live parts, and the clearance distances specified in 29 CFR 1926.416(a)(1). (Per 29 CFR 1926.416(a)(1); WAC 296-155-428(1))
  3. Training shall be provided upon initial assignment, when job duties change, or when new equipment or procedures are introduced. Retraining shall be conducted as necessary to ensure proficiency. All training shall be documented and records maintained by the Safety Officer.

8. INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

  1. The Safety Officer and Supervisors will conduct regular, unannounced inspections of job sites to ensure compliance with this Electrical Safety Program and all applicable regulations. Inspections will focus on proper grounding, GFCI usage, LOTO adherence, and overall electrical equipment condition.
  2. Any deficiencies identified during inspections will be documented, and corrective actions will be assigned with a clear timeline for completion. Follow-up inspections will verify the effectiveness of corrective measures.
  3. Non-compliance with this program will result in enforcement actions as outlined in the Violation Consequences section.

9. RECORDKEEPING

  1. The following records shall be maintained by MH Construction:
  • Electrical safety training records (5 years)
  • GFCI test records (1 year)
  • Lockout/Tagout procedures (duration of equipment use)
  • Electrical incident investigation reports (5 years)
  • Site inspection reports (3 years)

10. VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

  1. Violations of this Electrical Safety Program will be subject to progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract, depending on the severity and frequency of the violation. All incidents will be thoroughly investigated, and appropriate corrective actions will be implemented to prevent recurrence.

11. REFERENCES

  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926 Subpart K
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.400 Introduction
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.404 Wiring Design and Protection GFCI
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.416 General Requirements
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.417 Lockout and Tagging
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-426 Introduction
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-428 General Requirements
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-429 Lockout and Tagging of Circuits
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-444 General Requirements
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-447 Wiring Design and Protection
  • AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 21 Electrical Safety

sections.title: signs signals and barricades
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.
Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)
MISH 30 | Signs, Signals, and Barricades
Revision N/A | Effective: ** 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: ** Matt Ramsey | **Revision:** 3 | **Effective:** 04/07/2026


——————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926 Subpart G

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.200 Accident Prevention Signs and Tags

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.201 Signaling

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.202 Barricades

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.203 Definitions

WISHA

WAC 296-155-305 Signaling and Flaggers

WISHA

WAC 296-155-310 Barricades

WISHA

WAC 296-155-315 Definitions

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 22 Traffic Control

Purpose

This policy establishes guidelines for the proper use of signs, signals, and barricades to prevent accidents and ensure the safety of all personnel on MH Construction job sites. It aims to protect employees, subcontractors, and the public from hazards associated with construction activities, particularly those involving traffic and restricted areas.

Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors present on MH Construction job sites. Adherence to these guidelines is mandatory to maintain a safe working environment.

Definitions

Term

Definition

Barricade

An obstruction to deter the passage of persons or vehicles.

Sign

A warning of a hazard, temporarily or permanently affixed or mounted.

Signal

A message transmitted by hand, flag, or audible device.

Flagger

A person who controls traffic with a flag or other signaling device.

Traffic Control Zone

The area of a roadway where traffic control measures are in effect.

Responsibilities

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Provide necessary signs, signals, and barricades.
  • Ensure compliance with all applicable regulations.
  • Conduct regular site safety audits.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Implement and enforce this policy on job sites.
  • Ensure proper placement and maintenance of safety devices.
  • Train employees on signaling procedures and barricade use.

Employee Responsibilities

  • Adhere to all signs, signals, and barricades.
  • Report damaged or missing safety devices.
  • Participate in required training.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Comply with MH Construction's safety policies and all regulatory requirements.
  • Provide their own appropriate safety devices as required by their scope of work.

Procedures / Requirements

  1. All signs shall be furnished and displayed where hazards exist. Signs shall be visible and legible at all times. Per 29 CFR 1926.200(b), signs shall be of standard colors and sizes as specified in ANSI Z53.1-1967.
  2. Danger signs shall be used only where an immediate hazard exists. They shall have a red oval background with the word 'DANGER' in white letters, and a white background for the message. (29 CFR 1926.200(c)).
  3. Caution signs shall be used only to warn against potential hazards or to caution against unsafe practices. They shall have a yellow background with the word 'CAUTION' in black letters, and a yellow background for the message. (29 CFR 1926.200(d)).
  4. Exit signs, when required, shall be lettered in legible red letters, not less than 6 inches high, on a white field, and the principal strokes of the letters shall be at least three-fourths inch wide. (29 CFR 1926.200(e)).
  5. Accident prevention tags shall be used as a temporary means of warning employees of an existing hazard, such as defective tools, equipment, etc. Tags shall be attached to the hazard by a string, wire, or adhesive. (29 CFR 1926.200(g)).
  6. Signaling shall be conducted by a designated person when equipment is operating in areas where employees are exposed to the hazards of moving equipment. Hand signals shall be standardized and understood by all operators and signal persons. Per WAC 296-155-305, flaggers must wear high-visibility apparel.
  7. Barricades shall be erected to guard excavations, open pits, and other hazardous areas. They shall be visible and capable of withstanding anticipated forces. Per 29 CFR 1926.202, barricades shall conform to the requirements of ANSI D6.1-1971.
  8. Traffic control plans shall be developed and implemented for all projects impacting public roadways. This includes proper signage, flagging, and barricading as per AGC Safety and Health Manual Ch. 22 Traffic Control and WAC 296-155-305.
  9. All signs, signals, and barricades shall be adequately illuminated during hours of darkness or poor visibility to ensure their effectiveness. (29 CFR 1926 Subpart G).
  10. Signs, signals, and barricades shall be inspected regularly and maintained in good condition. Damaged or defaced devices shall be immediately repaired or replaced. (WAC 296-155-310).

Training Requirements

All employees whose work involves exposure to traffic or requires the use of signs, signals, or barricades shall receive training on this policy and relevant regulatory requirements. Training shall cover proper signaling techniques, placement of barricades, and recognition of different sign types. Training frequency shall be as needed, and documentation of training shall be maintained by the Safety Officer. Applicable OSHA training mandates include general safety awareness (29 CFR 1926.21) and specific training for flaggers (WAC 296-155-305).

Inspection and Enforcement

Compliance with this policy will be monitored through regular site inspections conducted by supervisors, foremen, and the Safety Officer. Inspections will verify the correct placement, condition, and use of signs, signals, and barricades. Any deficiencies identified will result in immediate corrective action. Non-compliance will be addressed according to the company's progressive discipline policy.

Recordkeeping

Records of training, site inspections, and corrective actions related to signs, signals, and barricades shall be maintained for a minimum of three years. The Safety Officer is responsible for the retention and accessibility of these records.

Violation Consequences

Violations of this policy will result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract, depending on the severity and frequency of the violation. MH Construction is committed to maintaining a safe workplace, and strict adherence to safety policies is paramount.

References

  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926 Subpart G
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.200 Accident Prevention Signs and Tags
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.201 Signaling
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.202 Barricades
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.203 Definitions
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-305 Signaling and Flaggers
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-310 Barricades
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-315 Definitions
  • AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 22 Traffic Control

MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 31 | Miscellaneous Construction Requirements

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

First-Aid Station

WISHA

Sanitation

WISHA
