Occupational Noise Exposure

AGC

Jobsite Safety Handbook

Purpose

This policy establishes guidelines for miscellaneous construction requirements to ensure a safe and healthy work environment. It protects against common jobsite hazards and promotes compliance with federal and state safety regulations.

Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors present on any MH Construction job site.

Definitions

Term

Definition

Competent Person

One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

First Aid

Emergency care or treatment given to an ill or injured person before regular medical aid can be obtained.

Sanitation

Measures taken to protect public health, including the provision of clean drinking water, adequate sewage disposal, and proper hygiene facilities.

Noise Exposure

The level and duration of sound that a worker is subjected to, which can potentially cause hearing damage if not properly controlled.

Jobsite

Any location where MH Construction, Inc. or its subcontractors are performing work.

Responsibilities

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Provide and maintain a safe work environment in compliance with all applicable OSHA and WISHA regulations.
  • Ensure adequate resources are available for safety training, equipment, and first aid supplies.
  • Establish and enforce disciplinary actions for safety violations.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Conduct regular job site inspections to identify and correct hazards.
  • Ensure all employees and subcontractors understand and follow safety procedures.
  • Provide immediate first aid for injuries and ensure proper medical attention is sought when necessary.
  • Enforce company safety policies and regulatory requirements.

Employee Responsibilities

  • Comply with all established safety rules, policies, and procedures.
  • Report all unsafe conditions, hazards, and incidents to their supervisor immediately.
  • Use all provided safety equipment and personal protective equipment (PPE) correctly.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Adhere to all MH Construction, Inc. safety policies and procedures, as well as all applicable OSHA and WISHA regulations.
  • Provide their employees with necessary safety training and equipment.
  • Coordinate all safety-related activities with the MH Construction, Inc. supervisor or foreman.

Procedures / Requirements

7.1 Medical Services and First Aid

  1. A suitable number of employees must be trained in first aid. Per 29 CFR 1926.50(c) and WAC 296-155-120, at least one employee must be adequately trained to render first aid.
  2. Adequate first-aid supplies must be readily available at all worksites. Per 29 CFR 1926.50(d) and WAC 296-155-125, these supplies must be approved by a consulting physician and regularly inspected.
  3. For larger projects or those in remote areas, a first-aid station with a qualified person must be established. Per WAC 296-155-130, this station must be clearly identified and accessible.

7.2 Sanitation

  1. Potable water must be provided for drinking, washing, and cooking. Per 29 CFR 1926.51(a) and WAC 296-155-140(1), it must meet public health standards.
  2. Non-potable water, if used, must be clearly marked and not used for hygiene or consumption. Per 29 CFR 1926.51(b).
  3. Adequate toilet facilities must be provided for all employees. Per 29 CFR 1926.51(c) and WAC 296-155-140(2), these facilities must be maintained in a sanitary condition.
  4. Washing facilities must be available for employees. Per 29 CFR 1926.51(f) and WAC 296-155-140(5), these should include soap and water or suitable cleansing agents.

7.3 Occupational Noise Exposure

  1. Exposure to noise must be controlled to prevent hearing loss. Per 29 CFR 1926.52 and WAC 296-155-145, engineering or administrative controls must be implemented when sound levels exceed permissible exposure limits.
  2. When controls are insufficient, personal protective equipment (PPE) such as earplugs or earmuffs must be provided and used. Per 29 CFR 1926.52(b) and WAC 296-155-145(2).
  3. A hearing conservation program must be implemented for employees exposed to noise at or above an 8-hour time-weighted average of 85 dBA. Per WAC 296-155-145(3).

7.4 Housekeeping

  1. Work areas, passageways, and stairs shall be kept clear of obstructions and debris to prevent slips, trips, and falls. Per AGC Jobsite Safety Handbook.
  2. Waste materials shall be removed regularly and disposed of in appropriate containers to maintain a safe and sanitary work environment.

7.5 Fire Prevention and Protection

  1. Fire extinguishers shall be provided and maintained in accessible locations. Employees must be trained in their proper use. Per 29 CFR 1926 Subpart C.
  2. Flammable and combustible materials shall be stored in approved containers and designated areas, away from ignition sources.

7.6 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)

  1. Appropriate PPE, such as hard hats, safety glasses, and high-visibility vests, shall be worn by all personnel on the jobsite as required by task and site conditions. Per 29 CFR 1926 Subpart C.
  2. Employees must be trained on the proper use, care, and maintenance of their assigned PPE.

Training Requirements

All employees whose work involves potential exposure to hazards covered in this section must receive initial and periodic refresher training. Training will cover proper first aid procedures (WAC 296-155-120), sanitation practices, and hearing conservation (WAC 296-155-145). Training records, including dates, attendees, and topics covered, will be maintained by the Safety Officer.

Inspection and Enforcement

Supervisors and foremen will conduct daily informal inspections, and the Safety Officer will conduct weekly formal inspections to ensure compliance with this policy and all applicable regulations. Any identified deficiencies will be documented, and corrective actions will be implemented promptly. Non-compliance will result in enforcement actions as outlined in the company's disciplinary policy.

Recordkeeping

The following records will be maintained by the Safety Officer for a minimum of five years: first aid training records, first aid supply inspection logs, noise exposure monitoring results, and inspection reports. These records will be made available upon request by regulatory agencies.

Violation Consequences

Violations of this policy or any applicable safety regulation will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and gravity of the violation, as well as any prior infractions.

References

AGC: Jobsite Safety Handbook

OSHA: 29 CFR 1926 Subpart C

OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.50 Medical Services and First Aid

OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.51 Sanitation

OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.52 Occupational Noise Exposure

WISHA: WAC 296-155-120 First-Aid Training

WISHA: WAC 296-155-125 First-Aid Supplies

WISHA: WAC 296-155-130 First-Aid Station

WISHA: WAC 296-155-140 Sanitation

WISHA: WAC 296-155-145 Occupational Noise Exposure

sections.title: respiratory protection
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 32 | Respiratory Protection Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey —————————————————————————————————————————————————— Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1910. 134 Respiratory Protection OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 32 | Respiratory Protection

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

——————————————————————————————————————————————————

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1910.134 Respiratory Protection

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.103 Respiratory Protection

WISHA

WAC 296-842

Respiratory Protection

WISHA

WAC 296-155-220

Respiratory Protection

AGC

Safety and Health

Manual Ch. 23 Respiratory Protection

Purpose

This policy establishes the minimum requirements for respiratory protection at MH Construction, Inc. It aims to prevent occupational diseases and injuries that may result from exposure to hazardous atmospheres, ensuring the health and safety of all personnel on our job sites.

Scope

This program applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors who may be exposed to respiratory hazards or are required to wear respiratory protection on any MH Construction job site.

Definitions

Term

Definition

Air-Purifying Respirator

A respirator with an air-purifying filter, cartridge, or canister that removes specific air contaminants by passing ambient air through the air-purifying element.

Atmosphere-Supplying Respirator

A respirator that supplies the respirator user with breathing air from a source independent of the ambient atmosphere, and includes supplied-air respirators (SARs) and self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) units.

Assigned Protection Factor (APF)

The workplace level of respiratory protection that a respirator or class of respirators is expected to provide to employees when the employer implements a continuing, effective respiratory protection program.

Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health (IDLH)

An atmosphere that poses an immediate threat to life, would cause irreversible adverse health effects, or would impair an individual's ability to escape from a dangerous atmosphere.

Fit Testing

The use of a protocol to qualitatively or quantitatively evaluate the fit of a respirator on an individual.

Responsibilities

The following responsibilities are assigned to ensure effective implementation and maintenance of the Respiratory Protection Program:

Company/Management Responsibilities

• Establish and maintain a written respiratory protection program (29 CFR 1910.134(c)).

• Provide appropriate respirators, training, and medical evaluations at no cost to employees (29 CFR 1910.134(c)(4)).

• Designate a program administrator qualified by training or experience (29 CFR 1910.134(c)(3)).

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

• Ensure employees under their supervision comply with the respiratory protection program.

• Ensure proper selection, use, and maintenance of respirators in their work areas.

• Provide initial and annual training to employees on respiratory hazards and proper respirator use.

Employee Responsibilities

• Use respirators in accordance with training and instructions.

• Inspect, clean, and maintain their assigned respirators.

• Report any respirator malfunctions or concerns to their supervisor.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

• Comply with MH Construction's Respiratory Protection Program and all applicable OSHA/WISHA regulations.

• Ensure their employees are medically cleared, fit-tested, and trained for respirator use.

Procedures / Requirements

7.1 Program Administration

The Respiratory Protection Program will be administered by a designated program administrator who is suitably trained and experienced. The program will be updated as necessary to reflect changes in workplace conditions that affect respirator use (29 CFR 1910.134(c)(1)).

7.2 Hazard Evaluation and Respirator Selection

MH Construction will identify and evaluate respiratory hazards in the workplace, including a reasonable estimate of employee exposures and identification of contaminants. Respirators will be selected based on these hazards and user factors, and must be NIOSH-certified (29 CFR 1910.134(d)(1)). For IDLH atmospheres, only full facepiece pressure demand SCBA or combination full facepiece pressure demand SAR with auxiliary self-contained air supply will be used (29 CFR 1910.134(d)(2)).

7.3 Medical Evaluation

Employees required to use respirators will undergo medical evaluations to determine their ability to use a respirator. This includes a medical questionnaire and follow-up medical examinations as required by 29 CFR 1910.134(e) and WAC 296-842-14005.

7.4 Fit Testing

Fit testing will be conducted for all employees using tight-fitting respirators. Fit tests will be performed prior to initial use, whenever a different respirator facepiece is used, and at least annually thereafter. Both qualitative fit testing (QLFT) and quantitative fit testing (QNFT) methods may be used, as per 29 CFR 1910.134(f) and WAC 296-842-15005.

7.5 Proper Use of Respirators

Respirators must be used in accordance with manufacturer's instructions and MH Construction's procedures. This includes proper donning and doffing, user seal checks, and understanding the limitations of the respirator. Employees must leave the respirator use area if they detect odor, taste, irritation, or experience dizziness or nausea (29 CFR 1910.134(g) and WAC 296-842-19005).

7.6 Maintenance and Care of Respirators

Respirators will be cleaned, disinfected, stored, inspected, and repaired according to 29 CFR 1910.134(h) and WAC 296-842-17005. Respirators must be stored to protect them from damage, contamination, dust, sunlight, extreme temperatures, excessive moisture, and damaging chemicals.

7.7 Breathing Air Quality and Quantity

For atmosphere-supplying respirators, MH Construction will ensure that breathing air meets the requirements of 29 CFR 1910.134(i) and WAC 296-842-24005, including specifications for oxygen content, contaminant levels, and dew point.

7.8 Voluntary Respirator Use

Employees may voluntarily use respirators if such use does not create a hazard. For voluntary use of filtering facepieces (dust masks), the employer is not required to include these employees in a written program. For other voluntary respirator use, medical evaluations and proper maintenance are still required (29 CFR 1910.134(c)(2) and WAC 296-842-11005).

Training Requirements

All employees required to use respirators will receive comprehensive training prior to initial use and at least annually thereafter. Training will cover:

• The nature, extent, and effects of respiratory hazards.

• Proper selection, use, inspection, cleaning, maintenance, and storage of respirators.

• The limitations and capabilities of the respirators.

• Emergency procedures, including self-rescue and use of respirators in IDLH atmospheres.

• Medical signs and symptoms that may limit or prevent the effective use of respirators.

Training will be conducted in a manner that is understandable to the employees and will include an opportunity for employees to ask questions and receive answers. Documentation of training will be maintained (29 CFR 1910.134(k) and WAC 296-842-16005).

Inspection and Enforcement

Compliance with this program will be monitored through regular workplace inspections, observations, and program audits. Supervisors are responsible for daily enforcement. Any deficiencies identified will be corrected promptly. Non-compliance will be addressed through MH Construction's disciplinary policy.

Recordkeeping

MH Construction will maintain records of medical evaluations, fit testing, and training for all employees required to use respirators. These records will be maintained for the duration of employment plus 30 years, as per OSHA requirements. The program administrator is responsible for maintaining these records (29 CFR 1910.134(m) and WAC 296-842-28005).

Violation Consequences

Failure to comply with the Respiratory Protection Program may result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract, consistent with MH Construction's progressive disciplinary policy. Violations may also lead to regulatory fines and penalties.

References

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1910.134 Respiratory Protection

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.103 Respiratory Protection

• WISHA: WAC 296-842 Respiratory Protection

• WISHA: WAC 296-155-220 Respiratory Protection

• AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 23 Respiratory Protection

sections.title: floor openings open sided surfaces and ramps
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 33 | Floor Openings, Open-sided Surfaces, and Ramps Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey —————————————————————————————————————————————————— 1.  REGULATORY AUTHORITY Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart M OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 33 | Floor Openings, Open-sided Surfaces, and Ramps

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey


——————————————————————————————————————————————————

1. REGULATORY AUTHORITY

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926 Subpart M

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.502(b) Guardrail Systems

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.502(i) Covers |

WISHA

WAC 296-880 Unified Fall Protection covers floor openings open-sided surfaces and ramps |

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 7 Fall Protection

2. PURPOSE

This policy establishes guidelines and requirements for the identification, evaluation, and control of hazards associated with floor openings, open-sided surfaces, and ramps. It aims to prevent falls and related injuries to employees, subcontractors, and visitors on MH Construction job sites.

3. SCOPE

This policy applies to all MH Construction employees, subcontractors, and visitors on MH Construction job sites where floor openings, open-sided surfaces, or ramps are present.

4. DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Floor Opening

An opening measuring 12 inches or more in its least dimension in any floor, platform, pavement, or yard, through which persons may fall.

Open-sided Surface

Any floor or platform edge which is 6 feet or more above an adjacent lower level and is not guarded by a wall or parapet at least 39 inches high.

Ramp

An inclined walkway or passageway used to connect different levels.

Guardrail System

A barrier erected to prevent employees from falling to lower levels.

Hole Cover

A cover placed over a floor opening to prevent persons from falling through.

5. RESPONSIBILITIES

5.1 Company/Management Responsibilities

• Provide fall protection systems and equipment.
• Ensure compliance with all applicable OSHA, WISHA, and AGC regulations.
• Conduct regular site inspections to identify and mitigate hazards.

5.2 Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

• Ensure all floor openings, open-sided surfaces, and ramps are properly guarded.
• Train employees on fall protection procedures.
• Enforce compliance with safety policies on site.

5.3 Employee Responsibilities

• Follow all fall protection procedures and use provided equipment correctly.
• Report any unguarded openings or hazardous conditions immediately.
• Never remove or alter fall protection systems without authorization.

5.4 Subcontractor Responsibilities

• Adhere to MH Construction\\\"s fall protection plan and all regulatory requirements.
• Provide their own compliant fall protection equipment and ensure its proper use.
• Coordinate with MH Construction supervisors regarding any fall hazard concerns.

6. PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

6.1 General Requirements for Fall Protection

Fall protection must be provided for employees working on walking/working surfaces with unprotected sides or edges 6 feet (1.8 meters) or more above a lower level. Per 29 CFR 1926.501(b)(1) and WAC 296-880-200.

6.2 Guardrail Systems

Guardrail systems shall be capable of withstanding a force of at least 200 pounds (890 N) applied within 2 inches (5.1 cm) of the top edge, in any outward or downward direction, at any point along the top edge. Per 29 CFR 1926.502(b)(3) and WAC 296-880-300(2).

The top edge height of toprails, or equivalent guardrail system members, shall be 42 inches (1.1 m) plus or minus 3 inches (8 cm) above the walking/working level. Per 29 CFR 1926.502(b)(1) and WAC 296-880-300(1).

6.3 Covers for Floor Openings

Covers for floor openings shall be capable of supporting, without failure, at least twice the maximum intended load that may be imposed on the cover at any one time. Per 29 CFR 1926.502(i)(3) and WAC 296-880-400(2).

All covers shall be secured when installed so as to prevent accidental displacement by the wind, equipment, or employees. Per 29 CFR 1926.502(i)(4) and WAC 296-880-400(3).

All covers shall be color coded or marked with the word "HOLE" or "COVER" to provide warning of the hazard. Per WAC 296-880-400(4).

6.4 Protection of Ramps

Ramps, runways, and other walkways shall be constructed, where possible, with a slope not exceeding 1 in 3. When the slope is steeper than 1 in 3, or when the surface is slippery, cleats or other means shall be provided to ensure a safe footing. Per WAC 296-155-505(1).

Ramps and walkways 6 feet or more above lower levels must be protected by guardrail systems. Per 29 CFR 1926.501(b)(1) and WAC 296-880-200.

6.5 Warning Line Systems

Where guardrail systems are not feasible, warning line systems may be used in conjunction with other fall protection measures, such as safety monitoring systems, for work on low-slope roofs. Per 29 CFR 1926.502(f) and WAC 296-880-500.

6.6 Securing Covers and Guardrails

All covers and guardrail systems must be securely installed and maintained to prevent accidental removal or displacement. Any damaged or deficient fall protection must be immediately reported and repaired or replaced. Per 29 CFR 1926.502(b) and (i).

7. TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

All employees who might be exposed to fall hazards from floor openings, open-sided surfaces, or ramps must receive training on the recognition and control of such hazards. Training shall include: the nature of fall hazards in the work area; the correct procedures for erecting, maintaining, disassembling, and inspecting the fall protection systems to be used; the use and operation of guardrail systems, covers, and other fall protection systems; and the role of each employee in the safety monitoring system when used. Per 29 CFR 1926.503 and WAC 296-880-600. Training frequency will be as needed, with documentation maintained by the Safety Officer.

8. INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

Supervisors and foremen are responsible for daily inspections of all floor openings, open-sided surfaces, and ramps to ensure proper fall protection is in place and maintained. Any deficiencies found will be immediately corrected. Non-compliance with this policy will result in corrective action as outlined in the "Violation Consequences" section.

9. RECORDKEEPING

Records of fall protection training, site inspections, and corrective actions will be maintained by the Safety Officer for a minimum of three years. These records will be made available upon request by regulatory agencies.

10. VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

Violations of this policy will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and frequency of the violation, and the potential for serious injury or fatality.

11. REFERENCES

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1926 Subpart M

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.502(b) Guardrail Systems

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.502(i) Covers |

• WISHA: WAC 296-880 Unified Fall Protection covers floor openings open-sided surfaces and ramps |

• AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 7 Fall Protection

sections.title: compressed gas compressed air
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 34 | Compressed Gas/Compressed Air Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey ________________________________________________________________________________ Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart J Welding and Cutting OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.
Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)
MISH 34 | Compressed Gas/Compressed Air
Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

________________________________________________________________________________

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926 Subpart J

Welding and Cutting

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.350

Gas Welding and Cutting cylinder storage

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.302(b)

Pneumatic Power Tools

WISHA

WAC 296-155-400

Gas Welding and Cutting cylinder handling

WISHA

WAC 296-155-380

Air Receivers

WISHA

WAC 296-155-325

General Requirements for Storage

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 9

Compressed Gas/Compressed Air

1. Purpose

The purpose of this policy is to establish safe work practices for the handling, storage, and use of compressed gas cylinders and compressed air equipment. This policy protects employees from the physical and health hazards associated with high-pressure systems, explosive gases, and pneumatic tools. By adhering to these guidelines, MH Construction, Inc. ensures compliance with regulatory standards and minimizes the risk of catastrophic failure, fire, or injury.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors on any MHC job site or facility where compressed gas or compressed air is utilized, stored, or transported.

3. Definitions

Term

Definition

Compressed Gas

Any gas or mixture of gases having, in a container, an absolute pressure exceeding 40 psi at 70°F (21.1°C).

Cylinder

A pressure vessel designed for pressures higher than 40 psia and having a circular cross-section, used to store and transport compressed gases.

Pneumatic Tool

A tool driven by compressed air, such as nail guns, jackhammers, and air wrenches.

Air Receiver

A tank used for the storage of air compressed by an air compressor, designed to smooth out the pulsating output of the compressor and provide a reserve of compressed air.

Regulator

A mechanical device used to control the delivery pressure of a gas from a cylinder or system to the required working pressure.

4. Responsibilities

4.1 Company/Management

  • Provide appropriate and safe equipment for the handling and use of compressed gases and air.
  • Ensure all employees receive comprehensive training on the hazards and safe operating procedures.
  • Maintain a system for the regular inspection and maintenance of all compressed gas and air equipment.

4.2 Supervisor/Foreman

  • Enforce compliance with all procedures outlined in this section.
  • Conduct daily visual inspections of cylinders, hoses, regulators, and pneumatic tools before use.
  • Ensure cylinders are properly secured and stored when not in use.

4.3 Employee

  • Follow all safety procedures and use provided personal protective equipment (PPE).
  • Inspect equipment prior to each use and immediately report any defects or damage to the supervisor.
  • Never use compressed air for cleaning purposes unless reduced to less than 30 psi and with effective chip guarding.

4.4 Subcontractors

  • Comply with all MH Construction, Inc. policies and applicable OSHA/WISHA regulations regarding compressed gas and air.
  • Provide their own compliant equipment and ensure their personnel are adequately trained.

5. Procedures / Requirements

5.1 Cylinder Storage and Handling

1. All cylinders must be stored in a secure, well-ventilated area, protected from physical damage and direct sunlight. Per 29 CFR 1926.350(a)(1) and WAC 296-155-400, valve protection caps must be in place and secured when cylinders are not in use or are being transported.
2. Cylinders must be stored upright and secured with a chain, strap, or cable to a stationary object to prevent tipping.
3. Oxygen cylinders in storage must be separated from fuel-gas cylinders or combustible materials (especially oil or grease) by a minimum distance of 20 feet or by a noncombustible barrier at least 5 feet high having a fire-resistance rating of at least one-half hour (29 CFR 1926.350(a)(10)).
4. Empty cylinders must have their valves closed, caps replaced, and be marked 'MT' or 'EMPTY' and stored separately from full cylinders.

5.2 Transporting Cylinders

1. Cylinders must not be hoisted or transported by means of magnets or choker slings. A suitable cradle, boat, or suitable platform must be used.
2. Cylinders must be moved by tilting and rolling them on their bottom edges. Do not drag or slide them.
3. When transported by powered vehicles, cylinders must be secured in an upright position.

5.3 Use of Compressed Gas

1. Cylinders, valves, couplings, regulators, hoses, and apparatus must be kept free from oily or greasy substances. Oxygen cylinders or apparatus must not be handled with oily hands or gloves.
2. Before connecting a regulator to a cylinder valve, the valve must be opened slightly and closed immediately (cracking) to clear the valve of dust or dirt.
3. The cylinder valve must always be opened slowly to prevent damage to the regulator.
4. When a cylinder is in use, the valve key or wrench must be kept in place on the valve spindle to allow for rapid emergency shutoff.

5.4 Hoses and Connections

1. Fuel gas and oxygen hoses must be easily distinguishable from each other (typically red for fuel gas, green for oxygen).
2. Hoses must be inspected before each shift. Defective hoses must be removed from service immediately.
3. Hose couplings must be of the type that cannot be unlocked or disconnected by means of a straight pull without rotary motion.

5.5 Pneumatic Power Tools

1. Per 29 CFR 1926.302(b), pneumatic power tools must be secured to the hose or whip by some positive means to prevent the tool from becoming accidentally disconnected.
2. Safety clips or retainers must be securely installed and maintained on pneumatic impact (percussion) tools to prevent attachments from being accidentally expelled.
3. All pneumatically driven nailers, staplers, and other similar equipment provided with automatic fastener feed, which operate at more than 100 psi pressure at the tool, must have a safety device on the muzzle to prevent the tool from ejecting fasteners unless the muzzle is in contact with the work surface.
4. Hoses exceeding 1/2-inch inside diameter must have a safety excess flow valve installed at the source of supply or branch line to reduce pressure in case of hose failure.

5.6 Compressed Air for Cleaning

1. Compressed air must not be used for cleaning purposes except where reduced to less than 30 psi, and then only with effective chip guarding and personal protective equipment.
2. Compressed air must never be directed at a person or used to clean clothing while it is being worn.

5.7 Air Receivers

1. Per WAC 296-155-380, all air receivers must be equipped with an indicating pressure gauge and with one or more spring-loaded safety valves.
2. Safety valves must be tested frequently and at regular intervals to determine whether they are in good operating condition.
3. A drain pipe and valve must be installed at the lowest point of every air receiver to provide for the removal of accumulated oil and water. Receivers must be drained frequently.

5.8 General Storage Requirements

1. Per WAC 296-155-325, storage areas must be kept free from accumulation of materials that constitute hazards from tripping, fire, explosion, or pest harborage.
2. Storage of compressed gases must not obstruct exit routes, stairs, or areas normally used or intended for the safe passage of personnel.

6. Training Requirements

All employees who handle, store, or use compressed gas cylinders or pneumatic tools must receive training prior to initial assignment and whenever new hazards are introduced. Training must cover:
• The physical and health hazards of the gases in use.
• Proper handling, moving, and storage procedures.
• Inspection and safe operation of regulators, hoses, and pneumatic tools.
• Emergency response procedures for leaks or equipment failure.
Training must be documented, including the employee's name, date of training, and the signature of the instructor. Records must be maintained by the Safety Officer.

7. Inspection and Enforcement

1. **Daily Inspections:** Supervisors and operators must conduct visual inspections of all cylinders, hoses, regulators, and pneumatic tools prior to each shift.
2. **Weekly Inspections:** The Safety Officer or designated competent person will conduct weekly site walks to verify proper storage, segregation, and securing of cylinders.
3. **Enforcement:** Any equipment found to be defective must be immediately tagged 'Out of Service' and removed from the work area. Non-compliance with storage or handling procedures will result in immediate corrective action.

8. Recordkeeping

The following records must be maintained by the Safety Officer:
• Training records for all authorized personnel (retained for the duration of employment plus 3 years).
• Inspection logs for air receivers and safety valves (retained for 5 years).
• Incident reports related to compressed gas or pneumatic tool failures (retained indefinitely).

9. Violation Consequences

Failure to comply with this policy will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. The standard progression is:
1. First Offense: Verbal warning and immediate retraining.
2. Second Offense: Written reprimand documented in the employee's personnel file.
3. Third Offense: Suspension without pay or termination, depending on the severity of the violation and potential for harm.

10. References

  • OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart J - Welding and Cutting
  • OSHA 29 CFR 1926.350 - Gas Welding and Cutting
  • OSHA 29 CFR 1926.302(b) - Pneumatic Power Tools
  • WISHA WAC 296-155-400 - Gas Welding and Cutting
  • WISHA WAC 296-155-380 - Air Receivers
  • WISHA WAC 296-155-325 - General Requirements for Storage
  • AGC Safety and Health Manual Chapter 9

sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC. sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 35 | Rigging

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey


--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926

Subpart CC

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.251

Rigging Equipment for Material Handling

WISHA

WAC 296-155-336 through 296-155-340

Rigging Requirements for Material Handling

WISHA

WAC 296-155-556 through 296-155-562

Crane Rigging Requirements

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 15 Cranes and Rigging

Purpose

The purpose of this policy is to establish clear guidelines and procedures for safe rigging operations within MH Construction, Inc. This ensures the protection of employees, equipment, and property from hazards associated with material handling and lifting activities, thereby preventing accidents and injuries on all job sites.

Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors engaged in or exposed to rigging operations on any MH Construction job site.

Definitions

Term

Definition

Competent Person

An individual who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Qualified Person

One who, by possession of a recognized degree, certificate, or professional standing, or who by extensive knowledge, training, and experience, has successfully demonstrated his ability to solve or resolve problems relating to the subject matter, the work, or the project.

Rigging

The process of preparing equipment and materials for lifting, moving, or positioning, including the selection, inspection, and use of slings, shackles, hooks, and other lifting accessories.

Working Load Limit (WLL)

The maximum load that can be applied to a lifting device or accessory when the force is applied in direct tension.

Sling

An assembly of wire rope, chain, synthetic fiber rope, or synthetic web, used to connect a load to a hoisting device.

Responsibilities

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • • Provide a safe work environment and necessary resources for safe rigging operations.
    • Ensure all rigging equipment is properly maintained and inspected.
    • Implement and enforce this rigging safety program.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • • Ensure all rigging operations are conducted safely and in accordance with this policy.
    • Verify that employees and subcontractors are trained and competent in rigging tasks.
    • Conduct regular inspections of rigging equipment and job site conditions.

Employee Responsibilities

  • • Adhere to all rigging safety procedures and policies.
    • Inspect rigging equipment before each use and report any defects immediately.
    • Participate in all required rigging training.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • • Comply with all MH Construction rigging safety policies and applicable regulations.
    • Ensure their employees are qualified and trained for rigging tasks.
    • Provide and maintain safe rigging equipment.

Procedures / Requirements

7.1 General Rigging Safety

  1. All rigging operations shall be planned and supervised by a competent person. The weight of the load must be known, and the rigging equipment selected must have a sufficient working load limit (WLL) for the task. Per 29 CFR 1926.251(a)(1), rigging equipment shall not be loaded in excess of its recommended safe working load.

7.2 Rigging Equipment Inspection

  1. Prior to each shift and before use, all rigging equipment, including slings, shackles, hooks, and hoists, must be visually inspected by a competent person for damage, wear, or defects. Defective equipment shall be immediately removed from service. Per WAC 296-155-336(2), rigging equipment shall be inspected prior to use on each shift and as necessary during use to ensure it is safe. Defective rigging equipment shall be removed from service.

7.3 Sling Selection and Use

  1. Slings shall be selected based on the type of load, environmental conditions, and hitch configuration. Sling angles must be considered to prevent overloading. Per 29 CFR 1926.251(c)(1), slings shall be visually inspected for damage or defects by a competent person before each day's use.

7.4 Crane Rigging Requirements

  1. When rigging for crane operations, a qualified rigger must be involved in the rigging and unrigging of loads. Communication between the rigger and crane operator is paramount. Per WAC 296-155-556, all crane rigging operations must comply with specific Washington State requirements, which may be more stringent than federal OSHA standards.

7.5 Load Securement and Stability

  1. Loads must be properly balanced and secured to prevent shifting or falling during lifting and movement. Tag lines should be used to control load rotation. Ensure that the load is clear of all obstructions before lifting.

7.6 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)

  1. Employees involved in rigging operations must wear appropriate PPE, including hard hats, safety glasses, gloves, and high-visibility clothing. Foot protection is also required to prevent injuries from falling objects. Per 29 CFR 1926 Subpart E, employers must ensure that employees use appropriate PPE.

7.7 Communication and Signaling

  1. Clear and consistent communication between all personnel involved in lifting operations is essential. Standard hand signals or radio communication must be used, and all signals must be understood by both the rigger and the equipment operator. Per WAC 296-155-340, standard hand signals shall be used or a qualified signal person shall be provided.

7.8 Storage of Rigging Equipment

  1. Rigging equipment not in use shall be stored in a manner that prevents damage, corrosion, or degradation. Slings should be hung or placed on racks to prevent kinking or crushing. Proper storage extends the life of the equipment and ensures its readiness for safe use.

Training Requirements

All employees involved in rigging operations, including supervisors and foremen, must receive comprehensive training on safe rigging practices, equipment inspection, load calculation, and signaling. Training shall be conducted by a qualified person and documented. Refresher training shall be provided as necessary, but at least every three years. Per 29 CFR 1926.21(b)(2), employers shall instruct each employee in the recognition and avoidance of unsafe conditions and the regulations applicable to his work environment to control or eliminate any hazards or other exposure to illness or injury.

Inspection and Enforcement

Compliance with this rigging safety program will be monitored through regular job site inspections conducted by supervisors, foremen, and the Safety Officer. Inspections will verify proper equipment use, adherence to procedures, and overall safety. Any non-compliance will result in immediate corrective action, which may include retraining, disciplinary action, or removal from duties until compliance is achieved.

Recordkeeping

Records of all rigging equipment inspections, maintenance, and repairs shall be maintained by the Safety Officer for the lifespan of the equipment. Training records, including dates, attendees, and topics covered, shall be kept for a minimum of three years. These records are essential for demonstrating compliance and for continuous improvement of safety practices.

Violation Consequences

Any violation of this rigging safety policy will be subject to MH Construction, Inc.'s progressive disciplinary policy. Consequences may range from verbal warnings and retraining for minor infractions to written warnings, suspension, or termination for serious or repeated violations. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature of the violation, its potential for harm, and the employee's safety record.

References

• 29 CFR 1926 Subpart CC - Cranes and Derricks in Construction

• 29 CFR 1926.251 - Rigging Equipment for Material Handling

• WAC 296-155-336 through 296-155-340 - Rigging Requirements for Material Handling

• WAC 296-155-556 through 296-155-562 - Crane Rigging Requirements

• AGC Safety and Health Manual Ch. 15 - Cranes and Rigging

sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 36 | Hand and Portable Power Tools

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1926 Subpart I

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.300 General Requirements

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.301 Hand Tools

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.302 Power-Operated Hand Tools

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.303 Abrasive Wheels and Tools

WISHA

WAC 296-155-350

General Requirements

WISHA

WAC 296-155-355

Hand Tools

WISHA

WAC 296-155-360

Power-Operated Hand Tools

WISHA

WAC 296-155-365

Abrasive Wheels and Tools

WISHA

WAC 296-155-367

Masonry Saws

AGC

N/A

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 24 Tools

1. PURPOSE

This policy establishes clear guidelines for the safe use, maintenance, and inspection of hand and portable power tools. It aims to prevent injuries, property damage, and ensure compliance with all applicable safety regulations on MH Construction job sites.

Adherence to these procedures is critical for maintaining a safe working environment and protecting all personnel from potential hazards associated with tool operation.

2. SCOPE

This policy applies to all MH Construction employees, subcontractors, and visitors who may use or be exposed to hand and portable power tools on any MH Construction job site or property.

3. DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Hand Tool

Any tool powered manually, such as hammers, wrenches, screwdrivers, and chisels.

Portable Power Tool

Any tool powered by electricity, compressed air, hydraulic pressure, or internal combustion, designed to be easily moved and operated by hand, such as drills, saws, grinders, and nail guns.

Guarding

A barrier or enclosure designed to prevent contact with dangerous moving parts of machinery or tools.

Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)

Equipment worn to minimize exposure to hazards that cause serious workplace injuries and illnesses.

Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)

Procedures to ensure that dangerous machines are properly shut off and not able to be started up again prior to the completion of maintenance or servicing work.

4. RESPONSIBILITIES

  • Company/Management Responsibilities:
  • Provide safe tools and equipment.
  • Ensure proper training is available.
  • Conduct regular safety audits.
  • Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities:
  • Ensure tools are in good working order before use.
  • Verify employees are trained and competent in tool operation.
  • Enforce safety rules and PPE requirements.
  • Investigate all tool-related incidents.
  • Employee Responsibilities:
  • Use tools only for their intended purpose.
  • Inspect tools before each use and report defects.
  • Wear appropriate PPE.
  • Never remove safety guards or devices.
  • Ensure proper storage of tools.
  • Subcontractor Responsibilities:
  • Adhere to all MH Construction tool safety policies.
  • Provide their employees with safe tools and necessary training.
  • Ensure their tools meet all regulatory standards.

5. PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

5.1 General Requirements (29 CFR 1926.300; WAC 296-155-350)

  1. 5.1.1 All hand and power tools and similar equipment, whether furnished by the employer or the employee, shall be maintained in a safe condition. Defective tools shall be immediately removed from service. (Per 29 CFR 1926.300(a))
  2. 5.1.2 Belts, gears, shafts, pulleys, sprockets, spindles, drums, fly wheels, chains, or other reciprocating, rotating, or moving parts of equipment shall be guarded if such parts are exposed to contact by employees or otherwise create a hazard. (Per 29 CFR 1926.300(b)(2))
  3. 5.1.3 All controls for power-operated tools shall be located so as to prevent accidental operation. (Per WAC 296-155-350(2))
  4. 5.1.4 Tools shall be secured from falling when working at heights. (Per WAC 296-155-350(4))

5.2 Hand Tools (29 CFR 1926.301; WAC 296-155-355)

  1. 5.2.1 Employers shall not issue or permit the use of unsafe hand tools. (Per 29 CFR 1926.301(a))
  2. 5.2.2 Wrenches, including adjustable, pipe, end, and socket wrenches, shall not be used when jaws are sprung to the point that slippage occurs. (Per 29 CFR 1926.301(b))
  3. 5.2.3 Impact tools, such as drift pins, wedges, and chisels, shall be kept free of mushroomed heads. (Per 29 CFR 1926.301(c))
  4. 5.2.4 Wooden handles of tools shall be kept free of splinters or cracks and shall be kept tight in the tool. (Per WAC 296-155-355(2))

5.3 Power-Operated Hand Tools (29 CFR 1926.302; WAC 296-155-360)

  1. 5.3.1 All portable power tools shall be equipped with a guard or so designed as to prevent the operator from coming into contact with the moving parts. (Per 29 CFR 1926.302(a)(1))
  2. 5.3.2 Electric power operated tools shall be of the approved double-insulated type or be grounded. (Per 29 CFR 1926.302(a)(7))
  3. 5.3.3 Pneumatic power tools shall be secured to the hose or whip by some positive means to prevent the tool from accidentally disconnecting. (Per 29 CFR 1926.302(b)(1))
  4. 5.3.4 Compressed air shall not be used for cleaning purposes except where reduced to less than 30 p.s.i. and then only with effective chip guarding and PPE. (Per 29 CFR 1926.302(b)(4))
  5. 5.3.5 Powder-actuated tools shall be operated only by employees who have been trained in their use and who have been issued a qualified operator’s card. (Per 29 CFR 1926.302(e)(1))
  6. 5.3.6 All fuel-powered tools shall be stopped while being refueled, serviced, or maintained. (Per WAC 296-155-360(7))

5.4 Abrasive Wheels and Tools (29 CFR 1926.303; WAC 296-155-365)

  1. 5.4.1 Abrasive wheels shall be used only on machines provided with safety guards. (Per 29 CFR 1926.303(b)(1))
  2. 5.4.2 The maximum peripheral speed of the abrasive wheel shall not be exceeded. (Per 29 CFR 1926.303(c)(1))
  3. 5.4.3 Work rests on grinding machines shall be kept adjusted closely to the wheel with a maximum opening of one-eighth inch to prevent the work from being jammed between the wheel and the rest. (Per WAC 296-155-365(3))

5.5 Masonry Saws (WAC 296-155-367)

  1. 5.5.1 Masonry saws shall be guarded with a semicircular enclosure over the blade. (Per WAC 296-155-367(1))
  2. 5.5.2 A method for retaining the blade in the event of a blade failure shall be provided. (Per WAC 296-155-367(2))

6. TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

All employees who use hand and portable power tools must receive comprehensive training on their safe operation, maintenance, and associated hazards. This training will cover proper tool selection, inspection procedures, safe operating practices, and the correct use of PPE. Training frequency will be determined by job requirements and regulatory updates, with refresher training provided as needed. Documentation of all training, including dates and attendees, will be maintained by the Safety Officer. Specific training mandates from OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart I and WISHA WAC 296-155-350 through 367 will be incorporated.

7. INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

Compliance with this policy will be monitored through regular, documented inspections of tools and work practices by supervisors and the Safety Officer. Inspections will occur at least monthly and prior to the start of any new project. Any non-compliance or unsafe conditions will result in immediate corrective action, which may include retraining, repair or replacement of tools, or disciplinary measures. All incidents involving tools will be thoroughly investigated to determine root causes and implement preventive actions.

8. RECORDKEEPING

Records of tool inspections, maintenance, training, and incident investigations will be maintained by the Safety Officer for a minimum of five years. These records are crucial for demonstrating compliance with regulatory requirements and for continuous improvement of safety programs. Access to these records will be provided upon request to regulatory bodies.

9. VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

Any violation of this Hand and Portable Power Tools policy will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and gravity of the violation, the potential for harm, and the individual’s safety record. MH Construction is committed to maintaining a zero-tolerance policy for safety infractions that endanger personnel or property.

10. REFERENCES

  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926 Subpart I
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.300 General Requirements
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.301 Hand Tools
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.302 Power-Operated Hand Tools
  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.303 Abrasive Wheels and Tools
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-350 General Requirements
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-355 Hand Tools
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-360 Power-Operated Hand Tools
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-365 Abrasive Wheels and Tools
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-367 Masonry Saws
  • AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 24 Tools
