sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 37 | Concrete and Masonry Construction

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

1. Regulatory Authority

The following regulatory citations apply to this section:

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1926 Subpart Q

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.700 Scope

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.701 General Requirements

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.702 Requirements for Equipment and Tools

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.703 Cast-in-Place Concrete

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.706 Requirements for Masonry Construction

WISHA

WAC 296-155-675

Scope Application and Definitions

WISHA

WAC 296-155-680

General Provisions

WISHA

WAC 296-155-682

Requirements for Equipment and Tools

WISHA

WAC 296-155-684

Requirements for Cast-in-Place Concrete

WISHA

WAC 296-155-697

Requirements for Masonry Construction

AGC

Safety and Health Manual

25 Concrete and Masonry

2. Purpose

This policy establishes safety guidelines and procedures for concrete and masonry construction activities to prevent injuries, illnesses, and fatalities. It aims to protect all personnel from hazards associated with these operations, ensuring a safe working environment and compliance with regulatory standards.

3. Scope

This program applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors involved in or present on job sites where concrete and masonry construction activities are performed.

4. Definitions

Term

Definition

Concrete Pumping

The process of conveying liquid concrete through a hose or pipe system using a concrete pump.

Formwork

The temporary or permanent molds into which concrete or similar materials are poured.

Rebar (Reinforcing Bar)

A steel bar or mesh of steel wires used as a tension device in reinforced concrete and reinforced masonry structures to strengthen and aid the concrete under tension.

Shoring

A system of supports used to prevent the collapse of a structure or excavation.

Limited Access Zone

An area alongside a masonry wall under construction, established to protect employees from falling masonry units.

5. Responsibilities

5.1 Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Provide a safe work environment, necessary resources, and ensure compliance with all applicable regulations.
  • Develop, implement, and maintain this MISH program.
  • Conduct regular safety audits and reviews.

5.2 Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Ensure all concrete and masonry operations are conducted safely and in accordance with this program.
  • Provide site-specific training and enforce safety rules.
  • Conduct daily pre-task planning and safety briefings.
  • Inspect equipment and tools before use.

5.3 Employee Responsibilities

  • Adhere to all safety policies, procedures, and instructions.
  • Report any unsafe conditions, equipment, or practices immediately to their supervisor.
  • Use all required personal protective equipment (PPE).

5.4 Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Comply with all aspects of this MISH program and applicable OSHA/WISHA regulations.
  • Provide their employees with appropriate training and PPE.
  • Coordinate all concrete and masonry work with MH Construction site management.

6. Procedures / Requirements

6.1 General Requirements for Concrete and Masonry Construction

  1. All employees involved in concrete and masonry operations must be trained in the recognition and avoidance of hazards associated with the work. (Per 29 CFR 1926.701(a))
  2. Protruding reinforcing steel onto and into which employees could fall must be guarded to eliminate the hazard of impalement. (Per 29 CFR 1926.701(b))
  3. No employee shall be permitted to work where they are exposed to the hazards of falling objects from above. (Per WAC 296-155-680(1))
  4. Bulk cement storage bins and silos must be equipped with a fixed ladder or stairway, and a safe means of entry and exit. (Per 29 CFR 1926.701(c))

6.2 Equipment and Tools

  1. All concrete mixers must be equipped with a guard on the mixer drum opening. (Per 29 CFR 1926.702(a))
  2. Power concrete trowels must be equipped with a deadman control. (Per 29 CFR 1926.702(b))
  3. Concrete buggies and powered concrete carts must have a brake system and be operated by trained personnel. (Per WAC 296-155-682(1))

6.3 Formwork, Shoring, and Reshoring

  1. Formwork and shoring must be designed, fabricated, erected, supported, braced, and maintained so that it will safely support all vertical and lateral loads. (Per 29 CFR 1926.703(a)(1))
  2. Drawings or plans for the jack layout, formwork, shoring, working decks, and scaffolds must be available at the jobsite. (Per 29 CFR 1926.703(a)(2))
  3. Shoring and reshoring must not be removed until the concrete has gained sufficient strength to support its weight and superimposed loads. (Per WAC 296-155-684(2))

6.4 Precast Concrete

  1. Precast concrete members must be handled, stored, and erected so that they will not be subjected to excessive stresses and will not be damaged. (Per 29 CFR 1926.704(a))
  2. A designated person must inspect the lifting inserts and their attachment before the lift. (Per 29 CFR 1926.704(b))

6.5 Masonry Construction

  1. A limited access zone must be established whenever a masonry wall is being constructed. (Per 29 CFR 1926.706(a)(1))
  2. The limited access zone must be equal to the height of the wall plus 4 feet, and run the entire length of the wall. (Per 29 CFR 1926.706(a)(2))
  3. All masonry walls over 8 feet in height must be adequately braced to prevent overturning and to prevent collapse unless the wall is designed to be self-supporting. (Per WAC 296-155-697(1))

6.6 Concrete Pumping

  1. Concrete pumping systems must be set up and operated in accordance with the manufacturer's specifications. (Per AGC Safety and Health Manual Ch. 25)
  2. Hoses and pipes must be secured to prevent whipping or uncontrolled movement during pumping operations.

7. Training Requirements

All employees involved in concrete and masonry construction activities must receive training on the hazards associated with the work, safe work practices, and the proper use of equipment and PPE. Training must be conducted by a qualified person and documented. Retraining will occur as necessary, but at least every three years, or when new hazards or equipment are introduced. (Per 29 CFR 1926.21(b)(2) and WAC 296-155-005(1)(c))

8. Inspection and Enforcement

Supervisors will conduct daily inspections of concrete and masonry work areas, equipment, and procedures to ensure compliance with this program and all applicable regulations. Any deficiencies identified will be corrected immediately. Non-compliance by employees or subcontractors will result in corrective action as outlined in the 'Violation Consequences' section.

9. Recordkeeping

Records of all safety training, equipment inspections, and incident investigations related to concrete and masonry construction will be maintained by the Safety Officer for a minimum of five years. These records will be readily accessible for review by employees, regulatory agencies, and management.

10. Violation Consequences

Violations of this Concrete and Masonry Construction safety program will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and frequency of the violation, and the potential for harm. All disciplinary actions will be documented.

11. References

The following regulatory standards and guidelines were referenced in the development of this program:

  • 29 CFR 1926 Subpart Q - Concrete and Masonry Construction
  • 29 CFR 1926.700 - Scope, applications, and definitions applicable to this subpart
  • 29 CFR 1926.701 - General requirements
  • 29 CFR 1926.702 - Requirements for equipment and tools
  • 29 CFR 1926.703 - Cast-in-Place Concrete
  • 29 CFR 1926.706 - Requirements for masonry construction
  • WAC 296-155-675 - Scope, application and definitions
  • WAC 296-155-680 - General provisions
  • WAC 296-155-682 - Requirements for equipment and tools
  • WAC 296-155-684 - Requirements for cast-in-place concrete
  • WAC 296-155-697 - Requirements for masonry construction
  • AGC Safety and Health Manual Ch. 25 - Concrete and Masonry

sections.title: commercial drivers drug and prevention program
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

38 | Commercial Drivers Drug and Prevention Program

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

49 CFR Part 382

FMCSA Controlled Substances and Alcohol Use and Testing

OSHA

49 CFR Part 40

DOT Drug and Alcohol Testing Procedures

OSHA

49 CFR Part 391

Qualifications of Drivers

WISHA

WAC 296-155-110

Accident Prevention Program

WISHA

RCW 46.25

Commercial Driver's License

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 2 Drug and Alcohol Programs

AGC

FMCSA Employer Handbook

Purpose

This policy establishes guidelines for the Commercial Drivers Drug and Alcohol Prevention Program. It ensures compliance with federal and state regulations, safeguarding the well-being of employees and the public by preventing substance abuse among commercial drivers.

Scope

This program applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees who operate commercial motor vehicles (CMVs) requiring a Commercial Driver"s License (CDL), as well as any subcontractors or visitors on MHC job sites who fall under the purview of DOT drug and alcohol testing regulations.

Definitions

Term

Definition

Commercial Motor Vehicle (CMV)

A motor vehicle or combination of motor vehicles used in commerce to transport passengers or property if the vehicle has a gross combination weight rating or gross vehicle weight rating of 26,001 or more pounds, whichever is greater, or is designed to transport 16 or more passengers, including the driver, or is of any size and is used to transport hazardous materials.

Commercial Driver"s License (CDL)

A driver"s license required to operate large or heavy vehicles in commerce.

Designated Employer Representative (DER)

An employee authorized by the employer to take immediate action to remove employees from safety-sensitive duties, or cause employees to be removed from safety-sensitive duties, and to make required decisions in the testing process.

Safety-Sensitive Function

All time from the time a driver begins to work or is required to be in readiness to work until the time he/she is relieved from work and all responsibility for performing work.

Substance Abuse Professional (SAP)

A person who evaluates employees who have violated a DOT drug and alcohol program regulation and makes recommendations concerning education, treatment, follow-up testing, and aftercare.

Responsibilities

Company/Management Responsibilities

• Establish and maintain a comprehensive drug and alcohol testing program in compliance with 49 CFR Part 382 and 49 CFR Part 40.
• Designate a qualified Designated Employer Representative (DER) to oversee the program.
• Ensure all employees performing safety-sensitive functions are informed of the program"s policies and procedures.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

• Recognize and document signs and symptoms of drug and alcohol use that may indicate a need for reasonable suspicion testing.
• Ensure employees under their supervision comply with all aspects of the drug and alcohol program.
• Refer employees for testing as required by company policy and federal regulations.

Employee Responsibilities

• Comply with all drug and alcohol testing requirements, including pre-employment, random, post-accident, reasonable suspicion, and return-to-duty tests.
• Refrain from using alcohol or controlled substances while performing safety-sensitive functions or within the prohibited timeframes.
• Report any prescription medication use that may impair their ability to safely operate a CMV.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

• Ensure their employees operating CMVs on MHC job sites comply with all applicable DOT drug and alcohol testing regulations.
• Provide proof of compliance with drug and alcohol testing programs upon request by MH Construction, Inc.

Procedures / Requirements

7.1 Pre-Employment Testing

All prospective employees who will perform safety-sensitive functions must pass a DOT pre-employment drug test before beginning employment. This is a mandatory requirement per 49 CFR Part 382, Subpart C.

7.2 Random Testing

Employees performing safety-sensitive functions are subject to unannounced random drug and alcohol testing. The selection process is scientifically random and ensures each employee has an equal chance of being selected, as outlined in 49 CFR Part 382, Subpart C. The annual minimum random testing rates are established by the FMCSA.

7.3 Post-Accident Testing

Following an accident involving a CMV, drug and alcohol testing will be conducted if the accident involves a fatality, or if the driver receives a citation for a moving violation and the accident involves bodily injury with immediate medical treatment away from the scene or disabling damage to a vehicle. This is in accordance with 49 CFR Part 382, Subpart C.

7.4 Reasonable Suspicion Testing

Employees will be subject to drug and alcohol testing when a trained supervisor or company official observes specific, contemporaneous, articulable observations concerning the appearance, behavior, speech, or body odors of the employee. These observations must be documented as per 49 CFR Part 382, Subpart C.

7.5 Return-to-Duty and Follow-Up Testing

Any employee who has violated a DOT drug and alcohol regulation must successfully complete the return-to-duty process, including evaluation by a Substance Abuse Professional (SAP) and a negative return-to-duty test, before resuming safety-sensitive functions. They will then be subject to unannounced follow-up testing for a period of 12 to 60 months, as prescribed by the SAP, per 49 CFR Part 382, Subpart D.

7.6 Refusal to Test

Refusal to submit to any required drug or alcohol test will be treated as a positive test result and will result in immediate removal from safety-sensitive functions and disciplinary action, up to and including termination. This is consistent with 49 CFR Part 382, Subpart D.

7.7 Washington State Specific Requirements

In addition to federal regulations, MH Construction, Inc. adheres to Washington State requirements, including WAC 296-155-110 for Accident Prevention Programs, which emphasizes a safe and healthful work environment. While federal regulations primarily govern drug and alcohol testing for CDL holders, MHC ensures its overall safety program aligns with both federal and state mandates. RCW 46.25 outlines the Commercial Driver"s License requirements in Washington State.

Training Requirements

All employees performing safety-sensitive functions, and their supervisors, will receive training on the effects of drug and alcohol use, recognition of signs and symptoms of impairment, and the company"s drug and alcohol testing policies. Supervisors will receive additional training on reasonable suspicion determination. Training frequency will be as required by 49 CFR Part 382, Subpart F, and documented in employee training records.

Inspection and Enforcement

Compliance with this program will be monitored through regular audits of testing records, observation of employee conduct, and adherence to supervisory responsibilities. Any non-compliance will be subject to corrective action, up to and including disciplinary measures as outlined in the company"s progressive discipline policy.

Recordkeeping

MH Construction, Inc. will maintain all records related to the drug and alcohol testing program, including test results, training records, calibration of testing equipment, and Substance Abuse Professional (SAP) reports. These records will be retained for the periods specified in 49 CFR Part 382, Subpart D, and 49 CFR Part 40, and will be managed by the Designated Employer Representative (DER).

Violation Consequences

Violations of this policy, including positive test results or refusal to test, will result in immediate removal from safety-sensitive duties. Disciplinary actions will follow a progressive discipline policy, which may include suspension, mandatory SAP evaluation and treatment, and/or termination of employment, in accordance with federal regulations and company policy.

References

OSHA: 49 CFR Part 382 FMCSA Controlled Substances and Alcohol Use and Testing

OSHA: 49 CFR Part 40 DOT Drug and Alcohol Testing Procedures

OSHA: 49 CFR Part 391 Qualifications of Drivers

WISHA: WAC 296-155-110 Accident Prevention Program

WISHA: RCW 46.25 Commercial Driver's License

AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 2 Drug and Alcohol Programs

AGC: FMCSA Employer Handbook

sections.title: silica
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 39 | Silica Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey 1.  REGULATORY AUTHORITY Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 39 | Silica

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

1. REGULATORY AUTHORITY

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.1153

Respirable Crystalline Silica Construction

WISHA

WAC 296-155-176

Lead adopted silica provisions

WISHA

WAC 296-62-07521

Silica General Industry adopted by reference for construction

WISHA

WAC 296-155-160

Gases Vapors Fumes Dusts and Mists

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 26

Silica

AGC

AGC Silica Compliance Guide

2. PURPOSE

This policy establishes guidelines and procedures to protect employees from exposure to respirable crystalline silica. Its purpose is to prevent silicosis and other silica-related diseases by controlling exposure to airborne silica dust on MH Construction job sites.

3. SCOPE

This program applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors on MH Construction job sites where potential exposure to respirable crystalline silica exists.

4. DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Respirable Crystalline Silica (RCS)

Crystalline silica particles small enough to be inhaled deep into the lungs, typically less than 10 micrometers in diameter.

Action Level (AL)

A concentration of airborne respirable crystalline silica of 25 µg/m3 (micrograms per cubic meter of air) as an 8-hour time-weighted average (TWA).

Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL)

A concentration of airborne respirable crystalline silica of 50 µg/m3 as an 8-hour TWA.

Exposure Control Plan (ECP)

A written plan that identifies all silica-generating tasks and outlines engineering controls, work practices, and respiratory protection measures to reduce employee exposure.

Competent Person

An individual designated by the employer who is capable of identifying existing and foreseeable silica hazards and has the authority to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

5. RESPONSIBILITIES

5.1 Company/Management Responsibilities

• Develop, implement, and maintain a written Exposure Control Plan (ECP) for respirable crystalline silica. • Ensure adequate resources are available for compliance with this program. • Provide initial and annual training to employees exposed to silica above the action level. • Ensure medical surveillance is provided as required by OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1153 and WISHA WAC 296-62-07521.

5.2 Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

• Implement the ECP on job sites and ensure employees follow established procedures. • Conduct regular inspections to identify silica hazards and ensure controls are effective. • Provide site-specific training and ensure proper use of engineering controls, work practices, and respiratory protection. • Investigate any silica-related incidents or near misses and implement corrective actions.

5.3 Employee Responsibilities

• Comply with all provisions of the ECP and MISH Silica program. • Use engineering controls, work practices, and respiratory protection as required. • Report any concerns about silica exposure or equipment malfunctions to their supervisor. • Participate in required training and medical surveillance programs.

5.4 Subcontractor Responsibilities

• Adhere to MH Construction's Silica Exposure Control Plan and all applicable OSHA/WISHA regulations. • Provide their own written ECP if their scope of work involves silica-generating tasks. • Ensure their employees are properly trained and equipped to work safely with silica-containing materials.

6. PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

6.1 Exposure Assessment

1. MH Construction will assess employee exposure to respirable crystalline silica if it is, or can reasonably be expected to be, at or above the action level (AL) of 25 µg/m3 as an 8-hour TWA. 2. The assessment will be performed by a competent person and may involve air monitoring or objective data. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1153(d) and WISHA WAC 296-62-07521(d)).

6.2 Exposure Control Plan (ECP)

1. A written ECP will be developed and implemented for all tasks that involve exposure to respirable crystalline silica at or above the AL. 2. The ECP will identify all silica-generating tasks, engineering controls, work practices, and respiratory protection required. 3. The ECP will be reviewed and updated at least annually, and whenever changes in tasks or equipment could result in new or increased exposures. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1153(f) and WISHA WAC 296-62-07521(f)).

6.3 Engineering Controls and Work Practices

1. MH Construction will use engineering controls (e.g., water delivery systems, local exhaust ventilation) and work practices to reduce employee exposure to RCS to or below the PEL of 50 µg/m3. 2. When selecting controls, MH Construction will consider the hierarchy of controls: elimination, substitution, engineering controls, administrative controls, and personal protective equipment. 3. Specific controls for common tasks are outlined in Table 1 of OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1153 and WISHA WAC 296-62-07521, which will be followed unless air monitoring demonstrates exposures are below the PEL. 4. Housekeeping procedures will minimize employee exposure to RCS, avoiding dry sweeping or compressed air for cleaning unless other methods are not feasible and employees are protected. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1153(c) and WISHA WAC 296-62-07521(c)).

6.4 Respiratory Protection

1. Respiratory protection will be provided and used when engineering and work practice controls are not sufficient to reduce exposures to or below the PEL. 2. Respirators will be selected, fitted, used, and maintained in accordance with OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134 and WISHA WAC 296-842. 3. Employees required to wear respirators will be included in a comprehensive respiratory protection program. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1153(e) and WISHA WAC 296-62-07521(e)).

6.5 Regulated Areas

1. Regulated areas will be established where employee exposure to respirable crystalline silica is, or can reasonably be expected to be, in excess of the PEL. 2. Access to regulated areas will be limited to authorized persons. 3. Warning signs will be posted at the entrances to regulated areas. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1153(h) and WISHA WAC 296-62-07521(h)).

6.6 Medical Surveillance

1. Medical examinations will be made available at no cost to employees who are required to wear a respirator for 30 or more days per year due to silica exposure, or who have been exposed at or above the AL for 30 or more days per year. 2. Examinations will include a medical and work history, physical exam, chest X-ray, and lung function test. 3. These examinations will be provided initially and at least every three years thereafter, or more frequently if recommended by a physician. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1153(i) and WISHA WAC 296-62-07521(i)).

6.7 Communication of Hazards

1. MH Construction will ensure that employees are informed about the hazards associated with respirable crystalline silica and the measures implemented to protect them. 2. This includes providing access to the ECP, safety data sheets (SDS), and warning signs. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1153(j) and WISHA WAC 296-62-07521(j)).

7. TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

All employees who have potential exposure to respirable crystalline silica at or above the action level must receive training prior to or at the time of initial assignment and annually thereafter. Training will cover: health effects of silica, tasks that can result in exposure, specific control measures, proper use of respiratory protection, medical surveillance program, and the contents of OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1153 and WISHA WAC 296-62-07521. Training documentation will be maintained by the Safety Officer and include dates, attendees, and topics covered. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1153(k) and WISHA WAC 296-62-07521(k)).

8. INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

The Safety Officer and site supervisors will conduct regular inspections to monitor compliance with this Silica program and the ECP. Inspections will verify that engineering controls are functioning correctly, work practices are being followed, and respiratory protection is used appropriately. Any non-compliance or deficiencies will be documented, and corrective actions will be implemented promptly. Failure to adhere to the program may result in disciplinary action as outlined in the Violation Consequences section.

9. RECORDKEEPING

MH Construction will maintain records of: • Air monitoring data (for at least 30 years). • Objective data (for at least 30 years). • Medical surveillance records (duration of employment plus 30 years). • Training records (for 3 years). All records will be maintained by the Safety Officer and made available to employees, former employees, and their representatives upon request. (Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1153(n) and WISHA WAC 296-62-07521(n)).

10. VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

Violation of this Silica program or any related safety procedures will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and frequency of the violation, as well as the potential for harm. All disciplinary actions will be documented and communicated to the involved parties.

11. REFERENCES

• OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1153 – Respirable Crystalline Silica in Construction • OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134 – Respiratory Protection • WISHA WAC 296-155-176 – Lead adopted silica provisions • WISHA WAC 296-62-07521 – Silica General Industry adopted by reference for construction • WISHA WAC 296-155-160 – Gases Vapors Fumes Dusts and Mists • WISHA WAC 296-842 – Respiratory Protection • AGC Safety and Health Manual Ch. 26 – Silica • AGC Silica Compliance Guide

sections.title: forklift powered industrial truck safety
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 40 | Forklift / Powered Industrial Truck Safety Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 1.  Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1910.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 40 | Forklift / Powered Industrial Truck Safety

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1910.178 Powered Industrial Trucks

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.602(c) Lifting and Hauling Equipment

WISHA

WAC 296-863

Powered Industrial Trucks

WISHA

WAC 296-155-615

Material Handling Equipment

AGC

AGC: Safety

and Health Manual Ch. 13 Equipment Safety

2. Purpose

This policy establishes guidelines for the safe operation of forklifts and powered industrial trucks within MH Construction, Inc. It aims to prevent accidents, injuries, and property damage associated with their use, ensuring a secure working environment for all personnel.

3. Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors operating or working near forklifts and powered industrial trucks on any MH Construction job site.

4. Definitions

Term

Definition

Powered Industrial Truck (PIT)

A mobile, power-driven vehicle used to carry, push, pull, lift, stack, or tier material.

Forklift

A common type of powered industrial truck equipped with a forked platform for lifting and moving materials.

Operator

An individual who has been trained, evaluated, and authorized by MH Construction, Inc. to operate a powered industrial truck.

Load Capacity

The maximum weight a powered industrial truck is designed to safely lift and transport.

Stability Triangle

The area on a forklift defined by the three points of contact the truck makes with the ground, crucial for maintaining stability.

5. Responsibilities

  • 5.1 Company/Management Responsibilities
  • Ensure compliance with all applicable OSHA and WISHA regulations (e.g., 29 CFR 1910.178, WAC 296-863).
  • Provide safe and properly maintained powered industrial trucks.
  • Establish and enforce safe operating procedures.
  • Ensure operators receive adequate training and certification.
  • 5.2 Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities
  • Ensure operators are trained and authorized before operating PITs.
  • Monitor work areas for safe operation and adherence to procedures.
  • Conduct daily inspections of PITs and remove unsafe equipment from service.
  • Investigate all incidents involving PITs and implement corrective actions.
  • 5.3 Employee Responsibilities
  • Operate PITs only if trained, authorized, and physically capable.
  • Conduct pre-shift inspections and report any defects immediately.
  • Adhere to all safe operating procedures, including speed limits and load capacities.
  • Wear appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE).
  • 5.4 Subcontractor Responsibilities
  • Ensure their employees operating PITs are trained and certified to MH Construction, Inc. standards and applicable regulations.
  • Comply with all MH Construction, Inc. PIT safety policies and procedures.
  • Provide proof of operator training and equipment maintenance records upon request.

6. Procedures / Requirements

  1. 6.1 Operator Training and Certification

All operators of powered industrial trucks must complete a training program consisting of formal instruction, practical training, and evaluation (Per 29 CFR 1910.178(l) and WAC 296-863-20005). Only trained and authorized personnel are permitted to operate PITs.

  1. 6.2 Pre-Operational Inspection

Before each shift, operators must conduct a visual and operational inspection of the PIT. This includes checking tires, lights, horn, brakes, steering, fluid levels, and mast operation. Any defects must be reported immediately, and the PIT removed from service until repaired (Per 29 CFR 1910.178(q) and WAC 296-863-30005).

  1. 6.3 Safe Operating Practices

Operators must always maintain a safe speed, keep loads stable and within capacity, and look in the direction of travel. Stunt driving, horseplay, or operating a PIT in an unsafe manner is strictly prohibited (Per 29 CFR 1910.178(n) and WAC 296-863-40005).

  1. 6.4 Pedestrian Safety

Operators must yield to pedestrians and maintain a safe distance. Pedestrians must be aware of PIT operations, avoid blind spots, and never walk under raised forks or loads. Warning devices such as horns and lights must be used when approaching intersections or blind spots (Per 29 CFR 1910.178(n)(4) and WAC 296-863-40010).

  1. 6.5 Loading and Unloading

Loads must be centered, stable, and within the rated capacity of the PIT. Forks must be fully engaged under the load. When loading or unloading trailers, wheels must be chocked, and the trailer brakes set (Per 29 CFR 1910.178(o) and WAC 296-863-40015).

  1. 6.6 Battery Charging and Fueling

Battery charging and fueling operations must be conducted in designated areas with adequate ventilation and fire suppression equipment. Smoking and open flames are prohibited in these areas (Per 29 CFR 1910.178(g) and WAC 296-863-50005).

  1. 6.7 Maintenance and Repair

Only authorized and qualified personnel are permitted to perform maintenance and repair on PITs. PITs requiring repair must be tagged out of service (Per 29 CFR 1910.178(q) and WAC 296-863-30005).

7. Training Requirements

All forklift and powered industrial truck operators must receive initial training and certification before operating equipment. Refresher training and evaluation must be conducted at least every three years, or when an operator is observed operating unsafely, is involved in an accident, or assigned to a different type of truck (Per 29 CFR 1910.178(l)(4) and WAC 296-863-20010). Training records, including dates, content, and operator evaluations, must be maintained by the Safety Officer.

8. Inspection and Enforcement

Supervisors and Foremen are responsible for daily visual inspections of PITs and work areas. Unannounced inspections will be conducted periodically by the Safety Officer. Non-compliance with this policy will result in immediate corrective action, which may include retraining, disciplinary action, or removal from PIT operating duties.

9. Recordkeeping

The Safety Officer is responsible for maintaining records of operator training and certification, daily inspection logs, maintenance records, and incident reports. Training and certification records will be retained for the duration of the operator"s employment. Inspection and maintenance records will be retained for a minimum of three years.

10. Violation Consequences

Violations of this Forklift / Powered Industrial Truck Safety policy will be subject to MH Construction, Inc."s progressive disciplinary policy, which may include verbal warnings, written warnings, suspension, and termination of employment. Subcontractors found in violation may face contract termination.

11. References

29 CFR 1910.178 Powered Industrial Trucks

29 CFR 1926.602(c) Lifting and Hauling Equipment

WAC 296-863 Powered Industrial Trucks

WAC 296-155-615 Material Handling Equipment

AGC Safety and Health Manual Ch. 13 Equipment Safety

sections.title: distracted driving and fleet safety policy
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 41 | Distracted Driving and Fleet Safety Policy Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926. 600 Equipment General OSHA OSH Act General Duty Clause Section 5(a)(1) OSHA 49 CFR 392.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 41 | Distracted Driving and Fleet Safety Policy

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey



Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29

CFR 1926.600 Equipment General

OSHA

OSH

Act General Duty Clause Section 5(a)(1)

OSHA

49

CFR 392.82 FMCSA No Texting While Driving

WISHA

WAC

296-155-610 Motor Vehicles on Construction Sites

WISHA

RCW

46.61.672 Handheld Wireless Device Prohibition Washington State

AGC

Safety

and Health Manual Ch. 13

AGC

Travelers

Audit Recommendation 26-3 Fleet Safety

Purpose

This policy establishes guidelines to prevent distracted driving incidents and ensure the safe operation of all company and personal vehicles used for MH Construction business. It aims to protect employees, the public, and company assets from the risks associated with impaired attention while driving.

Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction employees, subcontractors, and visitors operating vehicles on behalf of MH Construction or on MH Construction job sites.

Definitions

Term

Definition

Distracted Driving

Any activity that diverts attention from the primary task of driving, including using a mobile device, eating, drinking, or engaging with passengers.

Fleet Vehicle

Any vehicle owned, leased, or rented by MH Construction for business purposes.

Personal Vehicle

An employee-owned vehicle used for company business.

Mobile Device

Any portable electronic device capable of transmitting or receiving data, including cell phones, smartphones, and tablets.

Hands-Free Device

A device that allows a mobile phone to be used without being held by hand, typically through Bluetooth or an integrated vehicle system.

Responsibilities

Company/Management:

  • - Provide resources for safe fleet operation and training.
  • - Establish and enforce clear policies regarding distracted driving.
  • - Ensure vehicles are maintained in safe operating condition.

Supervisor/Foreman:

  • - Monitor employee compliance with fleet safety policies.
  • - Address unsafe driving practices promptly.
  • - Ensure pre-trip inspections are conducted.

Employee:

  • - Adhere to all distracted driving and fleet safety policies.
  • - Operate vehicles safely and defensively.
  • - Report any vehicle defects or unsafe conditions immediately.
  • - Complete required training.

Subcontractor:

  • - Ensure their employees comply with all applicable distracted driving and fleet safety regulations.
  • - Maintain their vehicles in safe operating condition.

Procedures / Requirements

1. Prohibition of Mobile Device Use:

  1. 1.1. Employees are prohibited from using handheld mobile devices for talking, texting, or data entry while operating a vehicle for company business. This includes company-owned, leased, rented, or personal vehicles. Per 49 CFR 392.82 FMCSA, commercial motor vehicle drivers are specifically prohibited from texting while driving.
  2. 1.2. If a mobile device must be used, the vehicle must be safely pulled over and parked in an appropriate location. Per RCW 46.61.672, Washington State law prohibits holding a wireless device while driving.
  3. 1.3. Hands-free devices are permitted for voice communication only, provided their use does not cause distraction. Voice-to-text features are also prohibited while driving.

2. Vehicle Maintenance and Inspection:

  1. 2.1. All company vehicles must undergo regular maintenance as per manufacturer recommendations. Records of maintenance must be kept by the designated department. Per 29 CFR 1926.600, equipment must be in safe operating condition.
  2. 2.2. Employees operating company vehicles must conduct a pre-trip inspection at the beginning of each shift to ensure the vehicle is in safe working order (e.g., tires, brakes, lights, fluid levels). Any defects must be reported immediately.

3. Driver Qualifications:

  1. 3.1. All employees operating vehicles for company business must possess a valid driver's license for the class of vehicle being operated.
  2. 3.2. Employees must notify their supervisor immediately of any changes to their driver's license status (e.g., suspension, revocation).

4. Seatbelt Use:

  1. 4.1. All occupants of a vehicle used for company business must wear seatbelts at all times while the vehicle is in motion. Per WAC 296-155-610, motor vehicles on construction sites must ensure seatbelt use.

5. Impaired Driving:

  1. 5.1. Operating a vehicle under the influence of alcohol, illegal drugs, or impairing prescription medication is strictly prohibited. This is a violation of the OSH Act General Duty Clause Section 5(a)(1) and will result in immediate disciplinary action up to and including termination.

6. Accident Reporting:

  1. 6.1. All vehicle accidents, regardless of severity, involving a company vehicle or a personal vehicle used for company business, must be reported to a supervisor immediately. An accident report must be completed within 24 hours.

7. Load Securement:

  1. 7.1. All tools, equipment, and materials transported in or on a vehicle must be properly secured to prevent shifting, falling, or creating a hazard during transit. Per 29 CFR 1926.600, equipment must be operated safely.

Training Requirements

All employees who operate vehicles for company business must receive initial training on this Distracted Driving and Fleet Safety Policy. Training will cover safe driving practices, the dangers of distracted driving, and company-specific procedures. Training will be refreshed annually or as policy changes occur. Documentation of training completion will be maintained by the Safety Officer.

Inspection and Enforcement

Compliance with this policy will be monitored through regular observations by supervisors and management. Unsafe driving practices or policy violations will be addressed immediately. Corrective actions may include retraining, verbal warnings, written warnings, and other disciplinary measures as outlined in the Violation Consequences section.

Recordkeeping

The following records will be maintained by the Safety Officer for a minimum of five years:

  • - Driver's license verification records.
  • - Vehicle maintenance and inspection logs.
  • - Accident reports.
  • - Employee training records.

Violation Consequences

Violations of this Distracted Driving and Fleet Safety Policy will result in progressive disciplinary action, which may include:

  • - First Offense: Verbal warning and retraining.
  • - Second Offense: Written warning and potential temporary suspension of driving privileges for company business.
  • - Third Offense: Further disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment and permanent revocation of driving privileges for company business.

Severe violations, such as driving under the influence, may result in immediate termination.

References

OSHA:

  • - 29 CFR 1926.600 Equipment General
  • - OSH Act General Duty Clause Section 5(a)(1)
  • - 49 CFR 392.82 FMCSA No Texting While Driving

WISHA:

  • - WAC 296-155-610 Motor Vehicles on Construction Sites
  • - RCW 46.61.672 Handheld Wireless Device Prohibition Washington State

AGC:

  • - Safety and Health Manual Ch. 13
  • - Travelers Audit Recommendation 26-3 Fleet Safety

sections.title: cold weather and winter operations safety
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 42 | Cold Weather and Winter Operations Safety Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 42 | Cold Weather and Winter Operations Safety

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.20

OSHA

OSH Act General Duty Clause Section 5(a)(1)

Employer must provide a workplace free from recognized hazards

WISHA

WAC 296-155-110

Accident Prevention Program

WISHA

WAC 296-62-095

Temperature Extremes Cold Stress

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 19 Environmental Hazards

1. Purpose

The purpose of this policy is to establish comprehensive guidelines and procedures for protecting employees from the hazards associated with cold weather and winter operations. This policy ensures that all personnel are equipped, trained, and monitored to prevent cold stress illnesses such as hypothermia, frostbite, and trench foot, while maintaining operational efficiency in adverse environmental conditions.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, vendors, and visitors who are exposed to cold weather conditions on any company job site or facility. It encompasses all operations conducted in environments where the temperature, wind chill, or dampness presents a risk of cold-related injury or illness.

3. Definitions

Term

Definition

Cold Stress

A condition that occurs when the body is unable to warm itself, leading to a drop in core temperature. Includes hypothermia, frostbite, and trench foot.

Wind Chill

The perceived decrease in air temperature felt by the body on exposed skin due to the flow of air.

Hypothermia

A potentially fatal condition where the body's core temperature drops below 95°F (35°C).

Frostbite

An injury to the body caused by freezing of the skin and underlying tissues, most common on extremities.

Trench Foot

An injury of the feet resulting from prolonged exposure to wet and cold conditions, which can occur at temperatures as high as 60°F if feet are constantly wet.

4. Responsibilities

4.1 Company/Management

  • Provide necessary resources, including funding for PPE and warming areas.
  • Ensure the Cold Weather Safety Program is implemented and enforced across all sites.
  • Monitor weather forecasts and issue company-wide alerts for extreme cold events.

4.2 Supervisor/Foreman

  • Conduct daily hazard assessments regarding weather conditions.
  • Ensure employees are wearing appropriate cold-weather PPE.
  • Schedule work/rest cycles based on temperature and wind chill factors.
  • Monitor workers for signs and symptoms of cold stress.

4.3 Employees

  • Wear required cold-weather PPE and dress in appropriate layers.
  • Report any signs or symptoms of cold stress in themselves or coworkers immediately.
  • Follow established work/rest schedules and utilize warming areas.

4.4 Subcontractors

  • Comply with all requirements of this MISH policy.
  • Provide their own employees with appropriate cold-weather PPE and training.

5. Procedures and Requirements

5.1 Hazard Assessment and Monitoring

  1. Supervisors must monitor weather conditions daily using reliable forecasts. Per WAC 296-62-095, employers must evaluate the workplace to determine if employees are exposed to cold stress hazards. Wind chill temperatures must be calculated to determine the actual risk level.

5.2 Work/Rest Schedules

  1. When temperatures drop below freezing, supervisors must implement work/rest schedules. Workers must be provided access to heated warming shelters. The frequency and duration of rest breaks must increase as the temperature and wind chill decrease.

5.3 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)

  1. Employees must dress in layers: an inner layer that wicks moisture, a middle layer for insulation, and an outer layer that is wind and waterproof. Insulated boots, gloves, and head protection are required. Hard hat liners must be compatible with the hard hat and not compromise its protective rating.

5.4 Hydration and Nutrition

  1. Employees must consume warm, sweet beverages and avoid caffeine and alcohol, which can increase heat loss. Adequate caloric intake is necessary to maintain body heat production.

5.5 Slip, Trip, and Fall Prevention

  1. Per 29 CFR 1926.20, the workplace must be free from recognized hazards. Ice and snow must be cleared from walkways, scaffolding, and work areas. Sand or ice melt must be applied to slippery surfaces. Footwear must have adequate traction.

5.6 Equipment and Machinery Operation

  1. Equipment must be inspected for cold-weather operability. Fluids and lubricants must be rated for winter use. Operators must allow machinery to warm up before use and be aware of reduced visibility due to frosted or fogged windows.

5.7 Emergency Response for Cold Stress

  1. If hypothermia or frostbite is suspected, immediately move the affected person to a warm area. Remove wet clothing. Do not rub frostbitten areas. Seek immediate medical attention. This aligns with the requirements of the WAC 296-155-110 Accident Prevention Program.

5.8 Driving and Transportation

  1. Company vehicles must be equipped with winter survival kits (blankets, flashlight, flares, shovel). Tires must have adequate tread. Drivers must adjust speed for road conditions and maintain increased following distances.

6. Training Requirements

All employees exposed to cold weather conditions must receive training prior to initial assignment and annually thereafter. Training must cover the recognition of cold stress symptoms, prevention methods, proper use of PPE, safe work practices, and emergency response procedures. Training records must be documented and maintained by the Safety Officer.

7. Inspection and Enforcement

Supervisors will conduct daily site inspections during winter operations to ensure compliance with this policy. The Safety Officer will conduct periodic audits. Non-compliance, such as failure to wear required PPE or ignoring work/rest schedules, will result in immediate corrective action.

8. Recordkeeping

The Safety Officer is responsible for maintaining all records related to this policy, including training rosters, incident reports related to cold stress, and daily hazard assessments. Records will be retained for a minimum of five (5) years or as required by specific OSHA/WISHA regulations.

9. Violation Consequences

Violations of this policy will be subject to the company's progressive discipline policy, which may include verbal warnings, written reprimands, suspension, or termination, depending on the severity of the violation and the risk posed to employee safety.

10. References

  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.20 -
  • OSHA: OSH Act General Duty Clause Section 5(a)(1) - Employer must provide a workplace free from recognized hazards
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-110 - Accident Prevention Program
  • WISHA: WAC 296-62-095 - Temperature Extremes Cold Stress
  • AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 19 Environmental Hazards -

sections.title: general waste management
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 43 | General Waste Management Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey REGULATORY CITATIONS A clearly labeled "Regulatory Authority" section Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 43 | General Waste Management

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

REGULATORY CITATIONS

A clearly labeled "Regulatory Authority" section

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.25 Housekeeping Waste Disposal

OSHA

40 CFR

Parts 260-262 EPA RCRA Hazardous Waste

WISHA

WAC 296-155-020

Housekeeping

WISHA

WAC 296-155-335

Disposal of Waste Materials

WISHA

WAC 173-303

Washington Dangerous Waste Regulations Ecology

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 3 Site Management

PURPOSE

This policy establishes guidelines for the proper management and disposal of waste materials generated at MH Construction job sites. It aims to prevent environmental contamination, ensure worker safety, and maintain compliance with all applicable federal, state, and local regulations regarding waste handling and disposal.

SCOPE

This policy applies to all MH Construction employees, subcontractors, and visitors present on MH Construction job sites. Adherence to these procedures is mandatory to ensure a safe and compliant work environment.

DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Hazardous Waste

Waste materials that pose a substantial or potential hazard to human health or the environment when improperly managed. Defined by 40 CFR Parts 260-262 and WAC 173-303.

Non-Hazardous Waste

Waste materials that do not meet the definition of hazardous waste, including general construction debris, office waste, and recyclable materials.

Waste Stream

The complete flow of waste from its source through generation, collection, transportation, processing, and disposal.

Housekeeping

Activities involved in maintaining a clean, orderly, and safe work environment, including the prompt removal of waste and debris.

Disposal

The final placement or destruction of waste materials, typically in a landfill, incinerator, or recycling facility.

RESPONSIBILITIES

Company/Management Responsibilities

• Establish and maintain a comprehensive waste management program.
• Provide necessary resources, training, and equipment for safe waste handling.
• Ensure compliance with all federal, state, and local waste disposal regulations.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

• Implement and enforce waste management procedures on job sites.
• Conduct regular inspections to ensure proper waste segregation and disposal.
• Provide site-specific training and guidance to employees and subcontractors.

Employee Responsibilities

• Adhere to all waste segregation and disposal procedures.
• Report any unsafe waste conditions or practices to their supervisor.
• Participate in waste management training as required.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

• Comply with all MH Construction waste management policies and procedures.
• Manage and dispose of their generated waste in accordance with project requirements and regulations.
• Coordinate waste removal with MH Construction site management.

PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

7.1 Waste Segregation

1. All waste materials shall be segregated at the point of generation into designated containers for hazardous, non-hazardous, recyclable, and compostable materials.
2. Clearly label all waste containers according to their contents. Per 29 CFR 1926.25(c), containers for waste shall be provided and maintained in a sanitary condition.
3. Do not mix hazardous waste with non-hazardous waste. Refer to WAC 173-303 for Washington Dangerous Waste Regulations Ecology.

7.2 Hazardous Waste Management

1. Identify and classify all hazardous waste in accordance with 40 CFR Parts 260-262 EPA RCRA Hazardous Waste and WAC 173-303.
2. Store hazardous waste in appropriate, clearly marked containers in a secure area, away from ignition sources and incompatible materials.
3. Ensure hazardous waste containers are kept closed except when adding or removing waste. Per 29 CFR 1926.25(a), debris shall be kept cleared from work areas, passageways, and stairs.

7.3 Non-Hazardous Waste Disposal

1. Non-hazardous construction debris (e.g., wood, drywall, concrete) shall be collected in designated dumpsters or roll-off containers.
2. General refuse (e.g., food waste, packaging) shall be placed in covered trash receptacles to prevent pest attraction and odor. Per WAC 296-155-020, all places of employment shall be kept clean and orderly.
3. Ensure regular removal of non-hazardous waste from the job site to prevent accumulation and maintain clear egress. Per WAC 296-155-335, waste materials shall be disposed of in a manner that does not create a hazard.

7.4 Recycling Program

1. Implement a recycling program for materials such as cardboard, plastics, metals, and clean wood scraps where feasible.
2. Provide clearly marked recycling bins and educate personnel on proper recycling practices.
3. Coordinate with local recycling facilities for collection and processing of recyclable materials.

7.5 Spill Prevention and Response

1. Maintain spill kits on site, readily accessible for immediate response to spills of hazardous materials.
2. Train personnel on spill prevention techniques and proper spill response procedures, including the use of personal protective equipment (PPE).
3. Report all spills, regardless of size, to the site supervisor immediately for appropriate action and documentation.

7.6 Waste Transportation

1. All waste transported off-site must be handled by licensed and approved waste haulers.
2. Ensure proper manifests and documentation accompany hazardous waste shipments, as required by federal and state regulations.
3. Verify that waste disposal facilities are properly permitted and authorized to receive the specific type of waste being transported.

TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

• All employees involved in waste handling, segregation, or disposal must receive initial and annual refresher training on this policy and relevant regulatory requirements.
• Training shall cover identification of hazardous waste, proper handling procedures, use of PPE, emergency response to spills, and recordkeeping.
• Documentation of all training sessions, including attendance records and topics covered, shall be maintained by the Safety Officer. Per 29 CFR 1926.21(b)(2), employers shall instruct each employee in the recognition and avoidance of unsafe conditions and the regulations applicable to his work environment to control or eliminate any hazards or other exposure to illness or injury.

INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

• The Safety Officer or designated supervisor will conduct weekly inspections of all job sites to ensure compliance with waste management procedures.
• Any non-compliance or deficiencies identified during inspections will be documented, and corrective actions will be implemented promptly.
• Repeated non-compliance or serious violations may result in disciplinary action as outlined in the Violation Consequences section.

RECORDKEEPING

• The Safety Officer is responsible for maintaining all records related to waste management, including training records, inspection reports, waste manifests, and disposal receipts.
• Records shall be retained for a minimum of three years, or longer as required by specific regulatory citations (e.g., hazardous waste manifests).

VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

• Non-compliance with this policy may result in disciplinary action, ranging from verbal warnings to termination of employment or contract, depending on the severity and frequency of the violation.
• Violations that lead to environmental damage or regulatory fines may result in more severe penalties, including legal action.

REFERENCES

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.25 Housekeeping Waste Disposal; 40 CFR Parts 260-262 EPA RCRA Hazardous Waste
• WISHA: WAC 296-155-020 Housekeeping; WAC 296-155-335 Disposal of Waste Materials; WAC 173-303 Washington Dangerous Waste Regulations Ecology
• AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 3 Site Management

sections.title: struck by and caught in between hazard prevention
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 44 | Struck-By and Caught-In/Between Hazard Prevention Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey ―――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――― Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart O OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 44 | Struck-By and Caught-In/Between Hazard Prevention

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey


――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――――

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1926 Subpart O

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.600 Equipment General

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.20

OSHA

OSH Act General Duty Clause Section 5(a)(1)

OSHA Fatal Four - General Duty Clause

WISHA

WAC 296-155-610

Motor Vehicles on Construction Sites

WISHA

WAC 296-880

Fall Protection overhead hazards

WISHA

WAC 296-155-035

General Requirements safe place standards

AGC

Chapter 27

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 27 Struck-By and Caught-In Hazards

Purpose

This policy establishes comprehensive guidelines and procedures to proactively prevent injuries and fatalities resulting from struck-by and caught-in/between hazards within all MH Construction operations. It aims to safeguard all personnel on MH Construction job sites by ensuring strict adherence to regulatory standards and fostering a robust, proactive safety culture that prioritizes hazard identification and mitigation.

Scope

This policy is universally applicable to all MH Construction employees, subcontractors, and any visitors present on MH Construction job sites. Strict adherence to these guidelines is a mandatory requirement for maintaining a secure and compliant working environment, thereby minimizing risks associated with struck-by and caught-in/between incidents.

Definitions

Term

Definition

Struck-By Hazard

An incident where a person sustains an injury from forcible contact or impact with an object or piece of equipment, such as falling tools, moving vehicles, or swinging loads.

Caught-In/Between Hazard

An incident where a person is squeezed, caught, crushed, pinched, or compressed between two or more objects, or between parts of an object, leading to injury. This includes being caught in machinery, between moving parts, or in collapsing structures.

Competent Person

An individual designated by MH Construction who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who possesses the authority to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them, as defined by OSHA 29 CFR 1926.32(f).

Exclusion Zone

A clearly delineated and controlled area established around operational equipment, material hoisting activities, or other identified high-risk zones where entry is strictly restricted to authorized and essential personnel only. This measure is critical for preventing struck-by or caught-in/between incidents.

Heavy Equipment

Any large-scale machinery utilized in construction activities, including but not limited to excavators, bulldozers, cranes, forklifts, and loaders. These machines inherently pose significant struck-by and caught-in/between risks due to their size, power, and operational dynamics.

Situational Awareness

The ability to identify, process, and comprehend the critical elements of information about what is happening with regards to the mission. More simply, it's knowing what is going on around you in the context of the work environment and potential hazards.

Responsibilities

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Establish and maintain a safe work environment, providing all necessary resources, equipment, and training to effectively prevent struck-by and caught-in/between hazards across all job sites.
  • Ensure that all employees receive comprehensive and documented training on hazard recognition, safe work practices, and emergency procedures specific to struck-by and caught-in/between risks.
  • Conduct regular, documented site inspections and audits to proactively identify, assess, and mitigate potential hazards, ensuring compliance with all applicable OSHA, WISHA, and company safety standards.
  • Implement and enforce disciplinary actions for non-compliance with safety policies, ensuring accountability at all levels of the organization.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Rigorously enforce all safety policies and procedures, particularly those pertaining to struck-by and caught-in/between hazards, ensuring consistent application on site.
  • Conduct daily Job Hazard Analyses (JHAs) and pre-task planning meetings to identify, evaluate, and implement controls for all potential hazards before work commences.
  • Verify the proper selection, use, and maintenance of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) and other safety devices by all personnel under their supervision.
  • Provide clear, concise instructions and direct supervision to employees working near heavy equipment, in excavations, or in any areas presenting potential struck-by or caught-in/between hazards.
  • Ensure all equipment operators are certified and competent, and that equipment is inspected daily and maintained in safe operating condition.

Employee Responsibilities

  • Strictly adhere to all MH Construction safety rules, established procedures, and instructions provided by supervisors and management.
  • Immediately report any observed unsafe conditions, practices, or equipment malfunctions to their direct supervisor without delay.
  • Consistently and correctly use all required PPE and safety equipment as mandated for specific tasks and work environments.
  • Maintain acute situational awareness at all times, especially when working in proximity to moving equipment, overhead activities, or in areas with potential for falling objects or pinch points.
  • Participate actively in all safety training programs and pre-task planning sessions.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Fully comply with all MH Construction safety policies and procedures, as well as all applicable federal (OSHA) and state (WISHA) regulations pertaining to struck-by and caught-in/between hazard prevention.
  • Ensure that all their employees are adequately trained, certified, and equipped to perform their assigned tasks safely and in accordance with industry best practices.
  • Actively coordinate with MH Construction supervisors to identify, assess, and implement effective control measures for all potential hazards on shared work sites, particularly those involving heavy equipment and material handling.

Procedures / Requirements

7.1 Site-Specific Hazard Assessment and Planning

Prior to the commencement of any work activity, a comprehensive site-specific hazard assessment shall be meticulously conducted by a designated competent person. This assessment will systematically identify all potential struck-by and caught-in/between hazards, encompassing, but not limited to: the movement of heavy equipment, potential for falling objects, excavation activities, rotating machinery, and confined spaces. The findings of this assessment will inform the development of a detailed Job Hazard Analysis (JHA) and a site-specific safety plan, in accordance with OSH Act General Duty Clause Section 5(a)(1).

7.2 Establishment of Exclusion Zones and Barricading

Clearly defined and rigorously enforced exclusion zones shall be established around all areas where heavy equipment is operating, materials are being hoisted, or other identified struck-by/caught-in/between hazards are present. These zones must be demarcated using physical barriers such as barricades, caution tape, and high-visibility signage. Access to these zones shall be strictly limited to authorized personnel only, as per 29 CFR 1926.20. Spotters or signal persons are mandatory when equipment operates near these zones or personnel.

7.3 Safe Operation of Vehicles and Heavy Equipment

All vehicles and heavy equipment utilized on MH Construction sites must undergo daily pre-operation inspections and be maintained in optimal working condition. Only operators who are certified, competent, and specifically authorized for the equipment they are operating are permitted. When operating equipment in areas with restricted visibility, near personnel, or during backing maneuvers, the use of trained spotters or signal persons is mandatory to prevent incidents. All operations must comply with 29 CFR 1926.600 Equipment General and WAC 296-155-610 Motor Vehicles on Construction Sites.

7.4 Prevention of Overhead Hazards and Falling Objects

Robust measures shall be implemented to prevent falling objects from overhead work areas. These measures include, but are not limited to, the installation of toe boards, debris nets, catch platforms, and the secure stacking and storage of materials. All tools and materials at height must be secured to prevent accidental dislodgement. Employees working below overhead operations must wear appropriate head protection (hard hats). These practices are in accordance with WAC 296-880 Fall Protection overhead hazards.

7.5 Trenching, Excavation, and Shoring Safety

All trenches and excavations 5 feet or deeper must be protected by an engineered protective system (sloping, shoring, or shielding) unless the excavation is made entirely in stable rock. A competent person must inspect excavations daily and after any rainstorm or other hazard-increasing event. Employees are strictly prohibited from working in unprotected trenches. Adequate means of egress, such as ladders or ramps, must be provided within 25 feet of employees in trenches 4 feet or more in depth. Compliance with 29 CFR 1926 Subpart P - Excavations is mandatory.

7.6 Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) Procedures

Comprehensive energy control procedures (Lockout/Tagout) shall be implemented for all equipment maintenance, servicing, or repair activities. This is to prevent the unexpected startup, energization, or release of stored energy, which could lead to severe caught-in/between incidents. All affected employees must be trained on LOTO procedures, and equipment must be de-energized, locked out, and tagged out before any work begins, as required by 29 CFR 1910.147 - The Control of Hazardous Energy (Lockout/Tagout).

7.7 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) Requirements

All employees shall be provided with, trained in the use of, and required to wear appropriate Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) to safeguard against identified struck-by and caught-in/between hazards. This includes, but is not limited to: hard hats (ANSI Z89.1), high-visibility clothing (ANSI/ISEA 107), safety glasses or goggles (ANSI Z87.1), safety footwear (ANSI Z41), and gloves. The selection and use of PPE must comply with 29 CFR 1926 Subpart E - Personal Protective and Life Saving Equipment.

7.8 Safe Material Handling and Storage Practices

Materials on job sites must be stored, stacked, and secured in a manner that prevents shifting, falling, or collapsing, thereby eliminating potential struck-by or caught-in/between hazards. Mechanical equipment used for material handling must only be operated by trained and authorized personnel. Clear and unobstructed pathways, aisles, and access routes must be maintained at all times. Compliance with 29 CFR 1926.250 - General Requirements for Storage is essential.

7.9 Working in Proximity to Power Lines and Utilities

When work activities are conducted near overhead power lines or underground utilities, strict adherence to minimum safe clearance distances is mandatory. Prior to any work, utility companies must be contacted to identify and mark all utility locations. If maintaining minimum clearance from overhead power lines is not feasible, the power company must be contacted to de-energize or insulate the lines. All operations must comply with 29 CFR 1926.1408 - Power line safety (up to 350 kV) - equipment operations and other relevant utility regulations.

7.10 Emergency Preparedness and Response

Comprehensive emergency procedures, including detailed plans for first aid, rescue operations, and site evacuation, must be developed, clearly communicated, and regularly practiced by all personnel. Emergency contact information, first aid kits, and other essential emergency equipment must be readily accessible and clearly marked at all job sites. These procedures are critical for minimizing harm in the event of an incident and align with WAC 296-155-035 General Requirements safe place standards.

Training Requirements

All employees whose job functions or work environments expose them to potential struck-by and caught-in/between hazards shall receive comprehensive initial training and periodic refresher training as deemed necessary by the Safety Officer. Training curriculum will encompass: thorough hazard recognition, implementation of safe work practices, correct selection and use of PPE, and detailed emergency response procedures. All training sessions, including dates, topics covered, and attendee rosters, shall be meticulously documented and maintained by the Safety Officer in accordance with 29 CFR 1926.21 - Safety training and education and WAC 296-155-035 General Requirements safe place standards.

Inspection and Enforcement

Supervisors are mandated to conduct daily informal inspections of their respective work areas to identify and address immediate hazards. The Safety Officer will conduct formal, documented site inspections on a weekly basis to ensure ongoing compliance with this policy and all applicable regulatory standards. Any identified non-compliance or unsafe condition will necessitate immediate corrective action. Repeated or severe violations will trigger progressive disciplinary action as explicitly outlined in the 'Violation Consequences' section of this manual.

Recordkeeping

The following critical records shall be meticulously maintained for a minimum retention period of five (5) years by the Safety Officer or designated personnel:

  • Site-specific hazard assessments and Job Hazard Analyses (JHAs).
  • Comprehensive employee safety training records, including dates, topics, and attendance.
  • All formal and informal inspection reports, including details of identified hazards and implemented corrective actions.
  • Incident investigation reports, particularly those related to struck-by and caught-in/between incidents, near misses, and disciplinary actions.
  • Equipment maintenance and inspection logs.

Violation Consequences

Non-compliance with the provisions of this policy will result in the application of a progressive disciplinary action framework. The severity of the disciplinary action, which may range from verbal warnings to immediate termination of employment or contract, will be determined based on the nature, severity, frequency, and potential impact of the violation. All disciplinary actions will be thoroughly documented, reviewed by senior management and the Safety Officer, and communicated to the involved parties.

References

  • 29 CFR 1926 Subpart O - Motor Vehicles, Mechanized Equipment, and Marine Operations
  • 29 CFR 1926.600 - Equipment General
  • 29 CFR 1926.20 - General Safety and Health Provisions
  • OSH Act General Duty Clause Section 5(a)(1) - General Duty Clause
  • WAC 296-155-610 - Motor Vehicles on Construction Sites
  • WAC 296-880 - Fall Protection overhead hazards
  • WAC 296-155-035 - General Requirements safe place standards
  • AGC Safety and Health Manual Ch. 27 Struck-By and Caught-In Hazards
  • 29 CFR 1926 Subpart P - Excavations
  • 29 CFR 1910.147 - The Control of Hazardous Energy (Lockout/Tagout)
  • 29 CFR 1926 Subpart E - Personal Protective and Life Saving Equipment
  • 29 CFR 1926.250 - General Requirements for Storage
  • 29 CFR 1926.1408 - Power line safety (up to 350 kV) - equipment operations
  • 29 CFR 1926.21 - Safety training and education
