sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 45 | Miscellaneous Safety Requirements

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1926 Subpart C

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.50 Medical Services and First Aid

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.51 Sanitation

WISHA

WAC 296-155-035

General Requirements

WISHA

WAC 296-155-040

Safe Place Standards

WISHA

WAC 296-155-120

through 296-155-140

AGC

Jobsite Safety Handbook

Purpose

This policy establishes guidelines to ensure a safe and healthy working environment for all personnel involved in MH Construction projects. It aims to prevent injuries, illnesses, and property damage by outlining essential safety requirements.

Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction employees, subcontractors, and visitors on MH Construction job sites. Adherence to these requirements is mandatory to maintain compliance with regulatory standards and company safety objectives.

Definitions

Term

Definition

First Aid

Immediate care given to an injured or ill person before professional medical help arrives.

Sanitation

Measures taken to maintain public health, including cleanliness and waste disposal.

Competent Person

One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

Medical Services

Provision of healthcare and emergency response for workplace injuries and illnesses.

Job Site

Any location where MH Construction or its subcontractors are performing work.

Responsibilities

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Establish and maintain a comprehensive safety program. Provide necessary resources for safety training and equipment. Ensure compliance with all applicable federal, state, and local safety regulations.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Implement and enforce safety policies on job sites. Conduct regular safety inspections and toolbox talks. Investigate all incidents and accidents, ensuring corrective actions are taken.

Employee Responsibilities

  • Adhere to all safety rules and procedures. Report hazards, injuries, and near misses immediately. Use personal protective equipment (PPE) as required and participate in safety training.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Comply with MH Construction's safety program and all regulatory requirements. Provide their employees with necessary safety training and equipment. Coordinate safety efforts with MH Construction site supervision.

Procedures / Requirements

7.1 Medical Services and First Aid

  1. MH Construction shall ensure that medical personnel are readily available for advice and consultation on matters of occupational health. Provisions shall be made for prompt medical attention in case of serious injury. Per 29 CFR 1926.50(b), a person certified in first aid shall be available at the worksite. First aid supplies shall be readily accessible and maintained in a sanitary, usable condition. Per WAC 296-155-040(1)(b), a first-aid station shall be provided at all worksites.

7.2 Sanitation - Potable Water

  1. An adequate supply of potable water shall be provided in all places of employment. Per 29 CFR 1926.51(a)(1), potable water shall be provided for drinking, washing, and cooking purposes. Drinking water shall be dispensed from a fountain, a covered container with a tap, or single-service cups. Common drinking cups are prohibited. Per WAC 296-155-120(1), potable water shall be provided at all construction sites.

7.3 Sanitation - Non-potable Water

  1. Non-potable water may be used for purposes such as fire protection and flushing toilets, provided it is clearly marked as 'Non-potable Water' and does not present a hazard to employees. No connection shall be made between a system furnishing potable water and a system furnishing non-potable water. Per 29 CFR 1926.51(b)(1), non-potable water systems must be identified.

7.4 Sanitation - Toilets and Handwashing Facilities

  1. Adequate toilet facilities shall be provided for all employees. These facilities shall be maintained in a sanitary condition and be readily accessible. Per 29 CFR 1926.51(c)(1), at least one toilet facility shall be provided for every 20 employees. Handwashing facilities, including soap and water or suitable cleansing agents, shall be available. Per WAC 296-155-130(1), toilet facilities must be provided and maintained.

7.5 Sanitation - Food Handling

  1. All food service facilities and operations shall be carried out in accordance with sound hygienic principles. Food shall be protected from contamination. Waste disposal containers shall be provided and emptied regularly. Per 29 CFR 1926.51(d)(1), all employees' food and beverages shall be protected from contamination.

7.6 Housekeeping

  1. Work areas, passageways, and stairs shall be kept clear of obstructions and debris to prevent slips, trips, and falls. Accumulations of combustible scrap and debris shall be removed at regular intervals. Per WAC 226-155-035(1), all places of employment shall be kept clean and orderly.

7.7 Waste Disposal

  1. Receptacles for waste shall be provided and maintained in a sanitary condition. Hazardous waste shall be disposed of in accordance with environmental regulations. Per 29 CFR 1926.51(e)(1), all garbage and other waste shall be disposed of at reasonable intervals.

7.8 Illumination

  1. Adequate illumination shall be provided in all work areas, particularly in confined spaces and during night operations, to ensure safe working conditions. Per WAC 296-155-035(2), adequate lighting shall be provided in all work areas.

Training Requirements

All employees shall receive training on general safety and health provisions applicable to their work, including first aid procedures and sanitation requirements. Training shall be conducted upon initial assignment and periodically thereafter. Documentation of training, including dates, topics, and attendees, shall be maintained by the Safety Officer. Per 29 CFR 1926 Subpart C, employers are responsible for initiating and maintaining accident prevention programs.

Inspection and Enforcement

Regular inspections of job sites shall be conducted by supervisors and the Safety Officer to ensure compliance with this policy and all regulatory standards. Any identified deficiencies shall be documented, and corrective actions implemented promptly. Non-compliance will result in enforcement actions as outlined in the Violation Consequences section.

Recordkeeping

Records of safety inspections, incident reports, first aid logs, and training documentation shall be maintained for a minimum of five years. The Safety Officer is responsible for ensuring all records are accurately kept and readily accessible for review by regulatory agencies. Per WAC 296-155-035(3), records of inspections shall be maintained.

Violation Consequences

Violations of this safety policy will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and frequency of the violation, as well as its potential to cause harm. All disciplinary actions will be documented.

References

OSHA: 29 CFR 1926 Subpart C; 29 CFR 1926.50 Medical Services and First Aid; 29 CFR 1926.51 Sanitation

WISHA: WAC 296-155-035 General Requirements; WAC 296-155-040 Safe Place Standards; WAC 296-155-120 through 296-155-140

sections.title: subcontractor management plan
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 46 | Subcontractor Management Plan Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey REGULATORY AUTHORITY Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.
Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)
MISH 46 | Subcontractor Management Plan
Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

REGULATORY AUTHORITY

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29

CFR 1926.16 Rules of Construction Multi-Employer Worksites

OSHA

OSHA

Multi-Employer Citation Policy CPL 02-00-124

WISHA

WAC

296-155-110(6) Accident Prevention Program Multi-Employer Responsibilities

AGC

Multi-Employer

Worksite Safety Guidelines

AGC

AGC

SHIP Best Practices Handbook

PURPOSE

This policy establishes guidelines for managing subcontractors to ensure a safe working environment on all MH Construction job sites. It aims to prevent accidents, injuries, and occupational illnesses by clearly defining safety expectations and responsibilities for all parties involved.

SCOPE

This plan applies to all MH Construction employees, all subcontractors, and any visitors present on MH Construction job sites. Adherence to these guidelines is mandatory to maintain a consistent safety standard across all projects.

DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Subcontractor

An individual or entity contracted by MH Construction to perform specific work on a project.

Multi-Employer Worksite

A worksite where employees of two or more employers are exposed to hazards.

Controlling Employer

The employer who has general supervisory authority over the worksite, including the power to correct safety and health hazards.

Exposing Employer

An employer whose own employees are exposed to a hazard.

Correcting Employer

An employer who is responsible for correcting a hazard.

RESPONSIBILITIES

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Establish and maintain a comprehensive safety program.
  • Ensure all subcontractors are pre-qualified based on safety performance.
  • Provide necessary resources for safety compliance.
  • Conduct regular site safety audits.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Enforce safety rules and procedures on site.
  • Conduct daily safety briefings and toolbox talks.
  • Monitor subcontractor safety performance.
  • Report and investigate all incidents and near misses.

Employee Responsibilities

  • Comply with all safety rules and regulations.
  • Report unsafe conditions or acts immediately.
  • Use personal protective equipment (PPE) as required.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Adhere to MH Construction's safety program and all applicable OSHA/WISHA regulations.
  • Provide their employees with necessary safety training and PPE.
  • Designate a competent person for safety on site.
  • Participate in site safety meetings and inspections.
  • Report all incidents and near misses to MH Construction management.

PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

7.1 Subcontractor Pre-Qualification

  1. All subcontractors must undergo a safety pre-qualification process prior to commencing work. This includes review of their safety program, EMR rates, OSHA 300 logs, and safety training records. Only subcontractors meeting MH Construction's safety standards will be approved.

7.2 Safety Orientation and Site-Specific Briefings

  1. All subcontractor personnel must attend a site-specific safety orientation before starting work. This orientation will cover site hazards, emergency procedures, and MH Construction's safety rules. Per 29 CFR 1926.21(b)(2) and WAC 296-155-100(4).

7.3 Hazard Communication and Coordination

  1. MH Construction and all subcontractors will coordinate to identify and control hazards on multi-employer worksites. This includes regular communication regarding potential hazards, safety measures, and incident reporting. Per OSHA Multi-Employer Citation Policy CPL 02-00-124.

7.4 Incident Reporting and Investigation

  1. All incidents, including near misses, injuries, and property damage, involving subcontractor personnel must be immediately reported to MH Construction management. A joint investigation will be conducted to determine root causes and implement corrective actions. Per WAC 296-155-110(6).

7.5 Safety Meetings and Inspections

  1. Subcontractors are required to participate in weekly safety meetings and daily toolbox talks conducted by MH Construction. Joint safety inspections will be performed regularly to ensure compliance with safety standards. Per AGC Multi-Employer Worksite Safety Guidelines.

7.6 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)

  1. Subcontractors must ensure their employees are provided with and properly use all required PPE for the tasks they perform. This includes, but is not limited to, hard hats, safety glasses, high-visibility clothing, and appropriate footwear. Per 29 CFR 1926 Subpart E.

7.7 Compliance with Regulatory Standards

  1. All subcontractors must comply with all applicable OSHA (29 CFR 1926) and WISHA (WAC 296-155) regulations. Where WISHA standards are more stringent, the WISHA requirements shall take precedence. Per 29 CFR 1926.16.

7.8 Emergency Procedures

  1. Subcontractors must be familiar with and adhere to MH Construction's emergency response plan, including evacuation procedures, first aid, and fire prevention. Site-specific emergency contacts will be provided during orientation.

TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

All MH Construction employees and subcontractor personnel must receive appropriate safety training relevant to their tasks and potential hazards. Training frequency will be determined by regulatory requirements and risk assessments. Documentation of all training, including dates, topics, and attendees, must be maintained by both MH Construction and subcontractors. Applicable OSHA training mandates include 29 CFR 1926.21 (Safety Training and Education) and WISHA WAC 296-155-100 (Accident Prevention Program).

INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

Compliance with this plan will be monitored through regular site inspections, safety audits, and observation of work practices. MH Construction management and safety officers will conduct these inspections. Any non-compliance or unsafe conditions will result in immediate corrective action, which may include stop-work orders, retraining, or removal from the job site. All corrective actions will be documented.

RECORDKEEPING

The following records must be maintained:

  • Subcontractor pre-qualification documents (MH Construction, project duration)
  • Site-specific safety orientation records (MH Construction, project duration)
  • Safety meeting minutes and toolbox talk rosters (MH Construction, 3 years)
  • Incident investigation reports (MH Construction, 5 years)
  • Safety inspection reports (MH Construction, 3 years)
  • Employee and subcontractor training records (MH Construction/Subcontractor, duration of employment/contract + 3 years)

VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

Non-compliance with this Subcontractor Management Plan or any applicable safety regulation will result in progressive disciplinary action. This may include, but is not limited to: verbal warnings, written warnings, mandatory retraining, temporary suspension from the job site, financial penalties (as per contract), and termination of contract. Severe violations or repeated non-compliance may lead to immediate contract termination and reporting to regulatory bodies. Per 29 CFR 1926.16.

REFERENCES

  • OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.16 Rules of Construction Multi-Employer Worksites
  • OSHA: OSHA Multi-Employer Citation Policy CPL 02-00-124
  • WISHA: WAC 296-155-110(6) Accident Prevention Program Multi-Employer Responsibilities
  • AGC: Multi-Employer Worksite Safety Guidelines
  • AGC: AGC SHIP Best Practices Handbook

sections.title: insurance requirements contractual risk transfer
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 47 | Insurance Requirements Contractual Risk Transfer Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey ———————————————————————————————————————— 1.  REGULATORY AUTHORITY Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 47 | Insurance Requirements Contractual Risk Transfer

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

————————————————————————————————————————

1. REGULATORY AUTHORITY

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.16

Rules of Construction

OSHA

OSH Act Section 4(b)(1)

General Duty Clause

WISHA

WAC 296-155-110

Accident Prevention Program

WISHA

RCW 51

Washington Industrial Insurance Workers Compensation

WISHA

RCW 51.12.070

Contractor Liability

AGC

Contract Documents

Subcontract Provisions

AGC

AGC Risk Management Manual

2. PURPOSE

This policy establishes clear guidelines for insurance requirements and contractual risk transfer within MH Construction, Inc. It aims to protect the company, its employees, and its projects from potential financial liabilities and operational disruptions arising from unforeseen incidents or contractual breaches.

3. SCOPE

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, vendors, and any other parties engaged in activities on MH Construction job sites or under contract with MH Construction, Inc.

4. DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Certificate of Insurance (COI)

A document issued by an insurance company/broker that verifies the existence of an insurance policy and summarizes its key terms and conditions.

Additional Insured

An individual or entity, other than the named insured, who is covered by an insurance policy. Typically required for subcontractors to protect MH Construction.

Hold Harmless Agreement

A contractual agreement where one party agrees not to hold the other party responsible for any loss, damage, or legal liability.

Waiver of Subrogation

An endorsement that prevents an insurer from seeking recovery from a third party who may have been responsible for a loss.

Contractual Risk Transfer

The process of shifting potential financial loss from one party to another through contractual agreements, such as insurance requirements and indemnification clauses.

5. RESPONSIBILITIES

5.1 Company/Management

• Establish and maintain comprehensive insurance policies.

• Ensure all contracts include appropriate insurance and indemnification clauses.

• Review and approve subcontractor insurance documentation.

5.2 Supervisor/Foreman

• Verify that all personnel on site, including subcontractors, adhere to safety protocols.

• Report any incidents or potential risks immediately.

5.3 Employee

• Adhere to all safety policies and procedures.

• Report any unsafe conditions or incidents to their supervisor.

5.4 Subcontractor

• Maintain required insurance coverage and provide valid Certificates of Insurance.

• Comply with all contractual insurance and indemnification requirements.

• Adhere to all MH Construction safety policies and regulatory requirements.

6. PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

6.1 General Insurance Requirements

All subcontractors and vendors working for MH Construction, Inc. must maintain adequate insurance coverage as specified in their contracts. This typically includes Commercial General Liability, Workers' Compensation, and Automobile Liability insurance. Per RCW 51, Washington Industrial Insurance Workers Compensation, all employers must provide industrial insurance for their employees.

6.2 Certificate of Insurance (COI) Submission

Prior to commencing any work, subcontractors must provide MH Construction, Inc. with a current Certificate of Insurance (COI) from their insurance provider. The COI must name MH Construction, Inc. as an Additional Insured on their Commercial General Liability policy. This aligns with the principles outlined in AGC Contract Documents Subcontract Provisions.

6.3 Minimum Coverage Limits

Minimum insurance coverage limits will be specified in each contract, but generally include: Commercial General Liability ($1,000,000 per occurrence / $2,000,000 aggregate), Automobile Liability ($1,000,000 combined single limit), and Workers' Compensation (statutory limits). These limits are established to ensure adequate protection against potential claims, consistent with OSH Act Section 4(b)(1) general duty clause.

6.4 Contractual Indemnification and Hold Harmless

All contracts with subcontractors will include indemnification and hold harmless clauses, requiring the subcontractor to defend, indemnify, and hold harmless MH Construction, Inc. from any claims, damages, losses, and expenses arising out of their work. This is a critical component of contractual risk transfer, as detailed in the AGC Risk Management Manual.

6.5 Waiver of Subrogation

Subcontractors shall provide a Waiver of Subrogation in favor of MH Construction, Inc. on their Workers' Compensation and Commercial General Liability policies. This prevents their insurers from seeking recovery from MH Construction, Inc. in the event of a loss, further solidifying risk transfer.

6.6 Compliance Monitoring and Audits

MH Construction, Inc. reserves the right to monitor subcontractor compliance with insurance requirements and conduct periodic audits of insurance documentation. Failure to maintain required coverage or provide updated COIs may result in suspension of work or contract termination. This proactive approach supports the Accident Prevention Program outlined in WAC 296-155-110.

6.7 Reporting Incidents and Claims

Any incident that may give rise to an insurance claim must be reported immediately to MH Construction, Inc. management. Prompt reporting is crucial for effective claims management and adherence to insurance policy terms. Per 29 CFR 1926.16, all contractors are responsible for initiating and maintaining accident prevention programs.

6.8 Contractor Liability

Per RCW 51.12.070, contractors are liable for ensuring that all subcontractors comply with industrial insurance requirements. MH Construction, Inc. will take all necessary steps to ensure compliance and mitigate associated risks.

7. TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

All project managers, supervisors, and personnel responsible for contract administration and subcontractor oversight will receive training on this policy. Training will cover the importance of insurance requirements, how to review COIs, and the implications of contractual risk transfer. Training will be conducted annually, and attendance will be documented. Applicable OSHA/WISHA training mandates related to general safety and hazard communication are also reinforced.

8. INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

Compliance with insurance requirements will be monitored through regular review of subcontractor contracts and COIs. Project managers are responsible for verifying current and adequate coverage before work commences and throughout the project duration. Any non-compliance will result in immediate corrective action, which may include work stoppage until compliance is achieved.

9. RECORDKEEPING

Copies of all subcontractor contracts, Certificates of Insurance, and any related insurance documentation will be maintained by MH Construction, Inc. for a minimum of seven years. The Director of Digital Operations and Infrastructure is responsible for ensuring these records are securely stored and readily accessible for audit purposes.

10. VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

Failure to comply with the insurance requirements and contractual risk transfer policies outlined in this document may result in progressive disciplinary actions. For subcontractors, this may include suspension of work, financial penalties, or termination of contract. For employees, non-compliance may lead to disciplinary action up to and including termination of employment, in accordance with MH Construction's HR policies.

11. REFERENCES

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.16 - Rules of Construction

• OSHA: OSH Act Section 4(b)(1) - General Duty Clause

• WISHA: WAC 296-155-110 - Accident Prevention Program

• WISHA: RCW 51 - Washington Industrial Insurance Workers Compensation

• WISHA: RCW 51.12.070 - Contractor Liability

• AGC: Contract Documents - Subcontract Provisions

• AGC: AGC Risk Management Manual -

sections.title: emergency response plan
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 48 | Emergency Response Plan Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey —————————————————————————————————————————————————— 1.  Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1926. 35 Employee Emergency Action Plans OSHA 29 CFR 1910.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 48 | Emergency Response Plan

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

——————————————————————————————————————————————————

1. Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR 1926.35

Employee Emergency Action Plans

OSHA

29 CFR 1910.38

Emergency Action Plans

OSHA

29 CFR 1910.120

HAZWOPER if applicable

WISHA

WAC 296-155-140

Sanitation Emergency

WISHA

WAC 296-800-310

Emergency Procedures

WISHA

WAC 296-824

Emergency Response

AGC

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 4

Emergency Response

2. Purpose

This policy establishes clear guidelines for emergency response to protect MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors. It aims to minimize harm, ensure rapid and effective action during emergencies, and comply with all applicable federal and state regulations.

3. Scope

This Emergency Response Plan applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors on MH Construction job sites. Adherence to this plan is mandatory to ensure the safety and well-being of all personnel and to maintain regulatory compliance.

4. Definitions

Term

Definition

Emergency Action Plan (EAP)

A written plan required by OSHA outlining employer and employee actions during workplace emergencies.

HAZWOPER

Hazardous Waste Operations and Emergency Response. OSHA standard 29 CFR 1910.120 covering hazardous substance clean-up operations and emergency response.

First Responder

Individuals trained and designated to provide initial assistance at an emergency scene, such as fire, medical, or hazardous material incidents.

Assembly Point

A pre-determined safe location where all personnel are to gather and be accounted for during an evacuation.

Incident Commander

The individual responsible for overall management of an emergency incident, including strategy, tactics, and resource allocation.

5. Responsibilities

5.1 Company/Management Responsibilities

  • • Develop, implement, and maintain a comprehensive Emergency Response Plan.
  • • Ensure adequate resources are available for emergency response and training.
  • • Conduct periodic reviews and updates of the Emergency Response Plan.
  • • Establish communication protocols with external emergency services.

5.2 Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • • Ensure all employees under their supervision are trained on the Emergency Response Plan.
  • • Designate and communicate assembly points for their work areas.
  • • Account for all personnel during an evacuation or emergency.
  • • Initiate emergency procedures and contact emergency services as required.

5.3 Employee Responsibilities

  • • Understand and follow the Emergency Response Plan.
  • • Know evacuation routes, assembly points, and emergency contact numbers.
  • • Report all emergencies promptly to their supervisor or management.
  • • Participate in emergency drills and training sessions.

5.4 Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • • Adhere to MH Construction, Inc.'s Emergency Response Plan while on site.
  • • Ensure their employees are aware of and comply with emergency procedures.
  • • Coordinate emergency response efforts with MH Construction, Inc. supervisors.

6. Procedures / Requirements

6.1 Emergency Evacuation Procedures

  1. 1. Upon alarm or notification, immediately cease work and proceed to the nearest safe exit.
  2. 2. Follow designated evacuation routes to the pre-determined assembly point. Per 29 CFR 1910.38(c)(3), floor plans or workplace maps should be posted.
  3. 3. Do not use elevators during fire emergencies. Use stairs only.
  4. 4. At the assembly point, report to your supervisor for accountability. Per 29 CFR 1910.38(c)(4), procedures for accounting for all employees after evacuation must be established.
  5. 5. Do not re-enter the building or area until an "all-clear" is given by emergency personnel.

6.2 Fire Prevention and Response

  1. 1. All employees must be aware of fire extinguisher locations and proper usage. Per WAC 296-800-300, employers must ensure fire extinguishers are readily accessible.
  2. 2. Report any fire hazards, such as faulty wiring or improper storage of flammable materials, immediately to a supervisor.
  3. 3. In case of a small, controllable fire, use the nearest appropriate fire extinguisher. Ensure your escape path is clear. Per 29 CFR 1910.157, employees must be trained on the use of fire extinguishers.
  4. 4. For large or uncontrollable fires, activate the fire alarm, evacuate the area, and call 911.

6.3 Medical Emergencies

  1. 1. For serious injuries or illnesses, immediately call 911. Provide clear and concise information about the incident and location.
  2. 2. Administer first aid only if trained and qualified to do so. Per 29 CFR 1926.50, adequate first aid supplies and trained personnel must be available.
  3. 3. Do not move an injured person unless they are in immediate danger.
  4. 4. Notify a supervisor immediately after ensuring the injured person is receiving appropriate care.

6.4 Hazardous Material Spills (HAZWOPER)

  1. 1. Identify the spilled material if safe to do so. Refer to Safety Data Sheets (SDS) for information on hazards and proper response. Per 29 CFR 1910.1200, SDS must be readily accessible.
  2. 2. For minor spills that can be safely contained by trained personnel, follow established clean-up procedures using appropriate PPE.
  3. 3. For large or unknown spills, or spills of highly hazardous materials, immediately evacuate the area, secure it, and call 911 and the designated HAZWOPER team. Per 29 CFR 1910.120, employees involved in hazardous waste operations must be trained.
  4. 4. Prevent the spill from entering drains or waterways.

6.5 Severe Weather and Natural Disasters

  1. 1. Monitor local weather alerts and warnings. Supervisors will disseminate critical information to all personnel.
  2. 2. In case of severe weather (e.g., thunderstorms, high winds), seek shelter in designated safe areas or sturdy structures.
  3. 3. For earthquakes, "Drop, Cover, and Hold On." After the shaking stops, evacuate to an open area away from buildings and power lines.
  4. 4. Follow instructions from emergency services and company management during and after natural disasters.

6.6 Communication Procedures

  1. 1. All emergency communications will be initiated by the Incident Commander or designated supervisor.
  2. 2. Use two-way radios, cell phones, or other designated communication devices to relay information during an emergency.
  3. 3. Emergency contact numbers for fire, police, ambulance, and HAZMAT teams will be prominently displayed at all job sites.
  4. 4. Establish a clear chain of command for reporting and decision-making during emergencies.

7. Training Requirements

All MH Construction, Inc. employees, including new hires, must receive initial and annual refresher training on the Emergency Response Plan. This training will cover evacuation procedures, fire safety, medical emergency protocols, and HAZWOPER awareness if applicable. Per 29 CFR 1910.38(e) and WAC 296-800-310(4), training must be provided to all employees. Training documentation, including dates, topics covered, and attendees, will be maintained by the Safety Officer for a minimum of three years.

8. Inspection and Enforcement

The Safety Officer will conduct regular inspections (at least quarterly) of all job sites to ensure compliance with the Emergency Response Plan, including checking emergency exits, fire extinguishers, and first aid supplies. Any deficiencies will be documented, and corrective actions will be implemented immediately. Non-compliance with this plan will result in disciplinary action as outlined in Section 10.

9. Recordkeeping

The following records related to emergency response will be maintained by the Safety Officer:
• Emergency Response Plan (current and previous revisions)
• Emergency contact lists
• Training records (dates, attendees, topics)
• Inspection reports and corrective actions
• Incident reports for all emergencies
All records will be retained for a minimum of five years. Per WAC 296-800-140, employers must maintain records of safety and health training.

10. Violation Consequences

Failure to comply with the MH Construction, Inc. Emergency Response Plan will result in progressive disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and gravity of the violation, as well as any previous infractions. All disciplinary actions will be documented.

11. References

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.35 Employee Emergency Action Plans

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1910.38 Emergency Action Plans

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1910.120 HAZWOPER if applicable

• WISHA: WAC 296-155-140 Sanitation Emergency

• WISHA: WAC 296-800-310 Emergency Procedures

• WISHA: WAC 296-824 Emergency Response

• AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 4 Emergency Response

sections.title: incident investigation root cause analysis
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 49 | Incident Investigation Root Cause Analysis Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey Regulatory Authority Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1904 Recording and Reporting OSHA 29 CFR 1904. 39 Reporting Fatalities OSHA 29 CFR 1926.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 49 | Incident Investigation Root Cause Analysis

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

Regulatory Authority

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29 CFR

1904 Recording and Reporting

OSHA

29 CFR

1904.39 Reporting Fatalities

OSHA

29 CFR

1926.20(b)(3) Frequent inspections

WISHA

WAC 296-27

Recording and Reporting Occupational Injuries and Illnesses

WISHA

WAC 296-155-110(5)

Accident Prevention Program Investigation

AGC

Chapter 5

Safety and Health Manual Ch. 5 Incident Investigation

Purpose

This policy establishes a systematic approach for investigating incidents to identify root causes, prevent recurrence, and ensure a safe working environment. It protects employees, subcontractors, and the company from hazards and operational disruptions.

Scope

This policy applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors on MH Construction job sites. All personnel are required to adhere to the procedures outlined herein.

Definitions

Term

Definition

Incident

An unplanned, undesired event that adversely affects completion of a task or results in injury, illness, property damage, or other loss.

Near Miss

An unplanned event that did not result in injury, illness, or damage but had the potential to do so.

Root Cause

The fundamental reason for an incident, which if eliminated, would prevent recurrence.

Corrective Action

An action taken to eliminate the cause of a detected nonconformity or other undesirable situation.

Preventative Action

An action taken to eliminate the cause of a potential nonconformity or other undesirable potential situation.

Responsibilities

Company/Management Responsibilities

  • Provide necessary resources for incident investigations, including training and equipment.
  • Ensure timely review of incident reports and implementation of recommended corrective actions.
  • Maintain a culture that encourages open reporting of incidents and near misses without fear of reprisal.

Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

  • Initiate immediate response to incidents, ensuring scene safety and first aid.
  • Ensure all incidents and near misses are reported promptly to the Safety Officer.
  • Participate actively in incident investigations and root cause analysis.
  • Implement and monitor corrective actions within their area of control.

Employee Responsibilities

  • Report all incidents, near misses, and unsafe conditions immediately to their supervisor or foreman.
  • Cooperate fully with all incident investigations.
  • Adhere to all safety procedures and policies to prevent incidents.

Subcontractor Responsibilities

  • Adhere to MH Construction's incident reporting procedures for their personnel and operations on MHC job sites.
  • Participate in joint incident investigations as required by MH Construction.
  • Implement corrective actions for incidents involving their employees or equipment.

Procedures / Requirements

7.1 Incident Reporting

All incidents, including injuries, illnesses, property damage, and near misses, must be reported immediately to the employee's supervisor or foreman. For serious incidents, such as fatalities or hospitalizations, the Safety Officer must be notified immediately. Per OSHA 29 CFR 1904.39, all fatalities must be reported within 8 hours, and all in-patient hospitalizations, amputations, or losses of an eye must be reported within 24 hours.

7.2 Initial Response and Scene Control

Upon notification of an incident, the supervisor/foreman must ensure the scene is secured to prevent further injury or damage. This includes providing first aid, calling emergency services if necessary, and preserving evidence. Only authorized personnel should enter the incident scene.

7.3 Investigation Team Assembly

For minor incidents, the supervisor/foreman may conduct the investigation. For serious incidents, a team led by the Safety Officer, including relevant supervisors, employees, and potentially external experts, will be assembled. This aligns with the spirit of WISHA WAC 296-155-110(5) regarding accident prevention program investigations.

7.4 Data Collection

The investigation team will collect all relevant data, including witness statements, photographs, videos, equipment logs, maintenance records, and environmental conditions. This comprehensive data collection is crucial for accurate root cause analysis.

7.5 Root Cause Analysis

The investigation team will utilize systematic methods (e.g., '5 Whys', Fishbone Diagram) to identify the underlying root causes of the incident, rather than just superficial symptoms. This process aims to uncover systemic failures or deficiencies. Per OSHA 29 CFR 1926.20(b)(3), frequent and regular inspections of job sites, materials, and equipment must be conducted by competent persons to prevent incidents.

7.6 Corrective and Preventative Actions

Based on the root cause analysis, specific corrective actions will be developed to eliminate the identified causes and prevent recurrence. Preventative actions will also be considered to address similar potential hazards across the organization. Actions will be assigned to responsible parties with clear deadlines.

7.7 Implementation and Follow-up

Assigned corrective and preventative actions must be implemented within the specified timeframe. The Safety Officer will follow up to ensure the effectiveness of these actions and make adjustments as necessary. This continuous improvement loop is vital for maintaining a robust safety program.

7.8 Communication of Findings

Key findings from incident investigations, including root causes and corrective actions, will be communicated to relevant personnel, including affected employees, supervisors, and management. Lessons learned will be shared to prevent similar incidents across all MH Construction operations.

Training Requirements

All employees and supervisors must receive training on incident reporting procedures, initial response protocols, and their roles in incident investigations. Supervisors and Safety Officers will receive additional training in root cause analysis techniques. Training frequency will be determined by the Safety Officer, with refresher training conducted annually or as needed. Documentation of all training will be maintained by HR and the Safety Officer. This aligns with general training requirements under OSHA and WISHA WAC 296-27 for recording and reporting occupational injuries and illnesses.

Inspection and Enforcement

Compliance with this incident investigation policy will be monitored through regular site inspections, safety audits, and review of incident reports. Any deviations or failures to follow procedures will result in corrective action, which may include retraining or disciplinary measures. The Safety Officer is responsible for overseeing the enforcement of this policy.

Recordkeeping

All incident reports, investigation findings, root cause analyses, and corrective/preventative actions will be documented and retained for a minimum of five (5) years. Records will be maintained by the Safety Officer and HR. This includes OSHA 300 logs, 301 incident reports, and 300A summaries as required by 29 CFR 1904.

Violation Consequences

Failure to comply with the Incident Investigation Root Cause Analysis policy may result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. The severity of the disciplinary action will depend on the nature and severity of the violation, as well as any previous infractions. MH Construction is committed to maintaining a safe workplace, and adherence to this policy is mandatory.

References

  • - OSHA: 29 CFR 1904 Recording and Reporting
  • - OSHA: 29 CFR 1904.39 Reporting Fatalities
  • - OSHA: 29 CFR 1926.20(b)(3) Frequent inspections
  • - WISHA: WAC 296-27 Recording and Reporting Occupational Injuries and Illnesses
  • - WISHA: WAC 296-155-110(5) Accident Prevention Program Investigation
  • - AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 5 Incident Investigation

sections.title: return to work program
sections.summary: MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.  Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH) MISH 50 | Return to Work Program Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey 1.  REGULATORY AUTHORITY The following regulatory citations and references apply to this section: Authority Citation Title/Description OSHA 29 CFR 1904 Recordkeeping OSHA OSH Act General Duty Clause WISHA RCW 51.
sections.body:
MH CONSTRUCTION, INC.

Industrial Safety & Health Program (MISH)

MISH 50 | Return to Work Program

Revision 3 | Effective: 04/07/2026 | Safety Officer: Matt Ramsey

1. REGULATORY AUTHORITY

The following regulatory citations and references apply to this section:

Authority

Citation

Title/Description

OSHA

29

CFR 1904 Recordkeeping

OSHA

OSH

Act General Duty Clause

WISHA

RCW

51.32 Washington Workers Compensation Benefits

WISHA

RCW

51.36 Medical Aid

WISHA

WAC

296-155-110 Accident Prevention Program

AGC

Safety

and Health Manual Ch. 5

AGC

AGC

Return-to-Work Best Practices

2. PURPOSE

This policy establishes a comprehensive Return to Work Program designed to facilitate the timely and safe return of injured or ill employees to productive employment. It aims to minimize the impact of workplace injuries and illnesses on both the employee and MH Construction, Inc., by providing appropriate accommodations and support while ensuring compliance with all applicable regulations.

3. SCOPE

This program applies to all MH Construction, Inc. employees, subcontractors, and visitors on MH Construction job sites. It covers all work-related injuries and illnesses, regardless of severity, and outlines the procedures for managing an employee's return to work after an absence due to such incidents.

4. DEFINITIONS

Term

Definition

Return to Work (RTW) Program

A structured process designed to assist employees who have been absent from work due to a work-related injury or illness in returning to suitable employment.

Light Duty/Modified Work

Temporary job assignments that accommodate an employee's medical restrictions while they recover from an injury or illness.

Medical Provider

A licensed healthcare professional responsible for diagnosing, treating, and providing work restrictions for injured or ill employees.

Job Hazard Analysis (JHA)

A process to identify potential hazards of tasks and jobs, and to prescribe controls to eliminate or reduce those hazards.

OSHA Recordable Injury/Illness

An injury or illness that meets specific criteria outlined by OSHA for recordkeeping purposes, typically requiring medical treatment beyond first aid, days away from work, restricted work, or transfer to another job.

5. RESPONSIBILITIES

5.1 Company/Management Responsibilities

• Establish and maintain a comprehensive Return to Work Program. • Ensure adequate resources are available for program implementation. • Provide training and support to supervisors and employees regarding the RTW process. • Review and update the program periodically to ensure effectiveness and compliance.

5.2 Supervisor/Foreman Responsibilities

• Report all injuries and illnesses promptly. • Cooperate with medical providers and HR/Safety to identify suitable modified work. • Communicate with injured employees regularly. • Ensure modified work assignments adhere to medical restrictions.

5.3 Employee Responsibilities

• Report all work-related injuries/illnesses immediately to their supervisor. • Seek prompt medical attention as needed. • Cooperate with medical providers and the company regarding treatment and return to work plans. • Adhere to all medical restrictions and modified work assignments.

5.4 Subcontractor Responsibilities

• Ensure their employees are aware of and follow their company's return to work policies. • Notify MH Construction of any serious injuries or illnesses involving their employees on MHC job sites. • Cooperate with MH Construction in coordinating any necessary accommodations for their employees working on MHC sites.

6. PROCEDURES / REQUIREMENTS

6.1 Injury/Illness Reporting

1. All work-related injuries or illnesses, regardless of severity, must be reported to the immediate supervisor within 24 hours of occurrence or discovery. 2. An incident report form must be completed for all reported incidents. 3. Per OSHA 29 CFR 1904, certain injuries and illnesses must be reported to OSHA within specific timeframes (e.g., fatalities within 8 hours, inpatient hospitalization, amputation, or loss of an eye within 24 hours).

6.2 Medical Treatment and Assessment

1. Employees must seek appropriate medical attention for work-related injuries or illnesses. 2. MH Construction will provide a list of approved medical providers. 3. A medical release form, outlining work restrictions and capabilities, must be provided to MH Construction by the treating physician. 4. Per RCW 51.36, injured workers are entitled to medical aid for their accepted claim.

6.3 Modified Duty Assignment

1. Upon receipt of medical restrictions, MH Construction will assess available modified duty positions that align with the employee's capabilities. 2. Modified duty assignments will be temporary and designed to facilitate a progressive return to full duty. 3. The employee's supervisor will review the modified duty assignment with the employee and ensure all restrictions are understood and followed. 4. Per OSH Act General Duty Clause, employers must provide a workplace free from recognized hazards, which extends to ensuring modified duty is safe and appropriate.

6.4 Communication and Coordination

1. Regular communication will be maintained between the injured employee, supervisor, HR, and the Safety Officer throughout the recovery and return to work process. 2. Case conferences may be held with the medical provider, employee, and company representatives to discuss treatment plans and return to work options. 3. Per WAC 296-155-110, employers must have an Accident Prevention Program that includes provisions for communication regarding injuries and return to work.

6.5 Return to Full Duty

1. A full medical release from the treating physician is required before an employee can return to full duty. 2. The Safety Officer or HR will review the full release and authorize the return to full duty. 3. Any concerns or ongoing restrictions will be addressed before the employee resumes their regular duties.

6.6 Accommodation for Disabilities

1. MH Construction will engage in an interactive process with employees with disabilities to determine reasonable accommodations, in accordance with the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) and Washington State law. 2. This includes exploring modifications to job duties, work environment, or equipment to enable the employee to perform essential job functions.

6.7 Washington Workers Compensation Benefits

1. Employees injured on the job are eligible for benefits under Washington State Workers' Compensation laws. 2. Per RCW 51.32, these benefits may include medical treatment, wage replacement, and vocational rehabilitation services. 3. MH Construction will assist employees in navigating the workers' compensation claim process.

7. TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

• All supervisors and foremen will receive training on the Return to Work Program, including their responsibilities in managing injured employees and facilitating modified duty assignments. • Employees will be informed of the RTW program during new hire orientation and through periodic safety meetings. • Training documentation will be maintained by the HR department for a minimum of three years.

8. INSPECTION AND ENFORCEMENT

• Compliance with the Return to Work Program will be monitored by the Safety Officer and HR department. • Regular reviews of modified duty assignments will be conducted to ensure adherence to medical restrictions and program guidelines. • Any non-compliance will be addressed through corrective actions, which may include retraining or disciplinary measures.

9. RECORDKEEPING

• All injury/illness reports, medical records, work restriction forms, and modified duty agreements will be maintained by the HR department. • Records will be kept confidential and in accordance with HIPAA and other privacy regulations. • Per OSHA 29 CFR 1904, records of work-related injuries and illnesses must be maintained for five years following the end of the calendar year to which they relate. • Per RCW 51.32, records related to workers' compensation claims must be maintained as per L&I requirements.

10. VIOLATION CONSEQUENCES

Violations of this Return to Work Program, including failure to report injuries, non-adherence to medical restrictions, or refusal of suitable modified duty, may result in progressive disciplinary action up to and including termination of employment. Subcontractors found in violation may face penalties as outlined in their contractual agreements with MH Construction.

11. REFERENCES

The following regulatory and company documents were referenced in the creation of this program:

• OSHA: 29 CFR 1904 Recordkeeping

• OSHA: OSH Act General Duty Clause

• WISHA: RCW 51.32 Washington Workers Compensation Benefits

• WISHA: RCW 51.36 Medical Aid

• WISHA: WAC 296-155-110 Accident Prevention Program

• AGC: Safety and Health Manual Ch. 5

• AGC: AGC Return-to-Work Best Practices
